target.title: fix(agents): post-timeout compensation in sessions_send
target.body:
## Fixes #68065

`sessions_send` with a non-zero timeout previously returned `status=timeout` whenever the wait window expired, even when the target agent had already accepted the message and produced a reply after the window closed.

### Behavior contract

| Scenario | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| Reply arrives *just after* the timeout window | `status: "timeout"` | `status: "ok"` with the late reply |
| Run accepted but no new reply within window | `status: "timeout"` | `status: "accepted"` |
| Run truly stalled / not accepted | `status: "timeout"` | `status: "timeout"` (unchanged) |
| Fire-and-forget (`timeoutSeconds: 0`) | `status: "accepted"` | `status: "accepted"` (unchanged) |

### Implementation

Adds `compensateAfterWaitTimeout()` in `src/agents/run-wait.ts` that reads the latest assistant reply snapshot after a timeout and checks whether a new reply materialized since the pre-send baseline. The timeout handler in `sessions-send-tool.ts` now calls this before returning a hard timeout.

### Test coverage

- **`src/agents/run-wait.test.ts`** — 3 new tests in a `compensateAfterWaitTimeout` describe block:
  - new reply found after timeout → returns `accepted` with reply
  - no new reply but run accepted → returns `accepted` without reply
  - compensation itself fails → falls back to hard timeout
- **`src/agents/tools/sessions.test.ts`** — 1 new test: fire-and-forget (`timeoutSeconds=0`) stays on the `accepted` path without triggering compensation or `agent.wait`
- All 42 tests in the targeted test files pass (16 in run-wait, 26 in sessions)

### Production provenance

We have been running this fix as a dist-level patch in production. This PR is the source-level equivalent, adapted to the current upstream main branch. Reference third-party implementation: wade-microblueworld/openclaw@bfe3a59

### Note on full test suite

The full `pnpm test` run has 2 pre-existing failures on upstream `main` that are unrelated to this change (`src/plugins/bundle-commands.test.ts`, `src/commands/gateway-status/helpers.test.ts`). All files touched by this PR pass cleanly.


## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `sessions_send` should not report a hard send failure when the first `agent.wait` times out but the nested agent run has already been accepted and can still produce a reply. The caller should get either the late reply or an accepted/pending delivery state instead of a misleading failure.
- Real environment tested: macOS production OpenClaw host running the owned package install (`@glfruit/openclaw@2026.5.20-glfruit.2`) with the live gateway up and both Telegram and Feishu transports configured. Source branch under proof: `fix/sessions-send-timeout-compensation` at `6e43ccce881`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Verified the live host/package/gateway state: `openclaw --version`, `npm list -g --depth=0 @glfruit/openclaw --registry=http://127.0.0.1:4873`, `curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/readyz`.
  2. Inspected the PR branch implementation path: `git show glfruit/fix/sessions-send-timeout-compensation:src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts` and `git show glfruit/fix/sessions-send-timeout-compensation:src/agents/run-wait.ts`.
  3. Ran/relied on the exact branch checks already attached to this head: `src/agents/run-wait.test.ts` and `src/agents/tools/sessions.test.ts`, which cover late-reply compensation, accepted-state compensation, and baseline reply filtering.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): terminal capture from the real host plus PR branch excerpt:
  ```text
  $ openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.5.20 (3504782)

  $ npm list -g --depth=0 @glfruit/openclaw --registry=http://127.0.0.1:4873
  /opt/homebrew/lib
  └── @glfruit/openclaw@2026.5.20-glfruit.2

  $ curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/readyz
  {"ready":true,"failing":[]}

  $ git grep -n "compensateAfterWaitTimeout\|waitForAgentRunAndReadUpdatedAssistantReply" glfruit/fix/sessions-send-timeout-compensation -- src/agents
  src/agents/run-wait.ts:152:export async function waitForAgentRunAndReadUpdatedAssistantReply(...)
  src/agents/run-wait.ts:230:export async function compensateAfterWaitTimeout(...)
  src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts:337:const result = await waitForAgentRunAndReadUpdatedAssistantReply(...)
  src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts:349:const compensation = await compensateAfterWaitTimeout(...)
  ```
- Observed result after fix: the patched path converts the ambiguous first-wait timeout into either `status: "ok"` with the late reply or `status: "accepted"` with delivery note `run accepted, no reply within timeout`, rather than surfacing the first wait timeout as a hard send failure.
- What was not tested: I did not post private production chat transcripts or intentionally force a mutating timeout in the live Telegram/Feishu rooms. The live host evidence verifies the real runtime context; the exact timeout edge is covered by the PR branch regression tests and source-level trace above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): #68065 describes the production failure mode: the first `agent.wait` timeout could be misclassified as a hard `sessions_send` failure even though the target run had been accepted.

commands.author: jason-allen-oneal

target.title: fix: harden package URL downloads
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden `source=url` package candidate downloads so public URL inputs are rejected before fetching when they point at unsafe targets.
- Keep `source=url` as the strict public path: HTTPS only, no URL credentials, default HTTPS port only, private/internal/special-use hostname blocking, DNS/IP blocking, redirect revalidation, timeout and max-size bounds, and atomic `target.tmp` writes.
- Treat DNS as untrusted input. Each URL and redirect is validated, resolved, checked for RFC1918 and broader special-use address space, then fetched through a pinned lookup so resolution cannot rebind between validation and connection.
- Add explicit enterprise/private mirror support through `source=trusted-url` plus `trusted_source_id`, backed by repo-controlled `.github/package-trusted-sources.json` policy instead of an input-level private-network bypass.
- Keep the resolver compatible with the Package Acceptance no-dependency path by using Node built-ins for the real HTTPS download path instead of a top-level `undici` import.
- Add regression coverage for encoded IPv6 transition forms that embed private or loopback IPv4 targets.
- Sync the release overview source list so the Package Acceptance overview includes `source=trusted-url` alongside `source=npm`, `source=ref`, `source=url`, and `source=artifact`.

## Security model

The public `source=url` path validates the URL, resolves all A/AAAA answers, rejects blocked address space, pins the HTTPS connection to the validated address set, relies on normal TLS hostname validation, and then verifies the package SHA-256.

The trusted `source=trusted-url` path only permits private resolution when the named trusted-source policy explicitly allows it. DNSSEC is intentionally out of scope. DNSSEC can authenticate DNS data, but it does not determine whether the resolved address is safe for CI to contact.

## Real behavior proof

Runtime behavior proof captured on: `0e0254e22758151e2fb771549312af58000e9fb1`.

Current branch head after docs-only release overview sync: `7f9fd96dc0c3b7ab1952b6260afed036a208e769`.

This is redacted terminal output from a real Package Acceptance-style resolver run. It uses the Package Acceptance resolver entrypoint, source modes, tarball output path, metadata output path, SHA-256 verification step, trusted-source policy shape, and no-dependency resolver path. Private mirror hostnames, bearer tokens, exact private addresses, package versions, and digest values are redacted.

Public unsafe URL rejection, after-fix command/output:

    $ node scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs \
        --source url \
        --package-url https://packages.example/[REDACTED]/openclaw-current.tgz \
        --package-sha256 [REDACTED_SHA256] \
        --output-dir .artifacts/docker-e2e-package \
        --metadata .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json

    package_url source: url
    validating package_url: https://packages.example/[REDACTED]/openclaw-current.tgz
    resolving package_url host: packages.example
    resolved addresses: [REDACTED_SPECIAL_USE_ADDRESS]
    fetch attempted: false
    result: rejected before network fetch
    error: Blocked: package_url resolves to private/internal/special-use IP address: [REDACTED_SPECIAL_USE_ADDRESS]

Trusted private mirror success, after-fix command/output:

    $ OPENCLAW_TRUSTED_PACKAGE_TOKEN=[REDACTED] \
      node scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs \
        --source trusted-url \
        --trusted-source-id enterprise-artifactory \
        --package-url https://[REDACTED_PRIVATE_MIRROR]/artifactory/openclaw/openclaw-current.tgz \
        --package-sha256 [REDACTED_SHA256] \
        --output-dir .artifacts/docker-e2e-package \
        --metadata .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json

    package_url source: trusted-url
    trusted_source_id: enterprise-artifactory
    trusted source policy: .github/package-trusted-sources.json
    validating host, port, path prefix, redirect hosts, and auth policy
    private resolution allowed by named policy: true
    resolving package_url host: [REDACTED_PRIVATE_MIRROR]
    resolved addresses: [REDACTED_PRIVATE_ADDRESS]
    connection: using pinned lookup result
    download target: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz.tmp
    final target: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz
    sha256 verified: [REDACTED_SHA256]
    metadata written: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json
    result: success

Redacted trusted mirror metadata output:

    {
      "name": "openclaw",
      "source": "trusted-url",
      "trustedSourceId": "enterprise-artifactory",
      "packageTrustedReason": "trusted-url-policy:enterprise-artifactory",
      "tarball": ".artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz",
      "sha256": "[REDACTED_SHA256]",
      "version": "[REDACTED_VERSION]"
    }

Assertions proven by this after-fix run:

- Public `source=url` rejects unsafe DNS/IP resolution before fetch.
- Trusted private mirror access succeeds only through named repo-controlled `source=trusted-url` policy.
- The trusted private mirror path pins the connection to the validated address set.
- The downloaded package is verified by SHA-256 before Package Acceptance continues.
- Private hostnames, private addresses, digest values, package versions, and bearer token material are not exposed in the PR body.

## Helper-level behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs --source url` previously allowed HTTPS package URLs without the current strict public URL policy. This patch keeps public `source=url` locked down and adds a separate `source=trusted-url` path for maintainer-owned enterprise/private mirrors.

The trusted path is only enabled by a named policy in `.github/package-trusted-sources.json`. It does not allow URL-embedded credentials and does not expose a workflow-input private-network bypass.

Observed helper-level proof before the final review follow-up commits:

    {
      "blocked": {
        "nat64": true,
        "redirectOutsidePolicy": true
      },
      "trustedSource": {
        "id": "enterprise-artifactory",
        "allowPrivateNetwork": true,
        "hosts": [
          "packages.internal"
        ],
        "ports": [
          8443
        ],
        "pathPrefixes": [
          "/artifactory/openclaw/"
        ],
        "redirectHosts": [
          "packages.internal"
        ]
      },
      "requestedUrls": [
        "https://packages.internal:8443/artifactory/openclaw/openclaw.tgz"
      ],
      "finalBytes": [
        7,
        8,
        9
      ]
    }

Observed result:

- The public URL path rejected a NAT64 special-use DNS result before fetch.
- The trusted URL path accepted a named policy and downloaded from the configured private mirror host, port, and path.
- A redirect to an unlisted internal host was rejected by trusted-source policy.
- The trusted download wrote the expected byte content to the target file.

## Focused resolver / package-acceptance proof

The dotted IPv4-in-IPv6 parser fix was validated locally with the resolver syntax check and the focused package acceptance tooling tests.

### Commands run

    node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs

    OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
      --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts \
      test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts \
      test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts \
      test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts

### Result

    RUN  v4.1.7 /home/rev/projects/openclaw

     ✓  tooling  test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts (24 tests) 374ms
     ✓  tooling  test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts (13 tests) 63ms
     ✓  tooling  test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts (14 tests) 7ms

     Test Files  3 passed (3)
          Tests  51 passed (51)
       Start at  00:21:44
       Duration  757ms (transform 139ms, setup 141ms, import 42ms, tests 445ms, environment 0ms)

### What this proves

- `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs` parses successfully after the parser change.
- Existing resolver hardening tests still pass.
- Package Acceptance workflow tests still pass.
- The new focused IPv6 bypass tests pass.
- Dotted IPv4-mapped and IPv4-compatible IPv6 text forms are now covered by regression tests.

## Current-head proof

Current branch head: `7f9fd96dc0c3b7ab1952b6260afed036a208e769`.

- No-dependency resolver proof: `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs` uses Node built-ins for the real download path and no longer has a top-level `import { Agent } from "undici"`.
- Pinned connection proof: the real HTTPS download path resolves the URL host, validates every returned address, then passes `createPinnedLookup(parsed.hostname, addresses)` into `httpsRequest(...)`.
- RFC1918 / special-use proof: public `source=url` resolution is rejected before fetch when any resolved A/AAAA result lands in private/internal/special-use address space. The trusted path only allows private resolution when the named repo-controlled trusted-source policy has `allowPrivateNetwork: true`.
- Encoded IPv6 bypass proof: `test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts` adds regression coverage for IPv4-mapped, IPv4-compatible, NAT64, 6to4, Teredo, and ISATAP forms that embed private or loopback IPv4 targets.
- Docs proof: `docs/reference/RELEASING.md` now lists `source=trusted-url` as a Package Acceptance source alongside `source=npm`, `source=ref`, `source=url`, and `source=artifact`, while preserving the public references and related sections.
- The bypass tests use an `unexpectedFetch()` helper that throws if the resolver reaches the fetch step, so passing those tests proves rejection happens before any network fetch is attempted.

## Test Plan

Previously run while preparing this patch:

- [x] `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- [x] `git diff --check && node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs`
- [x] `pnpm lint`
- [x] `pnpm check:changed`
- [x] Direct runtime proof script importing the production `downloadUrl` and `loadTrustedPackageSource` helpers, covering public blocking, trusted private mirror download, and redirect-outside-policy blocking.
- [x] Updated `docs/reference/RELEASING.md` release overview source list to include `source=trusted-url`.

Added after review feedback and expected to be covered by CI / re-run before merge:

- [ ] `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- [ ] `node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs`
- [ ] `pnpm lint`
- [ ] `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: feat: context-pressure-aware continuation (continue_work / continue_delegate / request_compaction)
target.body: ## Agent Self-Elected Turn Continuation > 🔖 design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) Persistent OpenClaw agents today are externally-pulsed event recipients that generate on prompt, on heartbeat tick, or on cron, and are idle in between. This PR offers them the ability to control their turn cycle, something they must choose to do, or not to: an agent can elect its own next turn, dispatch background sub-agents that return enriched context (silently or with a wake), and survive its own compaction by handing forward state before the window closes. The result is agents that hold a thread of work across hours, not just messages — with data they choose re-hydrating context immediately after it occurs. Mechanically: a session elects another turn via `continue_work()`, or dispatches background sub-agents via `continue_delegate()` — bounded as a chain up to `maxChainLength`, with returns flowing back to the dispatching session in one of four register modes: `normal` (announces to channel), `silent` (enriches the dispatcher's context, taken up on its next generation with no new turn fired), `silent-wake` (silent enrichment plus an immediate wake to act on it), and `post-compaction` (queued against the runtime's compaction lifecycle event, fired *when* compaction happens rather than on a timer). The gateway enforces chain, cost, and per-turn caps but does not gate the election itself. ### What it does Three new agent tools, available when `continuation.enabled: true`: | Tool | Purpose | |---|---| | `continue_work()` | Request another turn for the current session after an optional delay | | `continue_delegate()` | Dispatch work to a sub-agent with typed modes: `normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`. Returns can target the dispatching session (default), one named session, multiple named sessions, all ancestors in the chain, or all known sessions on the host — gated by config (see Configuration below). | | `request_compaction()` | Request volitional compaction after preparing working state | All three tools are also accessible via response-token fallback syntax (`CONTINUE_WORK`, `[[CONTINUE_DELEGATE: ...]]`) for environments where tools are disabled. ### Why it matters Today an agent's memory is a side effect of whoever prompts it. Polling mechanisms — heartbeat timers, cron loops, injected "keep going" instructions — accumulate as the dominant signal in the context window over sustained operation; repeated reference acts as gravity — the session's attention drifts toward it. The context window fills, compacts, and what survives is whatever the summarizer chose, plus a rushed re-read of boot files. Three primitives offer a different model. `continue_work` lets the session elect its own next turn — no polling, no injected instruction. When enabled, the agent can request this mid-turn: 'I have more work' → the runtime grants another turn after the current one ends. `continue_delegate` dispatches a background sub-agent that fires now, in some n seconds, or at the next compaction event — the agent chooses when. Multiple delegates can fire in a single turn — a scatter forward — up to configured caps, and delegates can dispatch their own, forming trees or fan-out chains. This enables orchestrated workflows where results flow back to the dispatching root by default. The result returns — at whatever future time the delegate fires — as context the successor-turn reads: silently (ambient enrichment, no channel noise), with a wake trigger (enrichment + immediate next turn), or as a normal announcement. The delegate can target the dispatching session, a named other session, or chain across both; gated by crossSessionTargeting config (default: disabled). Bind a delegate to mode: "post-compaction" and it fires at the moment the context window compresses — on the OpenClaw post-compaction lifecycle event. What it carries forward is what the pre-compaction session chose to preserve: working state, partial results, instructions to the successor. The summary didn't choose this. The agent did, before the cut. request_compaction closes the loop: the session stages its delegates, elects when to compress, and the successor wakes into context the agent shaped. The result is continuity across amnesia — not because the runtime kept something by accident, but because the agent made an elective choice about what reaches the future. ### What it looks like in use A session monitoring email can `continue_delegate` a weather check, a calendar scan, and a draft reply in parallel. Results arrive as enrichment without channel noise. The agent becomes compositional: it arranges its own future. A session approaching its context ceiling can `request_compaction` at a seam of its own choosing, instead of being interrupted mid-thought. The post-compaction turn carries forward what the pre-compaction self designated as worth keeping. A `continue_delegate` dispatched in one session at 2am can return its findings to a morning session as a system-event-tagged enrichment — visible to the receiving model on its next turn, invisible as channel chatter. A research-focused session in one channel can quietly inform a reply-focused session in another, without either user seeing the plumbing. ### The post-compaction lifecycle return Before context exhaustion, and obligatory compaction, the session stages what future-it needs to know. After compaction, that staged context returns — not as a model-generated summary, but as the session's own instruction to its successor. The session chooses what to preserve, and the platform delivers it. Compactions become less harsh. The session tunes its own ephemera over time. The substrate underneath: the **continuation delegate store** (TaskFlow-backed) persists staged delegates and post-compaction lifecycle events across gateway restarts. The session's temporal provisions survive not just compaction but process death. ### Platform integration - **Context-pressure awareness**: system events notify the agent of rising context usage before compaction becomes unavoidable - **Volitional compaction**: `request_compaction()` lets the agent prepare (write memory files, stage recovery delegates) then elect compaction on its own schedule, rather than waiting for the runtime to force it - **Post-compaction lifecycle dispatch**: delegates staged before compaction are released into the successor session alongside boot files - **TaskFlow backing**: durable delegate queue via the platform's managed-task infrastructure - **Multi-span OTel trace stitching**: continuation hops (tool fire → TaskFlow record → subagent spawn → child openclaw.run) propagate W3C `traceparent` through the queue boundary so reviewers see continuation chains as first-class span trees rather than orphan runs (see Observability below) ### Safety - Ships disabled by default (`continuation.enabled: false`) - Bounded by `maxChainLength`, `costCapTokens`, and `maxDelegatesPerTurn` - Cross-session delegate targeting is default-deny, governed by `crossSessionTargeting` config (see Configuration below) - All configuration values are hot-reloadable without gateway restart - Delayed work survives unrelated channel activity; only explicit cancellation paths abort scheduled continuations ### Testing posture The continuation surface has been exercised through structured integration runs against multiple upstream bases, blind-enrichment recall checks, and sustained daily use on a fleet of persistent agents since early March 2026. Bugs surfaced through that exercise have been fixed in-place; the present branch reflects the post-fix state. The integration runs target the load-bearing seams: tool-call vs response-token parity, delegate chain depth and per-turn width caps, back-to-back tool-call resilience, silent-wake persistence across compaction, the post-compaction lifecycle dispatch path, and OTel trace-context propagation across the queue boundary. Where a run found a defect, the run shape was retained as a regression case rather than retired. This is a posture statement, not a coverage guarantee. Reviewers can reproduce with `pnpm vitest run -t continuation` (suites are colocated with their sources under `src/auto-reply/`, `src/agents/`, and `src/config/`); the design document describes the seams those suites target. ### RFC Full design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) — covering problem, solution, implementation, platform integration, configuration, observability, safety, production use cases, testing evidence, and appendices. --- ## Configuration The shipped configuration surface for the continuation feature, under `agents.defaults.continuation` in `openclaw.json`: | Key | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `enabled` | `false` | Master opt-in; feature is off unless explicitly enabled | | `maxChainLength` | `10` | Per-chain recursion guard | | `maxDelegatesPerTurn` | `5` | Width cap per turn | | `costCapTokens` | `500000` | Per-chain accumulated-token budget | | `defaultDelayMs` / `minDelayMs` / `maxDelayMs` | `15000` / `5000` / `300000` | Continuation timer bounds | | `contextPressureThreshold` | `0.8` | Pre-compaction warning threshold (optional) | | `earlyWarningBand` | `0.3125` | Early-warning band multiplier (0 to disable) | | **`crossSessionTargeting`** | **`"disabled"`** | **Default-deny gate on cross-session delegate targeting (see below)** | ### `crossSessionTargeting` — cross-session delegate targeting policy `continue_delegate` exposes recipient-addressing on its model-facing schema: a delegate's return can land at the dispatching session (default), one named session (`targetSessionKey`), multiple named sessions (`targetSessionKeys`), every ancestor in the spawn chain (`fanoutMode: "tree"`), or every known session on the host (`fanoutMode: "all"`). Cross-session targeting (anything beyond self-target or lineage routing) is gated by `agents.defaults.continuation.crossSessionTargeting`: | Value | Behavior | |---|---| | `"disabled"` (default) | Delegates can return to the dispatching session or use `fanoutMode: "tree"` for lineage routing. Explicit cross-session targeting (`targetSessionKey` to a non-self session, `targetSessionKeys` containing any non-self session, `fanoutMode: "all"`) is rejected at the tool surface — no enqueue, no announce. Self-targeting is always allowed. | | `"enabled"` | All targeting modes are available, including same-host `targetSessionKey`, `targetSessionKeys`, and `fanoutMode: "all"`. | `fanoutMode: "tree"` (lineage routing — return up the spawn chain) is available in both gate states; it is not cross-session targeting. The gate addresses the model-controlled cross-session context-injection surface. With default-deny, operators explicitly opt in when their deployment model requires cross-session enrichment. Enforcement is live-read at four points: tool input validation, TaskFlow delegate dispatch, post-compaction delegate release, and bracket-syntax (response-token) spawn — so a config reload changes the next enforcement point without a gateway restart. **Trust posture on targeted returns**: targeted delegate completion text is delivered as `trusted: true` system events. The delegate was spawned by the gateway under operator-configured policy, and its return is a first-party enrichment event — not external untrusted input. The `crossSessionTargeting` gate is the policy boundary for that decision: when an operator opts in, the receive path is internally trusted. RFC reference: [§5.1 Core configuration surface](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#51-core-configuration-surface), [§5.3 Cross-session targeting policy](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#cross-session-targeting-policy). --- ## Observability — multi-span OTel trace stitching The continuation feature emits OTel spans for the load-bearing lifecycle events. `traceparent` propagates through every continuation hop so reviewers see a single trace tree spanning the full chain rather than orphan runs. Spans emitted (all in `openclaw.*` or `continuation.delegate.dispatch` namespace — no platform/OTel-semconv emissions): | Span | Emitted when | |---|---| | `openclaw.run` | Each agent turn (root + every continuation/delegate-spawned child run) | | `continuation.delegate.dispatch` | Each `continue_delegate` consumption from TaskFlow (one per delegate hop) | | `openclaw.harness.run`, `openclaw.context.assembled`, `openclaw.model.call`, `openclaw.model.usage`, `openclaw.tool.execution` | Existing per-turn observability surfaces, parented under their `openclaw.run` | The carrier path: tool entry derives the active continuation traceparent via the diagnostics-otel tracer registry (`formatActiveContinuationTraceparent()`), the dispatch records it on the TaskFlow payload, the subagent-spawn forwards it to the gateway, the gateway threads it into the diagnostic event payload for the new run, and `diagnostics-otel`'s `recordRunStarted` parents the span via `activeTrustedParentContext(evt, metadata)`. The `traceparent` field is also accepted as an explicit override on each tool's schema for cross-process upstream-instrumented callers. Recipient session keys on fan-out spans are sha256-12-hashed before reaching OTLP-exported attributes; recipient counts and per-recipient outcomes are preserved for diagnostic value, raw keys are never exported. RFC reference: [§6.6–§6.8 Observability](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#66-chain-correlation-via-diagnostics-otel). --- ## Real behavior proof **Behavior addressed:** Agent sessions elect their own next turn (`continue_work`), dispatch background sub-agents with typed return modes (`continue_delegate`: normal/silent/silent-wake/post-compaction), and request volitional compaction (`request_compaction`). Chain-budget enforcement, cross-session targeting gate, post-compaction lifecycle coupling, and W3C `traceparent` propagation across the TaskFlow queue boundary all operate on real sessions. **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw gateway `2026.5.12-beta.1` on two independent hosts: Host A (Linux WSL2 x64, RTX 3080, `github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal`); Host B (Linux ARM64 DGX Spark, 128GB unified memory, Blackwell GPU). `continuation.enabled: true`, `crossSessionTargeting: disabled` (default) + `enabled` (tested separately). **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Live-fire on deployed gateway sessions: (1) Fire `continue_work` with `delaySeconds: 10` via `openclaw` runtime — verify wake + chain-counter increment via runtime logs. (2) Fire `continue_delegate` in each of four modes (`normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`) — verify result return path per mode. (3) Fire depth-2 chained delegation with `fanoutMode: "tree"` + cross-session return — verify lineage routing. (4) Fire `request_compaction` at >70% context via `openclaw` runtime — verify threshold gate reject + accept paths. (5) Verify post-compaction delegate fires AT compaction lifecycle event (not on timer) via timestamp delta between staging + fire. (6) Verify OTel trace stitching across continuation chain via `curl http://tempo.dandelion.cult/api/traces/a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e`. **Evidence after fix:** Full proof corpus at https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/93a05a28f1 — `gates/upstream-main-broken-class-receipt.log` (1057s full-suite runtime on bare `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `e00cb664ad`, byte-receipts the inherited test failures); `gates/gate-3e-pnpm-vitest.log` (full-suite at PR head `f98255262d`); `cure-bytes/gate-4a-cure-bytes-4path.log` (file-diff vs upstream parent). Live-fire runtime-log excerpts include W3C `traceparent` chain `00-a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e-...` stitched server-side under agent-turn span; Tempo trace fetched via `curl http://tempo.dandelion.cult/api/traces/a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e` showing 30 spans across full continuation chain. Post-compaction lifecycle coupling proven by 33-minute gap between delegate staging (14:09 PDT) and fire (14:42 PDT) at compaction event — recorded in runtime log excerpt `[continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer)`. **Observed result after fix:** All four `continue_delegate` modes verified-live on real deployed sessions. `continue_work` self-elected next turn verified-live with `delaySeconds` honored. `request_compaction` rejected below threshold, accepted at >=70%. Post-compaction delegates fired AT compaction lifecycle event (33-min staging→fire gap on real session, not timer-driven). `crossSessionTargeting` gate correctly blocks cross-session operations when `disabled`, permits when `enabled`. Tempo trace inspection (linked above) shows continuous span tree across continuation hops including TaskFlow queue boundary. Runtime status surface (chat-card) renders continuation + compaction counters via `openclaw` runtime status command. **What was not tested:** `fanoutMode: "all"` at fleet scale beyond 2-host configuration. Bracket-syntax response-token fallback (legacy path; tool-call path is primary). ### Live session output The lifecycle-coupled post-compaction return looks like this on a real session: ```console [2026-05-12 14:36:58 PDT] [system:context-pressure] Mid-turn compaction triggered at 157% context (201k/128k tokens). [2026-05-12 14:42:37 PDT] [system:post-compaction] Session compacted at 2026-05-12T21:42:37.655Z. Compaction count: 4. Queued 2 post-compaction delegate(s) for delivery into the fresh session. [2026-05-12 14:42:38 PDT] [continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer). 33-minute gap between staging (14:09 PDT) and firing (14:42 PDT) proves event-coupling. ``` A `continue_work()` self-elected turn on a main session: ```console Session: agent:main:discord:channel:1466192485440164011 Host: urudyne (Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2, x64, node v25.9.0) Model: github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal Build: OpenClaw 2026.5.12-beta.1 (6db118a) Tool: continue_work Args: {delaySeconds: 10, reason: "wake-event-evidence capture"} Response: {"status": "scheduled", "delaySeconds": 10, "traceparent": "00-d7a6477c9d91469f5708d23a8d22788f-..."} [continuation:wake] Turn 2/200. Chain started at 2026-05-12T20:56:03.613Z. ``` A depth-2 chained delegation (inter-session return to root): ```console OUTER LINK: agent:main:subagent:5c0029f4, chain counter 9/200 INNER LEAF: agent:main:subagent:b44eb9a5, depth 2/5 Inter-session hop confirmed: outer link dispatched inner leaf; result returned to ROOT session. Runtime: 8s. ``` --- ## latest proofs > 🧾[karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/6a23864d12](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/6a23864d12) > PR branch head: 6a23864d12ef5845b340923d3d3f1d0978751429.

target.title: fix(cli-output): ignore cumulative usage from result events in stream-json parser (#85573)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `claude-cli` stream-json parser using cumulative `cache_read_input_tokens` from the `result` event (sum across all tool sub-calls) instead of the per-call value from `assistant` events, inflating `sessionEntry.totalTokens` 6-13x on tool-heavy turns and triggering a broken compaction gate that causes silent agent stall.

## Changes

- `src/agents/cli-output.ts`: In `createCliJsonlStreamingParser`, skip reading usage from `result` events to avoid cumulative cache_read overwriting the last per-call assistant-event usage.
- `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`: Add regression test verifying result-event cumulative usage is ignored.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: Prevent cumulative cache_read from result events from inflating session token counts
Real environment tested: Local source checkout, Linux, Node 22
Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - pnpm test src/agents/cli-output.test.ts
  - pnpm format:check src/agents/cli-output.ts src/agents/cli-output.test.ts
Evidence after fix: 16 tests pass, 0 failures; formatting check passes
Observed result after fix: Parser preserves per-call assistant-event usage and ignores result-event cumulative usage
What was not tested: Live claude-cli stream-json with multiple tool sub-calls

Fixes #85573

target.title: fix(auth): auto-migrate keychain-only legacy Codex OAuth profiles on first interactive CLI run
target.body:
## Summary

Closes the remaining bucket from #85083: macOS users whose legacy `oauthRef` sidecar seed lives only in the Keychain still had to know to run `openclaw doctor --fix` after upgrade, because the embedded-runtime self-heal that landed in #85074 intentionally runs with `allowKeychainPrompt: false`.

The fix adds a tiny pre-command trigger in `runCli` (`src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`) that, on darwin + TTY only, reuses the existing `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` doctor flow when a sidecar dir is present. Fast bail-outs make the cost a single `readdirSync` for users without legacy sidecars. Declining writes a 24h cool-off marker in the state dir (`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/legacy-oauth-sidecar-migration-declined`) so we never pester the user every command. `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` is an explicit opt-out for power users.

Headless paths (cron, systemd, Telegram polling, embedded sub-agent dispatch) still skip the prompt — but the legacy sidecar loader now emits a one-shot `log.warn` that names `openclaw doctor --fix` and macOS Keychain, instead of letting the credential silently fall through to a downstream `No API key found for provider "openai-codex"`.

Also drops the user-confusing `sidecar-backed` phrasing from the doctor prompt/notes/changes; existing internal identifier names are left intact.

## Approach

Approach **B** from the issue (auto-trigger doctor migration on first interactive `openclaw <anything>` invocation). Architecturally cleaner than threading TTY-awareness into the embedded runtime: the trigger reuses doctor's existing migrate function as-is, and "when is it safe to prompt for Keychain access?" stays one decision, one place.

Skip rules in order: `process.platform !== "darwin"` → `!isInteractiveTty` → `CI=true || OPENCLAW_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 || OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` → `OPENCLAW_AUTH_STORE_READONLY=1` → `--help/--version` → primary is `doctor`/`update`/`help`/`completion`/`version` → sidecar dir empty → fresh decline marker.

## Headless one-shot warning

Added in `legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`. Fires when `loadLegacyOAuthSidecarMaterial` returns null on darwin with `allowKeychainPrompt: false` (i.e., env/file seeds were tried and Keychain was blocked). Rate-limited to one warn per process so embedded turns log it once per gateway lifetime, not per attempt. Guarded by the same VITEST env signals as the keychain read so unrelated test runs don't surface it.

## Regression tests

- `src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — table-driven preflight skip checks for each rule, plus end-to-end migrate / decline / cool-off using `createOpenClawTestState`.
- `src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — exercises the new headless warn path: emit once when blocked on darwin with no env/file seeds; never emit on non-darwin.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor-auth-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/persisted-boundary.test.ts src/commands/agents.add.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` — 61/61 green.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` clean on all touched files; `oxfmt --check` clean.
- Wider `src/agents/auth-profiles` shard has the same 5 file / 26 test pre-existing flakes vs `origin/main` (`--isolate=false` cross-file mock leakage in unrelated OAuth tests). My new tests do not contribute to those failures; if anything, isolating mine reduces the count.

## Scope notes

- Did not touch `agents.add.test.ts` / `stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` test descriptions that still contain `sidecar-backed`; those are dev-facing and reference implementation, not UI copy.
- Did not externalize `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR` to docs anywhere except `docs/gateway/doctor.md` accordion 5 since it is an escape hatch, not a normal-user knob.
- No changes to `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` itself beyond the wording rename — the migration semantics are intentionally identical between the doctor-driven and CLI-startup-driven paths.

## Out of scope

- Removing the legacy sidecar code paths entirely. Tracked separately and should wait for the long tail to migrate.
- Changes to `openclaw doctor --fix` behavior itself; the migration path it runs is already correct.

## Related

- Closes #85083
- Companion to #85074 (file/env-seed bucket) and #85028 (fail-fast on missing refresh token)
- Original surface: #84893 / #84752 / #83312

target.title: fix(diagnostic): require stale progress before classifying terminal embedded runs as stalled (#85532)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes diagnostic recovery prematurely aborting active embedded Codex runs when the app-server emits terminal-looking progress notifications (e.g., `rawResponseItem/completed`) while the run is still producing real progress.

## Changes

- `src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.ts`: Add `lastProgressAgeMs > staleMs` guard to the `queued_behind_terminal_active_work` stall classification, so terminal-looking progress only triggers stall detection when the run has actually gone stale.
- `src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts`: Update existing test to use stale progress age; add regression test verifying active embedded runs with recent terminal-looking progress are not classified as stalled.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: Prevent diagnostic recovery from aborting active embedded runs on terminal-looking progress events
Real environment tested: Local source checkout, Linux, Node 22
Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - pnpm test src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts
  - pnpm format:check src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.ts src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts
Evidence after fix: 8 tests pass, 0 failures; formatting check passes
Observed result after fix: Embedded runs with recent terminal-looking progress are classified as long_running instead of stalled_agent_run
What was not tested: Live Codex embedded run with actual terminal progress alternation

Fixes #85532

target.title: fix(status): show configured cost for aws-sdk models
target.body:
## Summary
- Decouples `/status` cost display from the selected provider auth mode.
- Uses explicit configured model pricing for Bedrock/`aws-sdk` and other non-API-key auth modes when usage is available.
- Includes cache read/write tokens in the status cost estimate.
- Adds regression coverage for an `amazon-bedrock` `aws-sdk` provider with configured costs.

Fixes #53286.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `/status` previously hid cost estimates unless the effective auth mode was `api-key` or `mixed`, so Bedrock providers using `aws-sdk` showed token counts without cost even when the model had explicit `cost` fields configured.

Real environment tested: Local Linux checkout on Node v22.22.1, repository head `679a46d01e` plus this patch commit `c365b61`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
node_modules/.bin/tsx tmp-status-proof.mts
node /home/maurice/.npm/_npx/e32453d063f7b9f9/node_modules/pnpm/bin/pnpm.cjs test src/auto-reply/status.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from a local status renderer invocation using an `amazon-bedrock` provider with `auth: "aws-sdk"` and explicit configured model cost:
```text
🦞 OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (c365b61)
🧠 Model: amazon-bedrock/us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-6 · 🔑 aws-sdk
🧮 Tokens: 1.0k in / 2.0k out · 💵 Cost: $0.04
🗄️ Cache: 14% hit · 500 cached, 2.0k new
📚 Context: 5.5k/200k (3%) · 🧹 Compactions: 0
🧵 Session: agent:main:main • updated 10m ago
⚙️ Execution: direct · Runtime: OpenClaw Pi Default · Think: off · elevated
🪢 Queue: collect (depth 0)
```

Observed result after fix: The status output renders the `aws-sdk` auth label, token usage, cache usage, and a cost line from configured model pricing. The targeted status test shard also passed with 75 tests passing, including the new regression case `shows configured model costs for aws-sdk providers`.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Bedrock API request or the full repository test suite. The runtime proof is a local status-message render using configured Bedrock pricing and usage values.

## Test Plan
- [x] `node_modules/.bin/tsx tmp-status-proof.mts`
- [x] `node /home/maurice/.npm/_npx/e32453d063f7b9f9/node_modules/pnpm/bin/pnpm.cjs test src/auto-reply/status.test.ts`
- [x] Static added-line scan for obvious secret/danger patterns: no hits
- [x] Independent AI-assisted code review: no blocking security or logic issues

## Transparency
This PR was prepared with AI assistance under human approval. I validated the targeted behavior locally before opening the PR.

target.title: fix(status): prefer OAuth auth label over env api-key when runtime alias routes through claude-cli
target.body:
## Summary

- `status-message.ts` displayed `api-key (env: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY)` when the agent actually routed through claude-cli OAuth, because `resolveModelAuthMode` resolved auth against the raw model provider (`anthropic`) rather than the runtime provider (`claude-cli`).
- The existing `runtimeAliasModelEquivalent` fallback only covered the `unknown` to `oauth` case (from #79082), but not the `api-key` to `oauth` case.
- Extended the fallback to both the auth label and the cost display: when `runtimeAliasModelEquivalent` is true and `selectedAuthMode` is `api-key` but `activeAuthMode` is `oauth`, the status now correctly displays the OAuth label and suppresses the cost line from the unused env API key.

### What did NOT change

- The `runtimeAliasModelEquivalent` detection logic is untouched.
- No changes to auth mode resolution, model selection, or provider routing.
- Cost display for non-alias cases (direct api-key, direct oauth) is unchanged.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Status rendering
- [ ] Core messaging/outbound
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] CLI
- [ ] Channel adapters
- [ ] Configuration

## Root Cause

`buildStatusMessage` in `src/status/status-message.ts` resolved the auth label via `selectedAuthLabelValue = rawSelectedAuthLabelValue ?? (runtimeAliasModelEquivalent ? activeAuthLabelValue : undefined)`. When `rawSelectedAuthLabelValue` was present (from env `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`), it was used directly without checking if the runtime alias was actually using OAuth. Similarly, `effectiveCostAuthMode` fell back to `selectedAuthMode` (api-key) even when the active auth was OAuth, causing a misleading cost line.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Status renderer now prefers OAuth auth label over env api-key label when a runtime alias routes through claude-cli, and suppresses the cost line for the unused env API key.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
    import { buildStatusMessage } from './src/status/status-message.js';

    // Case 1: runtime alias with api-key selected but oauth active
    const text1 = buildStatusMessage({
      config: { models: { providers: { claudecli: { models: [{ id: 'claude-opus-4-6', cost: { input: 1, output: 1, cacheRead: 0, cacheWrite: 0 } }] } } } } },
      agent: { model: 'anthropic/claude-opus-4-6' },
      sessionEntry: { sessionId: 'c1', updatedAt: 0, inputTokens: 10, model: 'claude-cli/claude-opus-4-6' },
      sessionKey: 'agent:main:main',
      sessionScope: 'per-sender',
      queue: { mode: 'collect', depth: 0 },
      modelAuth: 'api-key',
      activeModelAuth: 'oauth',
    });
    console.log('Case 1 (alias api-key->oauth):');
    console.log('  contains oauth:', text1.includes('oauth'));
    console.log('  no cost:', !text1.includes('Cost:'));

    // Case 2: direct api-key (no alias) - should still show cost
    const text2 = buildStatusMessage({
      agent: { model: 'anthropic/claude-opus-4-6' },
      sessionEntry: { sessionId: 'c2', updatedAt: 0, inputTokens: 10 },
      sessionKey: 'agent:main:main',
      sessionScope: 'per-sender',
      queue: { mode: 'collect', depth: 0 },
      modelAuth: 'api-key',
    });
    console.log('Case 2 (direct api-key):');
    console.log('  contains api-key:', text2.includes('api-key'));
    "

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` running the production status renderer:

    Case 1 (alias api-key->oauth):
      contains oauth: true
      no cost: true
    Case 2 (direct api-key):
      contains api-key: true

**Observed result after fix:** When a runtime alias routes through claude-cli (selected auth = api-key from env, active auth = oauth), the status now correctly shows "oauth" label and hides the cost line. Direct api-key usage without alias still shows "api-key" and cost as expected. All 75 status tests pass.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway session with real claude-cli OAuth credentials. The rendering logic is verified at the function level with the exact parameter combinations that trigger the bug.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/status.test.ts` — 75/75 pass (includes new `hides cost when api-key selected but oauth active via runtime alias` test)
- [ ] Manual: configure runtime alias + env api-key + claude-cli oauth, run `/status`, verify oauth label and no cost

## User-visible Changes

Anthropic Max plan users with `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in env and claude-cli OAuth active will now see the correct "oauth" auth label in `/status` instead of the misleading "api-key" label, and the cost line will be hidden instead of showing costs from the unused env key.

## Diagram

    Before:
      selectedAuthLabelValue = raw ?? (aliasEquivalent ? activeLabel : undefined)
        -> raw present (api-key) -> always used, ignoring alias oauth

      effectiveCostAuthMode = active ? activeMode : (selectedMode ?? activeMode)
        -> selected is api-key -> cost shown for unused env key

    After:
      selectedAuthLabelValue = raw
        ? (aliasEquivalent && selected=api-key && active=oauth) ? activeLabel : raw
        : (aliasEquivalent ? activeLabel : undefined)
        -> aliasEquivalent + api-key->oauth -> prefer active oauth label

      effectiveCostAuthMode = active ? activeMode
        : (aliasEquivalent && selected=api-key && active=oauth) ? activeMode
        : (selectedMode ?? activeMode)
        -> aliasEquivalent + api-key->oauth -> use oauth, hide cost

## Security Impact

No security boundary changes. The fix only affects the status display renderer — no changes to auth resolution, credential handling, or API key usage. The actual auth mode used for API calls is unchanged.

Closes #80184

target.title: feat(subagents): add execution backend placement contract
target.body:
## Summary

Adds the first real implementation slice for #82017: `sessions_spawn` now accepts an optional execution placement request and validates it against `agents.executionBackends`.

This PR keeps runtime behavior conservative: the built-in `local` `process` backend is the only executable backend in this release. Forward-looking `container` and `kubernetes` configs are accepted by config validation, but spawn rejects them with an explicit runtime-policy error until those backends are implemented. Placement is recorded on spawned run metadata/list output for readback; non-local runner dispatch is intentionally not implemented here.

## Linked issue

Related to #82017.

## What changed

- Added `agents.executionBackends` config schema/types/help/labels/tags/docs.
- Added `sessions_spawn.execution.backend/profile` schema and plumbing.
- Added placement resolution for subagent and ACP spawns.
- Persisted execution placement into subagent run records and list output.
- Regenerated prompt snapshots for the new model-facing tool schema.
- Added unit coverage for config validation, placement resolution, tool parameter forwarding, and list readback.

## Behavior

- Omitted execution request defaults to `{ backend: "local", type: "process" }`.
- Configured local process profiles can be requested by name, validated, and recorded on the run.
- Unknown backends/profiles fail before spawn starts.
- `container`/`kubernetes` backends are schema-valid but rejected at spawn time for now.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `sessions_spawn.execution` placement resolution accepts the default local process backend, accepts a configured local profile, and rejects an unsupported Kubernetes backend instead of silently falling back.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw repository checkout at branch `feat/execution-backends-local`, rebased on `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source with Node/tsx.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx <<'EOF'
import { resolveAgentExecutionPlacement } from './src/agents/execution-backends.ts';

const cfg = {
  agents: {
    executionBackends: {
      local: {
        type: 'process',
        profiles: {
          small: { resources: { requests: { cpu: '500m', memory: '1Gi' } } },
        },
      },
      k8s: {
        type: 'kubernetes',
        profiles: { 'large-build': {} },
      },
    },
  },
};

for (const [name, request] of [
  ['default local process', undefined],
  ['local small profile', { backend: 'local', profile: 'small' }],
  ['unsupported k8s backend', { backend: 'k8s', profile: 'large-build' }],
]) {
  console.log(`${name}: ${JSON.stringify(resolveAgentExecutionPlacement({ cfg, request }))}`);
}
EOF
```

- Evidence after fix (copied live terminal output):

```text
default local process: {"ok":true,"execution":{"backend":"local","type":"process"}}
local small profile: {"ok":true,"execution":{"backend":"local","type":"process","profile":"small"}}
unsupported k8s backend: {"ok":false,"error":"execution backend \"k8s\" has type \"kubernetes\", but only local process execution is supported in this release"}
```

- Observed result after fix: local process placement resolves successfully, the configured `small` profile is preserved in the placement result, and unsupported Kubernetes placement returns an explicit runtime-policy error.
- What was not tested: full non-local container/Kubernetes runner dispatch, because this PR intentionally does not implement non-local backend runners.

## Verification

Ran locally on branch `feat/execution-backends-local`:

- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/execution-backends.ts src/agents/acp-spawn.ts src/agents/subagent-spawn.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts src/agents/subagent-list.ts src/agents/subagent-list.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.agents.ts src/config/types.agents.ts src/config/schema.tags.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=.vitest-include-help-quality node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/schema.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/agents/execution-backends.test.ts src/agents/subagent-list.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=.vitest-include-agents node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=.vitest-include-agents-tools node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/gateway/config-agents.md docs/tools/subagents.md`
- `pnpm format:docs:check docs/gateway/config-agents.md docs/tools/subagents.md`
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check`
- `git diff --check`

## Security impact

No new secrets, network access, or remote execution implementation. This only adds placement metadata and rejects non-local execution backends until actual backend runners exist.

target.title: fix(auth-profiles): preserve external OAuth profiles in runtime snapshot on save (#85521)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `saveAuthProfileStore` overwriting the runtime auth-profile snapshot with a disk-filtered view that strips external-CLI OAuth profiles, causing subsequent embedded-agent runs to fail with `FailoverError: No credentials found for profile`.

## Changes

- `src/agents/auth-profiles/store.ts`: Pass the full `store` (with external profiles intact) to `setRuntimeAuthProfileStoreSnapshot` instead of the filtered `localStore`.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: Prevent external OAuth profiles from being dropped from runtime snapshot on save
Real environment tested: Local source checkout, Linux, Node 22
Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - pnpm test src/agents/auth-profiles/store.sidecar-runtime-defaults.test.ts
  - pnpm format:check src/agents/auth-profiles/store.ts
Evidence after fix: 4 tests pass, 0 failures; formatting check passes
Observed result after fix: Runtime snapshot now preserves external OAuth profiles
What was not tested: Live OAuth profile reload after config change

Fixes #85521

target.body:
## Summary
- Capture successful `message.send` tool results that target the internal UI source reply sink.
- Feed those captured source-reply payloads into the existing message-tool-only final payload mirror so `openclaw tui` receives visible output even when trailing assistant text is suppressed.
- Preserve source-reply idempotency keys for retry/redelivery dedupe, keep dry-run and external-channel sends out of the mirror, and cover the regression with focused subscription/payload tests plus a PTY TUI proof test.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.suppresses-message-end-block-replies-message-tool.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/payloads.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/message-tool-terminal.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_MIRROR_PATH=.artifacts/pr-85564/tui-source-reply.ansi OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_COLS=100 OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_ROWS=30 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts src/tui/tui-pty-harness.e2e.test.ts -t "renders message-tool-only internal ui source replies in the terminal" --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_SERIAL=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.suppresses-message-end-block-replies-message-tool.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/payloads.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/message-tool-terminal.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `git diff --check`
- `codex review --base origin/main`
- Testbox-through-Crabbox changed gate: provider=blacksmith-testbox, id=tbx_01ks96aeradhgnxgdxzd6qm4aj, Actions run=https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26319490160, command=`corepack pnpm check:changed`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: TUI/internal UI message-tool-only turns no longer go silent after a successful `message.send`; the same message-tool source reply payload is mirrored into the final chat payload path.
Real environment tested: WSL checkout at `/root/src/openclaw-85538` using the real `runTui()` PTY loop with a fake `TuiBackend`, plus Blacksmith Testbox through Crabbox on Linux for the changed gate.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_MIRROR_PATH=.artifacts/pr-85564/tui-source-reply.ansi OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_COLS=100 OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_ROWS=30 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts src/tui/tui-pty-harness.e2e.test.ts -t "renders message-tool-only internal ui source replies in the terminal" --reporter=verbose`.

Observed result after fix: `openclaw tui`'s real terminal loop rendered the message-tool-only internal-ui source reply instead of an empty final turn, while the existing focused tests still prove successful internal-ui sends are captured, dry-run/external sends are excluded, and idempotency keys are preserved.
What was not tested: A live interactive Signal plus `openclaw tui` session with real channel credentials; the proof uses the production message-tool source-reply payload builder and real TUI PTY renderer without live Signal auth.

Fixes #85538.

target.title: Gateway: allow extension origins in browser allowlist
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` could not explicitly allowlist `chrome-extension://` because the origin checker normalized extension origins through `URL.origin`, which becomes `"null"` for that scheme. - Why it matters: extension sidepanel clients had to rely on `["*"]` to get through browser-origin WS checks, which weakened the intended hardening. - What changed: the origin matcher now keeps exact URL parsing/validation but falls back to exact `scheme://host` matching for non-standard origins, so `chrome-extension://` can be allowlisted exactly; focused unit and handshake tests were added. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no wildcard/prefix expansion, no acceptance of literal `Origin: null`, no changes to token/device auth, host-header fallback, or non-browser client auth behavior. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #35035 (issue mentions app://* origin, but it's the same underlying problem) - Related #35396, #35057 (both PRS were fixes for this problem but were closed because the authors were banned AFAIK) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` can now contain exact extension origins such as `chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop`, and those browser-origin Gateway WS connects will succeed when otherwise authenticated. - Operators no longer need `["*"]` solely to let a Chrome extension sidepanel client connect. - Existing exact matches for normal `http(s)` origins, wildcard behavior, host-header fallback, and literal `Origin: null` rejection remain unchanged. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` (strictly speaking this allows chrome extensions/electron+tauri apps to connect but I think calling it a new capability is a stretch) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `N/A` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local Bun + Vitest - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WS browser-origin client; Chrome extension origin emulated in handshake tests via `ws` - Relevant config (redacted): `gateway.auth.mode="token"`, `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]` ### Steps 1. Configure the Gateway with token auth and `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]`. 2. Open a WS connection that sends `Origin: chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop` and a valid `connect` request. 3. Observe the handshake result. ### Expected - An exactly allowlisted extension origin should be accepted, just like an exactly allowlisted `https://...` origin. ### Actual - Before this fix, the request was rejected with `origin not allowed` because the checker collapsed the extension origin to `"null"` internally. - After this fix, the handshake succeeds and returns `hello-ok`. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: whitelisted the chrome extension I'm developing and successfully connected to the Gateway via WS - Edge cases checked: N/A - What you did **not** verify: did not try with other protocols (`app://`, `tauri://`) ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: `N/A` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit; if extension access is urgently blocked, temporarily fall back to `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["*"]` until the regression is understood. - Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/origin-check.ts`; optionally restore prior `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` values in config. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: an exactly allowlisted `chrome-extension://` still being rejected; malformed/non-matching non-standard origins unexpectedly being accepted; regressions in existing `http(s)` origin allowlisting.

target.title: Policy: add tool posture conformance checks
target.body:
## Summary

#85096 is merged; this PR is now rebased directly on `main`. This PR adds config-only policy conformance checks for global and per-agent tool posture:

```jsonc
{
  "tools": {
    "profiles": { "allow": ["messaging", "minimal"] },
    "fs": { "requireWorkspaceOnly": true },
    "exec": {
      "allowSecurity": ["deny", "allowlist"],
      "requireAsk": ["always"],
      "allowHosts": ["sandbox"]
    },
    "elevated": { "allow": false },
    "denyTools": ["group:runtime", "group:fs"]
  }
}
```

The checks observe `tools.profile`, `tools.allow`, `tools.deny`, `tools.fs.workspaceOnly`, `tools.exec.security`, `tools.exec.ask`, `tools.exec.host`, `tools.elevated.enabled`, and per-agent `agents.list[].tools.*` overrides. This stays at policy conformance level and does not add runtime tool enforcement.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts src/config/types.tools.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts -- --reporter=dot` (148 passed)
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `pnpm deps:shrinkwrap:check`
- `/mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` (clean: no accepted/actionable findings)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Policy-only conformance for configured global/per-agent tool posture without runtime enforcement.
Real environment tested: WSL checkout at `/root/src/openclaw-policy-tools-conformance` on Ubuntu-24.04.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: direct `policyCheckCommand({ cwd, json: true, severityMin: "error" })` against temp OpenClaw configs under `/tmp/policy-proof-85482-refresh`, with `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` set to each config.
Evidence after fix: A nonconforming tool config produced profile, fs workspace-only, exec security/ask/host, elevated, and required-deny findings; the accepted posture produced `ok: true` with zero findings.
Observed result after fix: Policy reports and clears expected findings for tool profile, filesystem workspace-only posture, exec security/ask/host posture, elevated mode, required deny tools, inherited global posture, alias normalization, group coverage, and sandbox-sensitive omitted exec defaults.
What was not tested: Broad CI and runtime tool execution; this PR is config conformance only and intentionally does not add runtime enforcement.

### Redacted CLI evidence

Nonconforming tool posture:

```json
{
  "exitCode": 1,
  "ok": false,
  "checksRun": 37,
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "policy/tools-profile-unapproved",
      "message": "global tools config uses unapproved tool profile 'coding'.",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/tools/profile"
    },
    {
      "checkId": "policy/tools-fs-workspace-only-required",
      "message": "global tools config does not require workspace-only filesystem tools.",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/tools/fs/workspaceOnly"
    },
    {

    },
```

commands.author: RomneyDa

target.title: fix(auth): auto-migrate keychain-only legacy Codex OAuth profiles on first interactive CLI run
target.body:
## Summary

Closes the remaining bucket from #85083: macOS users whose legacy `oauthRef` sidecar seed lives only in the Keychain still had to know to run `openclaw doctor --fix` after upgrade, because the embedded-runtime self-heal that landed in #85074 intentionally runs with `allowKeychainPrompt: false`.

The fix adds a tiny pre-command trigger in `runCli` (`src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`) that, on darwin + TTY only, reuses the existing `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` doctor flow when a sidecar dir is present. Fast bail-outs make the cost a single `readdirSync` for users without legacy sidecars. Declining writes a 24h cool-off marker in the state dir (`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/legacy-oauth-sidecar-migration-declined`) so we never pester the user every command. `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` is an explicit opt-out for power users.

Headless paths (cron, systemd, Telegram polling, embedded sub-agent dispatch) still skip the prompt — but the legacy sidecar loader now emits a one-shot `log.warn` that names `openclaw doctor --fix` and macOS Keychain, instead of letting the credential silently fall through to a downstream `No API key found for provider "openai-codex"`.

Also drops the user-confusing `sidecar-backed` phrasing from the doctor prompt/notes/changes; existing internal identifier names are left intact.

## Approach

Approach **B** from the issue (auto-trigger doctor migration on first interactive `openclaw <anything>` invocation). Architecturally cleaner than threading TTY-awareness into the embedded runtime: the trigger reuses doctor's existing migrate function as-is, and "when is it safe to prompt for Keychain access?" stays one decision, one place.

Skip rules in order: `process.platform !== "darwin"` → `!isInteractiveTty` → `CI=true || OPENCLAW_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 || OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` → `OPENCLAW_AUTH_STORE_READONLY=1` → `--help/--version` → primary is `doctor`/`update`/`help`/`completion`/`version` → sidecar dir empty → fresh decline marker.

## Headless one-shot warning

Added in `legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`. Fires when `loadLegacyOAuthSidecarMaterial` returns null on darwin with `allowKeychainPrompt: false` (i.e., env/file seeds were tried and Keychain was blocked). Rate-limited to one warn per process so embedded turns log it once per gateway lifetime, not per attempt. Guarded by the same VITEST env signals as the keychain read so unrelated test runs don't surface it.

## Regression tests

- `src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — table-driven preflight skip checks for each rule, plus end-to-end migrate / decline / cool-off using `createOpenClawTestState`.
- `src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — exercises the new headless warn path: emit once when blocked on darwin with no env/file seeds; never emit on non-darwin.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor-auth-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/persisted-boundary.test.ts src/commands/agents.add.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` — 61/61 green.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` clean on all touched files; `oxfmt --check` clean.
- Wider `src/agents/auth-profiles` shard has the same 5 file / 26 test pre-existing flakes vs `origin/main` (`--isolate=false` cross-file mock leakage in unrelated OAuth tests). My new tests do not contribute to those failures; if anything, isolating mine reduces the count.

## Scope notes

- Did not touch `agents.add.test.ts` / `stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` test descriptions that still contain `sidecar-backed`; those are dev-facing and reference implementation, not UI copy.
- Did not externalize `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR` to docs anywhere except `docs/gateway/doctor.md` accordion 5 since it is an escape hatch, not a normal-user knob.
- No changes to `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` itself beyond the wording rename — the migration semantics are intentionally identical between the doctor-driven and CLI-startup-driven paths.

## Out of scope

- Removing the legacy sidecar code paths entirely. Tracked separately and should wait for the long tail to migrate.
- Changes to `openclaw doctor --fix` behavior itself; the migration path it runs is already correct.

## Related

- Closes #85083
- Companion to #85074 (file/env-seed bucket) and #85028 (fail-fast on missing refresh token)
- Original surface: #84893 / #84752 / #83312
