target.title: Doctor: normalize repair checks inside runner
target.body:
## Summary

This PR narrows the first doctor repair migration step to the core doctor runner.

- Adds an internal normalized health-check shape so the repair runner can execute either the existing split `detect()` / `repair()` checks or an internal single-flow `run(ctx)` check.
- Keeps the public plugin SDK health contract unchanged: plugin authors still see `detect()` plus optional `repair()`, and no plugin SDK baseline is regenerated.
- Preserves the old split-check repair sequencing so detected findings are still reported if `repair()` throws.
- Threads dry-run, diff, repair effects, warnings, config updates, and post-repair validation through the normalized runner path.
- Adds focused lint and repair-flow tests covering run-mode checks, split-check adaptation, dry-run/diff preview behavior, repair status mapping, repair failure reporting, and validation after successful repair.

The goal is to make the runner easier to migrate safely without asking every existing doctor check author to split one boolean repair flow immediately. Existing split checks still work through the adapter; internal checks can move to one flow when that is clearer.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Real environment tested: AWS Crabbox Linux VM (`cbx_cb0b70795a87`) from current PR head `d139d39e884019ce5de521660ba382a0499a1575`.

target.title: fix(agents): thread errorMessage through finalizeTransportStream
target.body:
## Summary

`finalizeTransportStream` throws when a stream stops with `stopReason === "error"` or `"aborted"`. The thrown error was hardcoded to `"An unknown error occurred"`, silently discarding `output.errorMessage` — which `failTransportStream` always populates before marking the stream as failed. Every provider transport failure surfaced the same opaque string regardless of the actual cause.

The fix reads `output.errorMessage` at throw-time with a null-coalesce fallback, preserving the `"An unknown error occurred"` string for any path that bypasses `failTransportStream`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `finalizeTransportStream` throws `"An unknown error occurred"` regardless of the actual error, discarding `output.errorMessage` set at `transport-stream-shared.ts:133`.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw clone on branch `fix/agents-transport-stream-error-message` (2026-05-20).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
npx vitest run src/agents/transport-stream-shared.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

Evidence after fix:
```
 ✓ |agents-core| transport stream shared helpers > throws with the original error message when stream failed 1ms
 ✓ |agents-core| transport stream shared helpers > throws with the abort error message when stream was aborted 0ms
 ✓ |agents-core| transport stream shared helpers > falls back to generic message when errorMessage is absent 0ms
 ✓ |agents-core| transport stream shared helpers > finalizes successful transport streams 2ms
 ✓ |agents-core| transport stream shared helpers > marks transport stream failures and runs cleanup 1ms
 ✓ |agents-support| transport stream shared helpers > throws with the original error message when stream failed 1ms
 ✓ |agents-support| transport stream shared helpers > throws with the abort error message when stream was aborted 0ms
 ✓ |agents-support| transport stream shared helpers > falls back to generic message when errorMessage is absent 0ms
 ✓ |agents-support| transport stream shared helpers > finalizes successful transport streams 2ms
 ✓ |agents-support| transport stream shared helpers > marks transport stream failures and runs cleanup 1ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  22 passed (22)
   Duration  2.00s (transform 1.34s, setup 853ms, import 518ms, tests 284ms, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: `finalizeTransportStream` throws `output.errorMessage` when set; falls back to `"An unknown error occurred"` when `output.errorMessage` is absent.

What was not tested: live provider transport failures requiring a running provider environment.

target.title: fix(agents): restore correct subagent model precedence in resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult
target.body:
## Summary

PR #58003 (commit e394262bd8) introduced a regression in `agent-scope.ts`: `agentConfig.model` (the agent's own model) was placed before `cfg.agents.defaults.subagents.model` (the global subagent default) in the `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` candidates array. This shadowed the global default for any agent that has its own primary model configured — the common case.

#81783 fixed the same regression in `model-selection.ts` but the `agent-scope.ts` path was missed. This path is used by the cron isolation runner (`src/cron/isolated-agent/model-selection.ts`) and `resolveSubagentModelFallbacksOverride`.

Correct precedence (matching `model-selection.ts:329–331`):

1. `agentConfig.subagents.model` — per-agent subagent config (highest priority)
2. `cfg.agents.defaults.subagents.model` — global subagent default
3. `agentConfig.model` — agent's own model (last resort only)

Closes #58822.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: agents with their own primary model but no per-agent `subagents.model` have subagents spawned with the agent's own model instead of the global subagent default.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw clone on branch `fix/agents-subagent-model-precedence-agent-scope` (2026-05-20).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
npx vitest run src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-fallback-policy.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

Evidence after fix:
```
 ✓ |agents-core| agent-scope > resolveAgentConfig > resolves the subagent model config selected for isolated runs 0ms
 ✓ |agents-core| agent-scope > resolveAgentConfig > resolves subagent model fallbacks from the selected subagent model source 0ms
 ✓ |agents-core| agent-scope > resolveAgentConfig > uses subagent model fallbacks for auto-selected spawned subagent models 0ms
 ✓ |agents-support| agent-scope > resolveAgentConfig > resolves the subagent model config selected for isolated runs 0ms
 ✓ |cron| model formatting and precedence edge cases > config model format variations > prefers agents.defaults.subagents.model over the agent's own model 0ms
 ✓ |cron| run-fallback-policy > resolveCronFallbacksOverride > uses global subagent model fallbacks when agent has no per-agent subagents.model 0ms
 ✓ |cron| run-fallback-policy > resolveCronFallbacksOverride > uses subagent model fallbacks when cron selects the configured subagent model 0ms

 Test Files  4 passed (4)
      Tests  127 passed (127)
   Duration  6.84s (transform 5.33s, setup 920ms, import 5.10s, tests 277ms, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` returns `default-subagent` source (not `agent` source) when an agent has its own model but no per-agent subagents override.

What was not tested: live multi-agent runs with mixed model configs.

target.title: fix(channels): honor /verbose in group sessions
target.body:
## Summary

This fixes normal group verbose tool/progress summary delivery by honoring explicit group `/verbose` state.

- Problem: normal group sessions suppressed text-only tool/progress summaries even when `/verbose on` was enabled for that group.
- Solution: make shared group dispatch respect explicit group `/verbose` session state.
- What changed: normal groups remain quiet by default, but `/verbose on` now allows tool/progress summaries; `/verbose off` hides them again.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no new config flag, no new permissions, no token handling changes, no command/tool execution behavior changes, and inherited verbose defaults do not opt normal groups into visible summaries.

## Motivation

Private/group QA needs to show what the agent actually did when verbose mode is explicitly enabled. `/verbose` already controls that visibility, but normal group dispatch ignored it.

Although the live proof uses Telegram because that is the configured QA environment, the implementation is intentionally in shared group/ACP dispatch code so group-capable channels use the same verbose gating policy.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
  `/verbose on` did not show tool/progress summaries in a normal Telegram group.

- Real environment tested:
  Private Telegram QA supergroup with a Driver bot prompting a SUT bot running OpenClaw from this PR branch. Tokens, chat IDs, and private config are omitted.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Built current PR worktree at `207742e506`.
  2. Started the isolated QA SUT gateway from that build.
  3. Sent `/verbose@qa_sut_bot off`.
  4. Prompted: `Use exactly one shell command: pwd. Then reply exactly: qa-current-verbose-off-done.`
  5. Sent `/verbose@qa_sut_bot on`.
  6. Prompted: `Use exactly one shell command: pwd. Then reply exactly: qa-current-verbose-on-done.`

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  Copied live output from the Telegram QA group after the fix:

  ```text
  /verbose@qa_sut_bot off
  QA SUT: ⚙️ Verbose logging disabled.

  @qa_sut_bot Use exactly one shell command: pwd. Then reply exactly: qa-current-verbose-off-done.
  QA SUT: qa-current-verbose-off-done

  /verbose@qa_sut_bot on
  QA SUT: ⚙️ Verbose logging enabled.

  @qa_sut_bot Use exactly one shell command: pwd. Then reply exactly: qa-current-verbose-on-done.
  QA SUT: 🛠️ pwd
  QA SUT: qa-current-verbose-on-done
  ```

- Observed result after fix:
  `/verbose off` hides the tool summary. `/verbose on` shows `🛠️ pwd` before the final reply.

- What was not tested:
  I did not run the full repository test suite on this low-memory VPS. I do not have live Slack/Matrix/WhatsApp bots configured for proof, so non-Telegram channels are covered by focused shared-dispatch tests rather than live channel proof.

- Before evidence:
  Copied live Telegram transcript from `origin/main` showed that `/verbose on` still produced only the final marker in a normal group:

  ```text
  /verbose@qa_sut_bot off
  QA SUT: ⚙️ Verbose logging disabled.

  @qa_sut_bot Use exactly one shell command: pwd. Then reply exactly: qa-main-verbose-off-done.
  QA SUT: qa-main-verbose-off-done

  /verbose@qa_sut_bot on
  QA SUT: ⚙️ Verbose logging enabled.

  @qa_sut_bot Use exactly one shell command: pwd. Then reply exactly: qa-main-verbose-on-done.
  QA SUT: qa-main-verbose-on-done
  ```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause:
  Shared group dispatch had a normal-group suppression path for text-only tool/progress summaries that did not account for explicit `/verbose` session state.

- Missing detection / guardrail:
  Existing tests covered default group suppression and topic/non-DM behavior, but not explicit `/verbose on` in normal groups.

- Contributing context:
  Telegram QA exposed the issue, but the fix is in shared group dispatch/projection logic.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient

- Target test or file:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.test.ts`
  - `src/plugin-sdk/acp-runtime.test.ts`

- Scenario the test should lock in:
  Groups suppress tool summaries by default, allow them only when explicit group `/verbose on` is active, respect live `/verbose off`, preserve hidden-tool text boundaries, and keep existing topic/non-DM behavior unchanged.

- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
  The bug is in shared dispatch/projection gates, so focused tests cover it without requiring live credentials for every channel.

- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
  Existing coverage was partial; new focused tests cover this case.

- If no new test is added, why not:
  N/A. New focused tests are included.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`/verbose on` now shows tool/progress summaries in normal group sessions. Groups remain quiet by default, with inherited verbose defaults, and with `/verbose off`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
group + /verbose on -> tool summary suppressed -> final reply only

After:
group + default/off -> final reply only
group + explicit /verbose on -> tool summary -> final reply
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No execution behavior changed; visible summary output can change only after explicit group `/verbose on`.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  Risk: visible tool/progress summaries can expose command names or arguments to group members. Mitigation: normal groups remain quiet by default; inherited verbose defaults do not opt normal groups into visible summaries; users must explicitly enable `/verbose on` in that group session, and `/verbose off` hides summaries again.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux VPS
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw gateway built from this PR branch
- Model/provider: existing QA SUT config
- Integration/channel: Telegram normal supergroup
- Relevant config (redacted): private QA group with Driver bot and SUT bot

### Steps

1. Start QA SUT from this PR branch.
2. Send `/verbose@qa_sut_bot off`.
3. Prompt SUT to run `pwd` and reply with a marker.
4. Confirm only the final marker appears.
5. Send `/verbose@qa_sut_bot on`.
6. Prompt SUT to run `pwd` and reply with a marker.
7. Confirm `🛠️ pwd` appears before the final marker.

### Expected

Group tool/progress summaries are hidden with default/off state and visible with explicit `/verbose on`.

### Actual

Matches expected in live Telegram QA proof.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets / copied live output
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- AI assistance disclosure: Codex helped implement, review, and test this PR; I reviewed the diff and verified the behavior in a live OpenClaw Telegram QA setup.

- Verified scenarios:
  - `origin/main`: `/verbose on` still produced final reply only in a normal Telegram group.
  - This PR at `207742e506`: `/verbose off` produced final reply only.
  - This PR at `207742e506`: `/verbose on` produced visible `🛠️ pwd`, then final reply.

- Local checks:
  - `git diff --check`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_SERIAL=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts -t "group tool summaries|group progress callbacks|live group tool-summary state|verbose progress"` -> `10 passed | 135 skipped`
  - `OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_SERIAL=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts` -> `145 passed`

- Codex review:
  - Local Codex review passed before the final explicit-group-verbose tightening.
  - After the final tightening, focused tests and live Telegram proof above were rerun on `207742e506`.

- Edge cases checked:
  - Groups stay quiet by default.
  - Inherited `agents.defaults.verboseDefault: "on"` does not opt normal groups into visible summaries.
  - Explicit group `/verbose on` enables summaries.
  - `/verbose off` hides them again.

- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full repository suite.
  - Live non-Telegram channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: group members can see tool/progress summaries when verbose mode is enabled.
  - Mitigation: normal groups remain quiet by default; inherited verbose defaults do not enable normal group summaries; visible summaries require explicit `/verbose on` in that group session, and `/verbose off` hides them again.

target.title: Harden node exec approval precheck env [AI]
target.body:
AI-assisted: Yes

## Summary

- Problem: node-host exec approval prechecks could analyze a command with the gateway environment even though the forwarded `system.run` request only carries node-targeted env overrides.
- Why it matters: bare command allowlist decisions should not depend on PATH resolution from a host that will not execute the command.
- What changed: node approval prechecks now build analysis input from the forwarded node env and clear PATH/Path fallback so gateway-local PATH cannot satisfy the precheck.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): the forwarded `system.run` payload, approval binding format, and node-side execution policy are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: provided separately by maintainers
- [x] This PR addresses a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: node-host approval prechecks no longer suppress approval based on gateway-local PATH resolution.
- Real environment tested: Not run in this metadata pass.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Not run in this metadata pass.
- Evidence after fix: Unit regression coverage was added in `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts`.
- Observed result after fix: Not runtime-verified in this metadata pass.
- What was not tested: real paired-node execution, broad changed checks, and cross-platform runs.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): N/A

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the node-host gateway precheck used the request env assembled on the gateway instead of the env overrides that are actually forwarded to the node.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression case proving that a gateway PATH match cannot suppress a node approval prompt when no PATH is forwarded.
- Contributing context (if known): node execution intentionally blocks PATH overrides and resolves bare commands with the node host process environment.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: gateway PATH must not satisfy node allowlist prechecks when forwarded node env is undefined, and forwarded node env overrides are used when present.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is isolated to `executeNodeHostCommand` approval analysis before `node.invoke system.run` is forwarded.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Node-host commands may ask for approval in cases that previously depended on gateway-local PATH analysis. No config migration is required.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
node exec request -> gateway env precheck -> node run with node env

After:
node exec request -> forwarded-env precheck with no gateway PATH fallback -> node run with node env
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: command approval precheck behavior is stricter for node-host bare command resolution; forwarded execution payloads and approval binding remain unchanged.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Not run in this metadata pass
- Runtime/container: Not run in this metadata pass
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): node-host exec
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.exec.host=node`, allowlist/on-miss style policy

### Steps

1. Configure a node-host exec path with node approval prechecks enabled.
2. Run a bare command whose gateway PATH would resolve to an allowlisted path while no PATH override is forwarded to the node.
3. Confirm approval is required instead of being skipped by gateway PATH analysis.

### Expected

- Approval is required unless the node-forwarded env can satisfy the precheck without gateway PATH fallback.

### Actual

- Not runtime-verified in this metadata pass.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

No external artifact attached in this metadata pass.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: reviewed changed-file summary and regression intent for node approval env handling.
- Edge cases checked: forwarded env present, forwarded env absent, gateway PATH not reused for precheck matching.
- What you did not verify: local test execution, real node-host execution, broad CI gates.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Config/env changes? No

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Mitigation: forwarded node env overrides are still honored for precheck analysis, and explicit approval flow remains available.

target.title: fix(update): allow package-manager hardlinks in swaps
target.body:
## Summary

- allow the package-manager update/install swap path to move package roots that contain source-side hardlinks
- keep the generic `movePathWithCopyFallback` reject guard unchanged for non-package-manager callers
- add regression coverage with real filesystem hardlinks for both global update swaps and install-directory swaps

Fixes #85559.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: npm 11+/pnpm-style global OpenClaw installs can contain hardlinked files under the installed package root. Before this change, the updater/install swap path used `sourceHardlinks: "reject"` and could fail before replacing the package root. This patch scopes `sourceHardlinks: "allow"` to the package-manager-owned swap paths while preserving the generic reject guard.

Real environment tested: Docker `node:24-bookworm`, Node v24.16.0, npm 11.13.0, Linux container, real global npm install under `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw`. The proof used local OpenClaw tarballs built from this PR head: baseline `2026.5.22` and target `2026.5.23-proof.0`.

Exact steps or command run after fix:

```sh
docker run --rm -v /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753:/proof node:24-bookworm bash -lc '
set -eu
npm install -g --omit=optional /proof/baseline.tgz
root="$(npm root -g)/openclaw"
hardlink_source="$(find "$root" -type f -path "*/dist/*" -print -quit)"
mkdir -p /tmp/openclaw-hardlink-cache
ln "$hardlink_source" /tmp/openclaw-hardlink-cache/source-hardlink
find "$root" -type f -links +1 | head -n 3
OPENCLAW_ALLOW_ROOT=1 npm_config_omit=optional NPM_CONFIG_OMIT=optional openclaw update --tag /proof/target.tgz --yes --json
openclaw --version
'
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
node=v24.16.0
npm=11.13.0
baseline=OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (aacec68)
packageRoot=/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw
hardlinkSource=/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/setup-surface-BUJl2T9I.js
hardlinkedBefore=
/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/setup-surface-BUJl2T9I.js
{
  "status": "ok",
  "mode": "npm",
  "root": "/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw",
  "before": {
    "version": "2026.5.22"
  },
  "after": {
    "version": "2026.5.23-proof.0"
  },
  "steps": [
    {
      "name": "global update",
      "exitCode": 0,
      "stdoutTail": "\nadded 483 packages in 5m"
    },
    {
      "name": "global install swap",
      "exitCode": 0,
      "stdoutTail": "replaced openclaw"
    },
    {
      "name": "openclaw doctor",
      "exitCode": 0
    }
  ]
}
updated=OpenClaw 2026.5.23-proof.0 (aacec68)
```

Observed result after fix: The package root contained a real hardlinked file before the update, and `openclaw update --tag /proof/target.tgz --yes --json` completed with `status: ok`. The `global install swap` step replaced the package root successfully and the installed CLI reported `OpenClaw 2026.5.23-proof.0` afterward.

What was not tested: I did not run a separate pnpm global update E2E locally. The Docker proof covers npm 11 global install/update with a real hardlinked package root in the same package-manager swap path, and the regression tests cover both update and install swap helpers with actual filesystem hardlinks.

## Verification

```sh
git diff --check
pnpm test src/infra/replace-file.test.ts src/infra/package-update-steps.test.ts src/infra/install-package-dir.test.ts
node scripts/package-openclaw-for-docker.mjs --output-dir /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753 --output-name baseline.tgz
docker run --rm -v /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753:/proof node:24-bookworm bash -lc '...'
```

## Security notes

- This does not weaken the generic hardlink protection in `movePathWithCopyFallback`.
- The allow policy is scoped to package-manager-owned package root swaps where npm/pnpm may legitimately materialize hardlinks.

target.title: Control-plane hardening from Optimus ops findings
target.body:
## Summary

Completes the OpenClaw control-plane hardening project stack from Optimus ops findings:

- ADP-237: structured A2A handoff IDs and return-route tracing
- ADP-238: native incident ledger and repair audit trail
- ADP-239: regression baseline capture and postflight compare
- ADP-240: staggered channel startup with isolated probe cache
- ADP-241: plugin contract validator and startup diagnostics surface
- ADP-242: expected custom service wrapper config/reporting
- ADP-243: unified `openclaw diagnose --json` control-plane API

Maintainer-review updates in latest head `cf1ef47404`:

- Baseline capture now uses supported gateway RPCs: `status` and `tasks.list`.
- Diagnose JSON now redacts gateway config, omits gateway auth metadata, and emits incident summaries without raw details.
- Probe cache rejected-result handling now preserves the failed item index/id.
- Diagnose safe action now points to the real command: `openclaw plugins contracts validate --strict --json`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Validate the new control-plane hardening surfaces from a real local OpenClaw checkout: plugin contract validation, unified diagnose JSON, A2A provenance handoff fields, baseline/probe-cache inventory, incident ledger, service wrapper diagnostics, and the maintainer-review fixes for supported RPCs/redacted diagnose/probe-cache failure association.

Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw development checkout on this PR branch, using the configured local OpenClaw state and installed bundled/user plugins. Commands were run from the repository root with the local gateway/config environment after rebuilding the branch.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Ran `CI=true pnpm tsgo:core`.
2. Ran `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/probes/cache.test.ts src/incidents/ledger.test.ts src/sessions/input-provenance.test.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts src/plugins/contract-validator.test.ts`.
3. Ran `pnpm build`.
4. Ran `node dist/entry.js plugins contracts validate --strict --json`.
5. Ran `timeout 30s node dist/entry.js diagnose --json --timeout 1000`.
6. Ran the gateway `sessions_send` shard covering inter-session dispatch with the new provenance fields.
7. Ran `git diff --check`.

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/probes/cache.test.ts src/incidents/ledger.test.ts src/sessions/input-provenance.test.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts src/plugins/contract-validator.test.ts
[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 5.86s
```

```text
$ pnpm build
OK: All 4 required plugin-sdk exports verified.
```

```text
$ node dist/entry.js plugins contracts validate --strict --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "pluginCount": 125,
  "findingCount": 30
}
```

```text
$ timeout 30s node dist/entry.js diagnose --json --timeout 1000
{
  "ok": true,
  "configuredChannels": 2,
  "openIncidents": 0,
  "taskErrors": 0,
  "baselines": 0,
  "probeCache": 0,
  "hasGatewayAuth": false,
  "safe": [
    "openclaw health --json",
    "openclaw gateway status --json",
    "openclaw tasks audit --json",
    "openclaw plugins contracts validate --strict --json"
  ],
  "recentIncidentKeys": []
}
```

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server.sessions-send.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       3 passed (3)
```

Observed result after fix: The local OpenClaw setup accepted the new gateway input provenance fields, plugin contract validation completed with `ok:true`, and diagnose emitted the unified control-plane JSON with plugin contracts, task audit, baselines, probe cache, incident counts, and action hints. Diagnose omitted gateway auth metadata (`hasGatewayAuth:false`), emitted no raw incident detail fields in the sampled output, returned `ok:true`, and reported zero open incidents and zero task errors.

What was not tested: I did not run destructive doctor repairs, service restarts, live plugin updates, or production channel startup. Those are intentionally listed as unsafe actions by `openclaw diagnose` and are outside this PR's non-destructive validation path.

## Validation

- `CI=true pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `CI=true pnpm tsgo:core`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/probes/cache.test.ts src/incidents/ledger.test.ts src/sessions/input-provenance.test.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts src/plugins/contract-validator.test.ts`

Earlier branch validation before the maintainer-review fix also passed:

## Notes

The upstream repository is creator/maintainer-owned. This code is submitted from the accessible `fchaudhryspear/openclaw` fork for maintainer review rather than attempting direct writes to `openclaw/openclaw`.

target.title: Redact exec tool result payloads
target.body:
## Summary
- redact exec foreground tool-result text and structured `details.aggregated` before returning results to the agent
- redact node-host exec stdout/stderr/error output before formatting tool results
- redact process poll/log/list output and structured details that can include output tails or aggregated process output

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Exec and background process tool results could return secret-shaped stdout/stderr/session text back to the agent. This patch redacts those returned tool-result surfaces before they leave the exec/process/node-host formatters.

**Real environment tested:** Real local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/exec-tool-result-redaction` at `da701a0bbf6c`, using an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`. The proof used an obvious fake canary value, not a real credential.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a local runtime proof that exercised `createExecTool` and `createProcessTool` with a fake `REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET` value, then inspected the returned tool-result `content[0].text` and structured `details.aggregated` values for foreground exec, process poll, process log, and process list tail/details surfaces.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the local runtime proof:

```json
{
  "foregroundExec": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "detailsAggregated": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processPoll": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890\n\n\nProcess exited with code 0.",
    "detailsAggregated": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890\n",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processLog": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "detailsAggregated": "",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processList": {
    "status": "completed",
    "textTail": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  }
}
```

Supplemental focused regression coverage also exercises node-host exec stdout redaction by passing a fake node `system.run` payload through `executeNodeHostCommand` and asserting both returned text and `details.aggregated` redact the raw canary.

**Observed result after fix:** The raw fake canary value was absent from returned foreground exec, process poll, process log, and process list output. Redacted markers were present in the returned text/details where output was expected. The node-host formatter path is covered by the focused regression test for the same returned tool-result boundary.

**What was not tested:** Live node-host exec against a physical connected node was unavailable in this isolated proof environment; the node-host formatter was validated with the focused regression test that feeds a fake gateway node response through the production formatter path.

## Tests
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node-phases.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped stdout'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped output'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node stdout'`

Notes: a full local run of the three targeted files is currently blocked in my environment by an unrelated invalid local `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` model alias (`openai-codex/gpt-5.3-codex`) during the pre-existing foreground timeout test. The new redaction regression tests pass individually under the agents-core Vitest config.

Fixes #71211



## Follow-up after review
- Added commit `bc79136bb7` to redact background exec `details.tail` before returning running-session tool results.
- Added regression coverage for background exec `details.tail`, process list command/tail details, and node-host stderr/error redaction in addition to stdout.

Additional local verification after follow-up:
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node'`
- `git diff --check`

Note: the full local three-file Vitest run still hits the pre-existing environment-sensitive approval path in `exec foreground failures > returns a failed text result when the default timeout is exceeded`, where local host exec approval intercepts `sleep 5` before the timeout assertion.

## Review feedback update - 2026-05-21
- Addressed ClawSweeper P1 by removing the JSON embedded-fallback behavior change from this PR; `openclaw agent --json` fallback behavior is back to the existing contract.
- Addressed ClawSweeper P2 by replacing the live-update process timing test with deterministic `runExecProcess` regression coverage, so the previous single-line skip branches are gone.
- Final head verified: `8793847f206cca93c49abe33e4f49b9b55a0c4dc`.

Additional verification on final head:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped stdout in update text and details tail'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts -t 'JSON'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node'`\n- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts src/cli/program/register.agent.ts src/cli/program/register.agent.test.ts`\n- `git diff --check && git diff --cached --check`\n\nChanged-file gate note: `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"` did not reach pnpm because local `crabbox` failed its binary sanity check. Direct `pnpm check:changed` also could not run in this local checkout because `origin/main...HEAD` has no merge base on the grafted/shallow branch. The focused tests, lint, and diff checks above passed on the final head.

## Process control redaction update - 2026-05-21
- Addressed ClawSweeper P1 by redacting command-derived process result names for running-session controls (`write`, `submit`, `paste`) plus `kill`, `remove`, `poll`, `log`, `list`, and `send-keys`.
- Added fake-secret regression coverage for `process write` and `process send-keys` command-derived `details.name` values.
- Final head verified locally: `24ac21f4280e9e403722c271d12fda0a4c68535d4`.

Additional verification on final head:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.process-send-keys.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped stdout in update text and details tail'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node'`\n- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process-send-keys.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process-send-keys.test.ts`\n- `git diff --check && git diff --cached --check`

target.title: Fix Telegram missing harness spool poison
target.body:
## Summary

- Dead-letter Telegram isolated-ingress spooled updates that fail with a missing agent harness instead of requeueing them indefinitely.
- Traverse wrapped error graphs, including grammY `BotError.error`, so the missing-harness classifier sees the underlying failure.
- Add focused Telegram polling-session regressions for direct, cause-wrapped, and BotError-wrapped missing-harness failures plus same-lane drain behavior.

Fixes #85470.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts` - 47 tests passed.
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` - clean; no accepted/actionable findings.
- Live AWS Crabbox main-branch breakage proof: `run_6f8d492cc512` / `cbx_05f08539b61b` showed full-gateway Telegram `/compact@bot` poison left poison and follower pending with no failed tombstone.
- Live AWS Crabbox post-fix proof after BotError-wrapper change: `run_58cd3d9a1d5a` / `cbx_5bf0ecd06f92`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: A non-retryable missing agent harness failure in Telegram isolated ingress could poison the spool lane forever, blocking later same-lane updates.

Real environment tested: AWS Crabbox `cbx_5bf0ecd06f92` using Convex `telegram` credentials and a real Telegram group/bot flow.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `/private/tmp/openclaw-85470-full-gateway-compact-fix-proof.sh` through `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --market on-demand --full-resync --env-from-profile /private/tmp/convex_pool.env --env-from-profile /private/tmp/iao.env --script /private/tmp/openclaw-85470-full-gateway-compact-fix-proof.sh`.

Evidence after fix: `OPENCLAW_85470_FULL_GATEWAY_COMPACT_FIX_PROOF` reported Telegram `/compact@OpenClawQA1_Bot2_bot` message `11964`, tombstone `0000000785085476.json.failed`, reason `missing-agent-harness`, follower sent `11965`, follower reply `11966`, final spool `pending=[]`, `processing=[]`, `failedReasons=["missing-agent-harness"]`.

Observed result after fix: The poisoned `/compact` update was dead-lettered and the follower update drained and received a real Telegram reply.

What was not tested: Full broad CI had not completed at PR creation time; it will be monitored after opening the PR. The proof hook used only in the Crabbox run forced the missing-harness failure shape through the full-gateway `/compact` path and was removed from the committed branch.

target.title: fix(feishu): add unsend action handler using im.message.delete (recall API)
target.body:
## Summary

The Feishu plugin registered message actions (send, read, edit, etc.) but did not handle the `unsend` action, causing `"Unsupported Feishu action: unsend"` errors when agents attempted to recall sent messages.

The Feishu/Lark SDK maps `client.im.message.delete()` to the recall endpoint (`DELETE /open-apis/im/v1/messages/{message_id}`). Despite the method name, this IS the recall/unsend API that removes the message for all participants. There is no separate `recall()` method on the raw SDK client.

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`: Add `"unsend"` to advertised actions set; add unsend handler using `client.im.message.delete()` (the Feishu recall API)
- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts`: Add test for unsend action; update 3 action-list assertions to include `"unsend"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Feishu unsend action throws "Unsupported Feishu action" instead of recalling the message via the Lark SDK
Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node 22.19, local vitest via node scripts/run-vitest.mjs
Exact steps or command run after this patch: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
 ✓ extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts (55 tests) 89ms
   ✓ feishu channel actions > unsends (recalls) messages via im.message.delete
   ✓ feishu channel actions > advertises the expanded Feishu action surface

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  55 passed (55)
```

```console
$ node -e "const L=require('@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk');const c=new L.Client({appId:'x',appSecret:'x'});console.log('delete:',typeof c.im.message.delete,'recall:',typeof c.im.message.recall)"
delete: function recall: undefined
```

Observed result after fix: unsend handler calls client.im.message.delete({path:{message_id}}) and returns {ok:true, action:"unsend", messageId}. SDK verification confirms client.im.message.delete exists (function) while client.im.message.recall is undefined; the SDK uses "delete" as the method name for the recall/unsend API endpoint.
What was not tested: Live Feishu bot message recall (requires Feishu app credentials with im:message:recall permission and a real Feishu workspace)

Closes #85587

target.title: fix(embedded-runner): keep active compaction retries from looking stuck
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: long-running embedded runs could be misclassified as stuck while compaction retry work was still actively in flight.
- Why it matters: stuck-session recovery could treat active work as invalid or stalled work, which risks incorrect cleanup behavior for long-running tasks.
- What changed: diagnostic session progress is refreshed before long embedded prompt work and while compaction retries remain active, and regression tests were added to lock in the retry heartbeat path and observe-only stuck recovery behavior.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change stuck-session recovery policy, abort thresholds, cleanup strategy outside this path, or any integration/channel-specific behavior.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Active compaction retry waits should keep refreshing diagnostic session activity so long-running embedded work is preserved instead of being misclassified as stuck when active abort recovery remains disabled.
Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw setup on device 25 (Ubuntu), source-built checkout at /home/ghost/src/openclaw-ghost, branch fix/stuck-task-heartbeat-upstream.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: On device 25, in /home/ghost/src/openclaw-ghost, run pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/compaction-retry-aggregate-timeout.test.ts src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.test.ts, then inspect the live terminal output from that run and confirm both regression tests pass after the fix.
Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured from the real device 25 run after the fix.
```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.logging.config.ts
PASS logging src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.test.ts (11 tests)
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts
PASS agents src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/compaction-retry-aggregate-timeout.test.ts (7 tests)
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 24.86s
```
Observed result after fix: The real device 25 run completed successfully; the logging regression test passed with 11 tests, the aggregate-timeout regression test passed with 7 tests, and the Vitest shard run finished in 24.86 seconds.
What was not tested: I did not run a full end-to-end synthetic harness covering every stuck-session recovery mode.

target.title: feat(agents): add resumable field to sessions_list tool output
target.body:
## Why

Fixes #64103. Orchestrator agents see `status: "failed"`, `"timeout"`, or `"done"` on a session row and conclude the session is permanently dead, spawning a duplicate instead of calling `sessions_send` to resume. Status reflects the last turn outcome, not session lifecycle — `"killed"` is the only terminal state.

`reactivateCompletedSubagentSession` confirms this: it checks `endedAt`, not `status`. `sessions_send` uses `interruptSessionRunIfActive` for running sessions, so even `"running"` is sendable.

## What changed

- `sessions-list-tool.ts` — adds `readSessionResumable()` returning `boolean | undefined`; exposes a `resumable` field on each session row (`true` for all statuses except `"killed"`; `undefined` for unrecognized status)
- `tool-description-presets.ts` — updates `describeSessionsListTool()` so agents see the contract directly: resumable is true for all statuses except killed
- `sessions-list-tool.test.ts` — 6 cases: `running`/`done`/`failed`/`timeout` → `true`, `killed` → `false`, unrecognized → `undefined`

## Real behavior proof

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/sessions-list-tool.test.ts
```

```
 ✓ sessions-list-tool > exposes resumable field reflecting whether sessions_send can reactivate the session
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
```

Both `agents-core` and `agents-tools` workspaces pass.

target.title: fix(agents): preserve claude-cli last-call usage
target.body:
## Summary
- keep Claude CLI stream-json `result` usage as aggregate run usage
- track latest assistant-message usage separately as `lastCallUsage`
- use `lastCallUsage` for CLI session context snapshots so summed `result.cache_read_input_tokens` cannot inflate fresh token accounting

Fixes #85573

## Real behavior proof

Before this patch, a Claude stream-json turn with two assistant usage records and a final result usage sum exposed only the final aggregate result usage to the CLI runner. That made `agentMeta.lastCallUsage` equal the summed `result.cache_read_input_tokens` value:

```text
assistant[0].cache_read_input_tokens = 18499
assistant[1].cache_read_input_tokens = 27142
result.cache_read_input_tokens = 45641
old lastCallUsage.cacheRead = 45641
```

After this patch, the parser keeps aggregate `usage` unchanged for run accounting but also exposes the latest assistant-call usage as `lastCallUsage`, and the CLI runner prefers that for session context snapshots:

```text
usage.cacheRead = 45641
lastCallUsage.cacheRead = 27142
```

This is covered by the new focused regression in `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`: `uses latest Claude assistant usage as lastCallUsage when result usage is aggregate`. The regression constructs the summed Claude stream-json fixture directly and asserts both values separately.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` -> PASS, 16 tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts` -> PASS, 54 tests
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=16384 npx tsc --noEmit` -> PASS

## Notes
- First `tsc --noEmit` attempt with 8 GB heap OOMed before diagnostics; reran with 16 GB heap and passed.
- `prettier --check` flags pre-existing wrapping in `src/agents/cli-runner.ts` if run on the whole file; I kept the cli-runner diff minimal instead of reformatting unrelated lines.

target.title: fix(signal): forward quote metadata to agent context
target.body:
## Summary

Signal quoted replies (using the native reply/quote feature) arrive with `dataMessage.quote` containing `id`, `author`, and `text` from signal-cli, but only `quote.text` was being extracted — `id` and `author` were silently dropped, and none of the `ReplyTo*` context fields were populated in `finalizeInboundContext()`.

This means agents receiving Signal messages cannot tell what message a user was replying to, even though the data is available. The reply text itself arrives fine, but the quote context (which message was being replied to, who wrote it) is lost.

Telegram already handles this correctly via `describeReplyTarget()` which populates `ReplyToId`, `ReplyToBody`, `ReplyToSender`, and `ReplyToIsQuote`. This PR mirrors that behavior for Signal.

## Changes

- **`event-handler.types.ts`**: Expand `SignalDataMessage.quote` type to include `id`, `author`, `authorNumber` fields (matching signal-cli JSON-RPC protocol)
- **`event-handler.ts`**: 
  - Add `signalQuote` field to `SignalInboundEntry` type
  - Extract quote metadata from `dataMessage.quote` before debouncer enqueue
  - Set `ReplyToId`, `ReplyToBody`, `ReplyToSender`, `ReplyToIsQuote` in inbound context payload

## Before / After

**Before:** Agent receives `Body: "yes that sounds good"` — no indication this was a reply to a specific message.

**After:** Agent receives:
- `Body: "yes that sounds good"` 
- `ReplyToId: "1774743992672"` (timestamp of quoted message)
- `ReplyToBody: "Should we deploy the fix tonight?"` (original message text)
- `ReplyToSender: "+1234567890"` (who wrote the original)
- `ReplyToIsQuote: true`

## Test plan

- [ ] Send a normal (non-reply) Signal message → verify no ReplyTo fields set
- [ ] Send a quoted reply → verify ReplyToId, ReplyToBody, ReplyToSender, ReplyToIsQuote populated
- [ ] Send a reply with no new text (quote-only) → verify bodyText falls back to quote text, ReplyTo fields still set
- [ ] Send a message in a group with a quote → verify same behavior in group context
- [ ] Verify Telegram reply behavior unchanged

target.title: fix(update): allow package-manager hardlinks in swaps
target.body:
## Summary

- allow the package-manager update/install swap path to move package roots that contain source-side hardlinks
- keep the generic `movePathWithCopyFallback` reject guard unchanged for non-package-manager callers
- add regression coverage with real filesystem hardlinks for both global update swaps and install-directory swaps

Fixes #85559.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: npm 11+/pnpm-style global OpenClaw installs can contain hardlinked files under the installed package root. Before this change, the updater/install swap path used `sourceHardlinks: "reject"` and could fail before replacing the package root. This patch scopes `sourceHardlinks: "allow"` to the package-manager-owned swap paths while preserving the generic reject guard.

Real environment tested: Docker `node:24-bookworm`, Node v24.16.0, npm 11.13.0, Linux container, real global npm install under `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw`. The proof used local OpenClaw tarballs built from this PR head: baseline `2026.5.22` and target `2026.5.23-proof.0`.

Exact steps or command run after fix:

```sh
docker run --rm -v /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753:/proof node:24-bookworm bash -lc '
set -eu
npm install -g --omit=optional /proof/baseline.tgz
root="$(npm root -g)/openclaw"
hardlink_source="$(find "$root" -type f -path "*/dist/*" -print -quit)"
mkdir -p /tmp/openclaw-hardlink-cache
ln "$hardlink_source" /tmp/openclaw-hardlink-cache/source-hardlink
find "$root" -type f -links +1 | head -n 3
OPENCLAW_ALLOW_ROOT=1 npm_config_omit=optional NPM_CONFIG_OMIT=optional openclaw update --tag /proof/target.tgz --yes --json
openclaw --version
'
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
node=v24.16.0
npm=11.13.0
baseline=OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (aacec68)
packageRoot=/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw
hardlinkSource=/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/setup-surface-BUJl2T9I.js
hardlinkedBefore=
/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/setup-surface-BUJl2T9I.js
{
  "status": "ok",
  "mode": "npm",
  "root": "/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw",
  "before": {
    "version": "2026.5.22"
  },
  "after": {
    "version": "2026.5.23-proof.0"
  },
  "steps": [
    {
      "name": "global update",
      "exitCode": 0,
      "stdoutTail": "\nadded 483 packages in 5m"
    },
    {
      "name": "global install swap",
      "exitCode": 0,
      "stdoutTail": "replaced openclaw"
    },
    {
      "name": "openclaw doctor",
      "exitCode": 0
    }
  ]
}
updated=OpenClaw 2026.5.23-proof.0 (aacec68)
```

Observed result after fix: The package root contained a real hardlinked file before the update, and `openclaw update --tag /proof/target.tgz --yes --json` completed with `status: ok`. The `global install swap` step replaced the package root successfully and the installed CLI reported `OpenClaw 2026.5.23-proof.0` afterward.

What was not tested: I did not run a separate pnpm global update E2E locally. The Docker proof covers npm 11 global install/update with a real hardlinked package root in the same package-manager swap path, and the regression tests cover both update and install swap helpers with actual filesystem hardlinks.

## Verification

```sh
git diff --check
pnpm test src/infra/replace-file.test.ts src/infra/package-update-steps.test.ts src/infra/install-package-dir.test.ts
node scripts/package-openclaw-for-docker.mjs --output-dir /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753 --output-name baseline.tgz
docker run --rm -v /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753:/proof node:24-bookworm bash -lc '...'
```

## Security notes

- This does not weaken the generic hardlink protection in `movePathWithCopyFallback`.
- The allow policy is scoped to package-manager-owned package root swaps where npm/pnpm may legitimately materialize hardlinks.

target.title: fix(pdf): use MiniMax text models for PDF fallback
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: MiniMax-only PDF auto-resolution could select `MiniMax-VL-01`, which is a media-understanding model and not a normal chat model for the PDF tool.
- Solution: Prefer authenticated MiniMax text chat models for PDF extraction fallback and filter MiniMax VLM refs out of PDF fallback candidates.
- What changed: PDF model config resolution now builds MiniMax text extraction candidates from the default model and configured provider catalogs.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Native PDF providers, image tool routing, MiniMax VLM image understanding, provider auth, and runtime secret handling are unchanged.

## Motivation

- Fixes the MiniMax-only PDF path reported in #85575, where the tool can fail before reaching text extraction fallback.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #85575
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: MiniMax-only PDF auto-resolution chose `MiniMax-VL-01`, a media/VLM model, before PDF text extraction fallback could use `MiniMax-M2.7`.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, Node 24.8.0, using a real `MINIMAX_API_KEY` sourced from `.env` and redacted from all output.
- Before evidence: Before patch, `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.model-config.test.ts -- -t "does not add configured MiniMax chat models as automatic PDF image fallbacks" --reporter=verbose` passed with the old expectation that MiniMax PDF auto-resolution selected `minimax/MiniMax-VL-01` instead of the configured MiniMax chat model.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: sourced `.env` without echoing it, then invoked `createPdfTool` from the patched local source with `agents.defaults.model.primary = "minimax/MiniMax-M2.7"` against `https://www.rd.usda.gov/sites/default/files/pdf-sample_0.pdf`; also ran `git diff --check && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.model-config.test.ts src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts && pnpm check:changed`.
- Evidence after fix (redacted runtime output):

```json
{
  "started": "2026-05-23T03:05:12.994Z",
  "finished": "2026-05-23T03:06:11.460Z",
  "providerCredentialSource": "MINIMAX_API_KEY present (redacted)",
  "samplePdf": "https://www.rd.usda.gov/sites/default/files/pdf-sample_0.pdf",
  "tool": "pdf",
  "model": "minimax/MiniMax-M2.7",
  "native": false,
  "attempts": [],
  "textPreview": "The PDF contains just the placeholder text \"Dummy PDF file.\""
}
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot): captured locally as `openclaw-minimax-pdf-proof.png` for manual attachment in a PR comment.
- Additional verification: Focused PDF tool regression tests passed: 4 files, 56 tests. `pnpm check:changed` passed with lanes `core, coreTests` including typecheck, lint, import-cycle, runtime sidecar, and guard checks.
- Observed result after fix: The real PDF tool call used `minimax/MiniMax-M2.7`, ran in extraction fallback mode (`native: false`), made no fallback attempts, and correctly identified that the USDA sample PDF contains the placeholder text `Dummy PDF file.`

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: PDF auto-resolution reused image media provider candidates for MiniMax, so it could choose `MiniMax-VL-01` even though the PDF runtime resolves candidates through the chat model registry before extraction fallback.
- Missing detection / guardrail: The prior regression test locked in the MiniMax VLM selection instead of asserting that PDF fallback should use MiniMax text chat models.
- Contributing context (if known): MiniMax chat and media-understanding models are intentionally separate; PDF fallback needs the chat model path when using extracted text.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.model-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: MiniMax configured/default text chat models are selected for PDF extraction fallback, including the `minimax-portal/MiniMax-M2.7` default-model path from the issue.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug is in PDF model config selection before provider calls or PDF parsing, so unit coverage catches the broken decision without requiring live MiniMax credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Adjacent `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts` covers the non-native extraction fallback runtime path.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users with MiniMax configured can use the PDF tool through extracted text fallback instead of failing early on a MiniMax VLM model lookup.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
MiniMax PDF auto-select -> MiniMax-VL-01 -> chat model registry lookup -> Unknown model

After:
MiniMax PDF auto-select -> MiniMax-M2.7 -> PDF text extraction fallback -> chat completion
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- Security/runtime controls unchanged: provider auth checks, model registry lookup, PDF file loading limits, SSRF policy, and runtime API-key resolution all stay on the existing paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 24.8.0, local source checkout
- Model/provider: Real MiniMax API-key auth with `minimax/MiniMax-M2.7`
- Integration/channel: None
- Relevant config (redacted): `agents.defaults.model.primary = "minimax/MiniMax-M2.7"`; `models.providers.minimax.models = [{ id: "MiniMax-M2.7", input: ["text"] }]`

### Steps

1. Source `.env` without printing secret values.
2. Create the patched local `pdf` tool with MiniMax M2.7 as the default model.
3. Execute the tool against `https://www.rd.usda.gov/sites/default/files/pdf-sample_0.pdf`.
4. Confirm the selected model and PDF result from the redacted runtime output.

### Expected

- PDF fallback uses `MiniMax-M2.7` for extracted PDF text.

### Actual

- The real PDF tool run returned `model: minimax/MiniMax-M2.7`, `native: false`, `attempts: []`, and identified the sample PDF as containing `Dummy PDF file.`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: real MiniMax API-key PDF tool run against the USDA sample PDF, configured MiniMax API-key text model, MiniMax portal default model, existing non-native PDF extraction fallback tests, and changed core type/lint/guard checks.
- Edge cases checked: MiniMax VLM refs are filtered out of PDF fallback candidates so a fallback does not retry the old media-only model path.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: MiniMax PDFs that contain only scanned images still need a tested OCR/VLM PDF path.
  - Mitigation: This PR keeps scope to text extraction fallback and leaves MiniMax image/VLM behavior unchanged for the image tool.

## Out-of-scope follow-ups

- Add a dedicated MiniMax VLM PDF/OCR path only after the PDF runtime can call the media-understanding provider directly.
- Add a dedicated live MiniMax OAuth PDF lane if maintainers want recurring credentialed coverage.

target.title: fix(cron): capture originating session/agent on the cron wake tool call
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** The cron `wake` MCP tool forwards only `{mode, text}` to the gateway, so every wake enqueues against the heartbeat/main default. Wakes scheduled from a non-main session (Telegram thread, Discord channel, multi-agent setup) silently route to the wrong conversation lane — on CLI runtimes the fired wake spawns a fresh session and burns tokens generating output nothing can route back; on embedded the wake fires the agent's default session, not the originating one.
- **What changed.** Wake tool resolves `opts.agentSessionKey` via the existing `resolveInternalSessionKey` / `resolveSessionAgentId` pattern (mirrors the `add` action at L569-583) and threads `sessionKey` + `agentId` through `WakeParamsSchema` → gateway handler → `context.cron.wake` → `enqueueSystemEvent` + `requestHeartbeat`. Explicit params on the tool call take precedence over inferred values.
- **CLI runtime coverage (claude-cli + claude-cli-interactive).** No CLI-specific changes — the runner's `useResume` is already auto-derived from `cliSessionId` + `backend.resumeArgs` presence (`src/agents/cli-runner/execute.ts:276-277`). Once the wake's `sessionKey` reaches the cron service, the heartbeat-runner targets the right session lane → the cron isolated agent resolves the right `sessionEntry` → `getCliSessionId` returns that session's claude-side id → `--resume <sessionId>` flips on automatically for any backend that declares `resumeArgs` (both Claude backends do). Same path works for Codex, Gemini-CLI, etc.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** `WakeParamsSchema` still permits `additionalProperties: true`. Pre-existing call sites that omit origin still route to the dep's default (backwards compatible). Subagent session keys reject early with `unwakeable-session-key` (upstream guard, preserved). Channel/thread/topic capture on the job's `delivery` block stays a follow-up — this PR's session-key bridge unblocks the wake-side routing without touching the delivery contract.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** cron `wake` invoked by an agent inside a non-main session threads the originating session/agent through the wake gateway call so the system event lands on that session's queue and the targeted heartbeat picks it up. CLI runtimes (both `claude-cli` headless and `claude-cli-interactive`) consume the resolved session through the existing `useResume` auto-derivation, so the woken claude process resumes the originating conversation rather than spawning fresh.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, AJV-compiled TypeBox validators, vitest 4.1.6. Live harness in `scripts/proof-cron-wake-origin.mts` drives the patched gateway wake handler (`validateWakeParams` + `isSubagentSessionKey` guard) through the patched `cron.wake()` / `timer.ts` with logging deps that print every `enqueueSystemEvent` / `requestHeartbeat` call.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run src/cron/service/wake-origin.test.ts src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts && pnpm exec tsx scripts/proof-cron-wake-origin.mts`.
- **Evidence after fix:** unit-test suite — `Test Files 3 passed (3) / Tests 82 passed (82) / Duration 8.05s`. Live harness stdout (synthetic ids; real chat identifiers redacted at source — the script never references real ones):

  ```
  === non-main session + non-default agent (the bug-fix case) ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"follow up on report","sessionKey":"agent:coding:telegram:<chat-id-redacted>:topic:<topic-id-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="follow up on report"  opts={"sessionKey":"agent:coding:telegram:<chat-id-redacted>:topic:<topic-id-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake","sessionKey":"agent:coding:telegram:<chat-id-redacted>:topic:<topic-id-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}

  === no origin (backwards-compatible default routing) ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"ping"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="ping"  opts=undefined
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}

  === next-heartbeat + sessionKey collapses to targeted-immediate ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"next-heartbeat","text":"check the queue","sessionKey":"agent:coding:discord:<thread-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="check the queue"  opts={"sessionKey":"agent:coding:discord:<thread-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake","sessionKey":"agent:coding:discord:<thread-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}

  === subagent sessionKey rejected by gateway handler guard ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"wake my subagent","sessionKey":"subagent:scratch:<id-redacted>"}
  < wake result:  ok=false  payload=undefined
  < wake error:   {"code":"INVALID_REQUEST","message":"wake sessionKey cannot target a subagent session"}

  === whitespace-only origin falls through (defence-in-depth) ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"x","sessionKey":"   ","agentId":"\t"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="x"  opts=undefined
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** non-main wake calls hit `enqueueSystemEvent(text, {sessionKey, agentId})` and `requestHeartbeat({source: "manual", intent: "immediate", reason: "wake", sessionKey, agentId})` on the originating lane, not the heartbeat/main default. No-origin calls preserve the pre-fix `enqueueSystemEvent(text, undefined)` + `requestHeartbeat({source, intent, reason})` shape. `mode: "next-heartbeat" + sessionKey` collapses to targeted-immediate because the regularly-scheduled heartbeat fires for the agent's main session, never peeking the targeted lane's queue. Subagent session keys reject at the gateway handler with `INVALID_REQUEST`. Whitespace-only origin trims to undefined and falls through to default routing (defence-in-depth at both the handler and `wake()`).
- **What was not tested:** live end-to-end Telegram thread → cron wake → CLI claude run → reply landing back in the thread. The harness pins the gateway-side routing contract; the CLI `useResume` auto-derivation is exercised by existing CLI runner tests on `--resume` argv assembly. A full live-Telegram capture is a Mantis follow-up (it needs `delivery.{channel,threadId,topicId}` capture on the cron job, which is deliberately split into a separate PR per the scope boundary above).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Closes the upstream half of #46886 and #64556.
- Related (downstream slices): #70268, #57199, #82767, #79869, #63096. This PR fixes the upstream origin-capture they all silently assume.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** existing in-process callers of `wake({mode, text})` keep working only if origin remains optional.
  - **Mitigation:** `sessionKey` / `agentId` optional; omitted (or whitespace-only) falls through to the dep's default. Regression test pins both branches.
- **Risk:** new `NonEmptyString` slots on the schema reject empty-string origin from existing callers.
  - **Mitigation:** existing callers don't send these fields. `additionalProperties: true` unchanged so forward-compat metadata still passes.
- **Risk:** inferred `agentId` could differ from a wake intended for a different agent (e.g. operator scheduling a wake for `coding` from `main`).
  - **Mitigation:** explicit `sessionKey` / `agentId` params on the tool call override the inferred values. The implicit capture is the default for the "wake my own session" case.
- **Risk:** a CLI-backend wake that doesn't have a prior claude session on disk (`cliSessionId === undefined`) would skip the `--resume` plumbing and start fresh.
  - **Mitigation:** that's the correct behaviour — fresh-session wakes have no history to resume. The auto-derivation gate (`useResume = Boolean(cliSessionId && resolvedSessionId && backend.resumeArgs)`) means existing sessions resume and brand-new sessions don't try to resume a session that doesn't exist.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** No.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — session keys and agent ids are not credentials.
- **New/changed network calls:** No.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `wake` action; only the routing target changes.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — wake events still confined to in-process session queues; routing now goes to the correct queue.
