target.title: fix(exec): block configured denied path operands
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: issue #11829 calls out agent access to provider/API key material as a security roadmap gap, and the issue discussion proposed a configurable `tools.exec.deniedPaths` surface for tenant-controlled secret paths.
- Solution: add `tools.exec.deniedPaths` as an exec preflight guard that rejects commands referencing configured paths such as `/run/secrets/**` before gateway/node/sandbox execution. The guard now inspects command-carrier and shell-wrapper payloads, sandbox checks resolve relative operands in the container path namespace, node-host checks run after node target resolution using the target node platform namespace, config loading preserves authored denied-path `~` patterns until execution, home-relative denied patterns such as `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` resolve against the selected exec HOME, and `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands are expanded before denied-path matching.
- What changed: runtime guard, global/agent config wiring, zod/type/schema help/labels/tags, docs, generated config baseline, config path normalization, and regression tests for direct paths, shell-wrapper payloads, command substitutions, sandbox container-relative traversal, node-host Windows path semantics, home-relative denied patterns, and HOME-prefixed command operands.
- What did NOT change: no changes to model catalog filtering, auth proxy docs, or the existing models.json key-stripping work already covered by other PRs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #11829
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

- This gives operators a config-level guard for mounted secret directories, especially deployments that keep provider credentials in predictable paths such as `/run/secrets/**`. It is defense-in-depth rather than a replacement for sandboxing or secret isolation.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: exec commands can currently reference mounted sensitive paths unless deployment isolation catches the access; after this patch a configured denied path is rejected before host execution.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node 22.22.0, invoking the real `createExecTool` runtime with `host: "gateway"`, `security: "full"`, `ask: "off"`, and `deniedPaths: ["/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/**"]`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx` importing `./src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts`, creating the exec tool, and calling `tool.execute(...)` for these commands:
  - `cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"`
  - `bash -lc 'cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"'`
  - `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key")"'`
- Evidence after fix (copied live local runtime output):

```text
blocked: cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
blocked: bash -lc 'cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"'
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
blocked: bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key")"'
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
```

- Observed result after fix: the exec tool throws before running each command, including the shell-wrapper and command-substitution forms, so the denied path is not handed to gateway execution.
- Additional sandbox namespace proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` now covers `host: "sandbox"`, `workdir: "/workspace"`, `deniedPaths: ["/run/secrets/**"]`, and `cat ../../run/secrets/provider.key`. The preflight resolves the operand against `/workspace` using POSIX container semantics and rejects `/run/secrets/provider.key` before `buildExecSpec` is called.
- Additional node namespace proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts` now covers `host: "node"` after a Windows node target resolves with `platform: "win32"`, `workdir: "C:\\Work"`, `deniedPaths: ["C:\\Secrets\\**"]`, and `type C:\\Secrets\\provider.key`. The node path preflight rejects the command before `system.run` is called. It also covers case-insensitive Windows matching with `type c:\\secrets\\provider.key`.
- Additional home-relative proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` covers `deniedPaths: ["~/.openclaw/credentials/**"]` and blocks commands referencing the resolved HOME credential path via literal resolved paths, `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key`, and a shell-wrapper `${HOME}/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` payload. `src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts` verifies authored `tools.exec.deniedPaths` entries stay home-relative during generic config path normalization so the exec guard can resolve them later in the selected runtime namespace.
- Additional config-through-runtime namespace proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` flows `normalizeConfigPaths({ tools: { exec: { deniedPaths: ["~/.openclaw/credentials/**"] } } })` into sandbox exec with `HOME=/workspace` and proves `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` is blocked as `/workspace/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key`. `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts` flows the same normalized config into a Windows node target with `HOME=C:\\Users\\agent` and proves `C:\\Users\\agent\\.openclaw\\credentials\\provider.key` is blocked before `system.run`.
- What was not tested: a full multi-tenant Docker deployment with real mounted provider secrets.
- Before evidence: the new regression tests failed before the follow-up fixes because `bash -lc 'cat "<deniedFile>"'`, `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "<deniedFile>")"'`, the sandbox container-relative traversal, the Windows node target command, the home-relative denied pattern, and `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands all resolved successfully with `(no output)` or left path prefixes unexpanded instead of rejecting.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: exec had approval/safe-bin/env guards, but no config-level path denylist for sensitive operand paths. The first implementation scanned only the outer argv tokens, used host path semantics for sandbox relative operands, checked node targets before the target platform was known, let generic config normalization consume authored home-relative denied patterns before runtime namespace selection, did not normalize home-relative denied patterns, and did not expand common `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands before matching.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression test asserted that a configured secret path is blocked inside a shell wrapper, command substitution, sandbox container-relative path, Windows node target path, `~/...` denied pattern, or HOME-prefixed command operand before execution.
- Contributing context (if known): issue #11829 covers a broader roadmap; this PR implements only the `tools.exec.deniedPaths` part.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts`, `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts`
- Scenarios the tests lock in:
  - direct `cat "<deniedFile>"` is rejected;
  - `bash -lc 'cat "<deniedFile>"'` is rejected;
  - `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "<deniedFile>")"'` is rejected;
  - sandbox `cat ../../run/secrets/provider.key` from `/workspace` is rejected as `/run/secrets/provider.key` before `buildExecSpec`;
  - node-host `type C:\Secrets\provider.key` is rejected after resolving the target node as `platform: "win32"` and before `system.run`;
  - node-host Windows denied-path matching is case-insensitive for `C:\Secrets\**` versus `c:\secrets\provider.key`;
  - `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` is runtime-resolved and blocks the selected HOME credential path;
  - config-loaded `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` stays authored through generic path normalization and blocks sandbox `HOME=/workspace`;
  - config-loaded `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` stays authored through generic path normalization and blocks Windows node `HOME=C:\Users\agent`;
  - `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` and shell-wrapper `${HOME}/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` operands are expanded before matching and rejected.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises `createExecTool` directly and proves the preflight guard runs even when approvals are bypassed.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config key: `tools.exec.deniedPaths`. Commands that reference matching paths now fail before exec starts.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
exec command -> env/path setup -> host execution may receive /run/secrets/provider.key

After:
exec command -> env/path setup -> denied path preflight -> Security Violation before host execution
```

## Verification

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts` -> 119 passed
- `pnpm config:schema:check` -> ok
- `pnpm config:docs:check` -> ok
- `pnpm tsgo:core` -> ok
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/config/normalize-paths.ts src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts src/config/io.ts` -> all matched files use correct format
- `git diff --check` -> ok

The PR adds a shared `tryChmodSync` helper and routes exec approvals, task registry, task-flow registry, and plugin-state SQLite permission hardening through it for K8s fsGroup-owned paths.

Reproducibility: yes. Current main source rethrows chmod failures in exec approvals and uses raw chmod in the store paths, and the PR discussion includes concrete Docker/Kubernetes-style fsGroup terminal proof; I did not run a live pod in this read-only pass.

target.title: fix(infra): tolerate chmod EPERM on K8s fsGroup-mounted PVC
target.body:
## Summary

On Kubernetes deployments with `fsGroup`-mounted PVCs, `chmod(dir, 0o700)` fails with EPERM because the state directory is owned by root (with group ownership set to the fsGroup GID). This crashes exec approvals, task registry, task-flow registry, and plugin-state initialization on startup.

This PR extracts a shared `tryChmodSync` helper that:
1. Tries `chmodSync(target, mode)` as before
2. On EPERM/EACCES/EROFS: verifies the process can actually access the path via `accessSync`
3. If accessible → tolerates the failure (the fsGroup grants adequate permissions via group bits)
4. If not accessible → rethrows the original error

## Root cause

```
EPERM: operation not permitted, chmod '/home/openclaw/.openclaw'
```

K8s `fsGroup` volumes are owned by `root:<fsGroup>` with setgid bit. The container process runs as a non-root UID in the fsGroup, so it has full access via group membership but cannot `chmodSync` a root-owned directory.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/infra/chmod.ts` | New `tryChmodSync` helper — chmod with EPERM/EACCES/EROFS tolerance |
| `src/infra/chmod.test.ts` | 9 tests covering all error codes and access verification paths |
| `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` instead of raw `chmodSync` |
| `src/tasks/task-registry.store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |
| `src/tasks/task-flow-registry.store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |
| `src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/chmod.test.ts
 ✓ infra  ../../src/infra/chmod.test.ts (12 tests) 16ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  12 passed (12)
   Duration  152ms
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `chmod(dir, 0o700)` throws EPERM on K8s fsGroup-mounted PVC, crashing exec approvals and state initialization. The helper tolerates EPERM when access is verified, and rejects only the genuinely dangerous case (unexpected other-WRITE permission).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at `bfadbe3e37`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`. Also reproduced on OrbStack Kubernetes v1.33.9.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main (`a002c416c7`), `chmodSync(dir, 0o700)` on a K8s fsGroup volume throws EPERM and crashes the gateway on startup. Test file `src/infra/chmod.test.ts` does not exist on upstream/main.

### Permission boundary policy (updated in `bfadbe3e37`)

K8s fsGroup volumes commonly set directories to mode `2775` (setgid + rwxrwxr-x). The helper now:
- **Accepts** `2775` (other has read+execute but NOT write) ✅
- **Accepts** `2755`, `2751`, etc. (other read/execute without write) ✅
- **Rejects** `2777` or any mode with unexpected other-WRITE (`0o002`) ❌ — this signals a genuine security misconfiguration

This boundary aligns with the real K8s fsGroup behavior: volume plugins set read+execute on `other`, but never write.

### Live K8s reproduction

Pod spec:
```yaml
securityContext:
  runAsUser: 1000
  runAsGroup: 1000
  fsGroup: 1000
volumes:
  - name: state
    emptyDir: {}  # simulates fsGroup PVC
```

Terminal output from `kubectl exec`:
```
=== K8s fsGroup EPERM Reproduction ===
Node: v24.15.0
User: uid=1000(node) gid=1000(node) groups=1000(node)

--- State directory (fsGroup mount) ---
drwxrwsr-x 1 root node  0 May 20 07:00 .openclaw

Owner: 0:1000
Mode: 0o2775

--- Main branch: chmod(dir, 0o700) ---
chmod FAILED: EPERM EPERM: operation not permitted, chmod '/home/openclaw/.openclaw'

--- PR fix: tryChmodSync(dir, 0o700) ---
chmod failed with EPERM - checking access...
accessSync(R_OK|W_OK|X_OK) PASSED ✅
actualOtherWrite=0, requestedOtherWrite=0 → tolerating
→ tryChmodSync tolerates EPERM, gateway starts normally

--- Verify: can create state files ---
Write+Read: ✅ {"test":true}
Cleanup: ✅
```

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/chmod.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
 ✓ infra  ../../src/infra/chmod.test.ts (12 tests) 16ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  12 passed (12)
   Duration  152ms
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 12 chmod tests pass — EPERM tolerance with accessSync fallback, K8s fsGroup mode 2775 accepted, world-writable (other-WRITE) rejected, and normal chmod behavior preserved.
- **What was not tested**: Full OpenClaw gateway startup inside a live K8s cluster. The chmod behavior is validated via mocked fs calls and real K8s `kubectl exec` reproduction.

Closes #83619



---

### Independent verification: Docker fsGroup simulation (2026-05-21)

Docker container (node:24-bookworm-slim, aarch64) simulating K8s fsGroup — directories owned by `root:appgroup` mode `2775`, process running as `uid=1500(appuser)` in `gid=1500(appgroup)`. OpenClaw 2026.5.19.

**EPERM demonstrated on real `ensureDir()` code path** (exec-approvals-CIzTChNA.js:134):
```
Target: /data/openclaw-state/exec-approvals/approvals.json
Dir owner: root:appgroup (not us), mode: 2775
User: 1500:1500

CURRENT MAIN ensureDir(): CRASHED ❌
  EPERM: operation not permitted, chmod '/data/openclaw-state/exec-approvals'

PR #83695 ensureDir() with tryChmodSync: OK ✅
  EPERM tolerated, access verified via group bits.
```

**Directory is fully usable** (proof that EPERM is the only problem):
```
accessSync(R_OK|W_OK|X_OK): PASS
writeFileSync:               PASS
readFileSync:                PASS
unlinkSync:                  PASS
```

**Security boundary preserved**:
| Scenario | tryChmodSync |
|----------|-------------|
| K8s fsGroup dir `2775` (other r-x) | ✅ Tolerated |
| K8s fsGroup file `0660` → `0o600` | ✅ Tolerated |
| World-writable dir `0777` | ❌ Rejected |
| File `0664` → `0o600` (other-read leak) | ❌ Rejected |


### Broader proof: all four store paths (2026-05-21)

Docker fsGroup simulation covering all affected `chmodSync` call sites. All dirs `root:appgroup` mode `2775`, all files `root:appgroup` mode `0660`, process `uid=1500`.

| Store | dir `chmodSync(0o700)` | file `chmodSync(0o600)` |
|-------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| exec-approvals | EPERM ❌ → tryChmodSync OK ✅ | EPERM ❌ → tryChmodSync OK ✅ |
| task-registry | EPERM ❌ → tryChmodSync OK ✅ | EPERM ❌ → tryChmodSync OK ✅ |
| task-flow-registry | EPERM ❌ → tryChmodSync OK ✅ | EPERM ❌ → tryChmodSync OK ✅ |
| plugin-state | EPERM ❌ → tryChmodSync OK ✅ | EPERM ❌ → tryChmodSync OK ✅ |

target.title: fix(browser): allow upload from inbound media directory
target.body:
`browser.upload` rejected managed WebChat attachments because upload validation only accepted `DEFAULT_UPLOAD_DIR`. This PR keeps that uploads root and adds the managed inbound media root, including the model-visible `media://inbound/<id>` and `media/inbound/<id>` forms used by WebChat attachment staging.

Fixes #83544

AI-assisted: yes. Codex generated the patch; I reviewed the diff and verified the upload path validation behavior locally.

## Summary

- **Problem:** WebChat attachments are stored as managed inbound media, but `browser.upload` only accepted paths under the temp uploads directory.
- **Why it matters:** An agent that receives a WebChat file attachment can be unable to upload that file into a browser file input.
- **What changed:** Browser upload path validation now uses shared helpers that validate each requested file independently against the uploads directory and then the managed inbound media directory. The helpers also resolve `media://inbound/<id>` and sandbox-relative `media/inbound/<id>` before root validation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Arbitrary local paths are still rejected. Managed media references are limited to a single first-level inbound media id, and the final resolved paths still go through the existing root validators.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83544
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `browser.upload` can now resolve managed inbound media supplied as an absolute inbound path, `media://inbound/<id>`, or sandbox-relative `media/inbound/<id>`, while rejecting paths outside both allowed roots. Mixed multi-file uploads can combine temp upload files and inbound media files in one request.
- Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw checkout, Node v24, branch `fix/browser-upload-inbound-media`, using the browser extension upload path validators and Playwright upload entry tests.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/paths.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.set-input-files.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.upload-paths.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.screenshots-element-selector.test.ts --reporter=verbose

OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > resolves canonical inbound media URI references before root validation

Each requested file is validated independently, so mixed uploads containing both temp upload files and inbound media files pass when every file belongs to one allowed root. Files outside uploads and inbound media still fail validation; nested/escaped media references are rejected.
- What was not tested: Full live WebChat/Gateway/browser session with a real user attachment. The local proof covers the exact browser upload validation helpers and both Playwright upload entry points that gate the file input behavior.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The upload action originally hardcoded the temp uploads directory. The first fix added the physical inbound media directory but did not resolve the managed media reference forms WebChat exposes to agents, and validated the whole file list against one root at a time.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing upload tests mocked path validation and did not cover model-visible WebChat media references, mixed-root multi-file uploads, or the file chooser use-time validation path.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

After:

Nested references, path traversal, symlinks, hardlinks, and files outside both roots remain rejected by the existing validators.

target.title: fix(cron): add Authorization header to provider preflight probe
target.body:
## Summary

The cron job preflight check sends `GET {baseUrl}/models` to verify local provider connectivity before running a job, but was doing so **without an Authorization header**. For providers behind auth middleware (LiteLLM, vLLM, etc.), this generates spurious 401 errors on every cron run.

## Root Cause

`model-preflight.runtime.ts` → `probeLocalProviderEndpoint()` called `fetchWithSsrFGuard` with `init: { method: "GET" }` — no auth header.

## Fix

Added Authorization header from the provider's API key, resolved from the same sources as normal inference calls (config apiKey → auth-profiles.json → env var).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Cron job preflight GET `/models` to local LiteLLM proxy returns 401 because no Authorization header is sent, causing "No api key passed in" errors on every cron run.

- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw 2026.5.18 (npm install, Oracle VPS, Ubuntu 24.04, Node.js v24.15.0). LiteLLM proxy at http://127.0.0.1:4000 configured in openclaw.json. Auth method: LITELLM_API_KEY environment variable.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** (1) Applied the patch to `dist/model-preflight.runtime-*.js` on the running OpenClaw installation. (2) Waited for the next scheduled cron job run to trigger the preflight probe. (3) Monitored LiteLLM proxy logs via `pm2 logs litellm-proxy`. (4) Monitored OpenClaw gateway logs for cron job execution.

- **Evidence after fix:**

  Screenshot of terminal comparison:

  ![Before/after terminal screenshot](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/samson1357924/openclaw/fix/cron-preflight-clean/evidence-preflight-fix.jpg)

  **Before (no auth header — cron preflight triggers 401):**

  OpenClaw gateway logs (cron operations functional, just auth errors on probe):
  ```
  17:30:05+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ cron.status 158ms
  17:30:05+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ channels.status 188ms
  17:30:05+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ cron.list 192ms
  17:30:05+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ cron.runs 207ms
  ```

  LiteLLM proxy log showing the failed preflight probe (full stack trace):
  ```
  1|litellm-proxy  | 17:30:05 - LiteLLM Proxy:ERROR: auth_exception_handler.py:95 - Exception occured - No api key passed in.
  1|litellm-proxy  | Requester IP Address:127.0.0.1
  1|litellm-proxy  | Traceback (most recent call last):
  1|litellm-proxy  |   File ".../user_api_key_auth.py", line 988, in _user_api_key_auth_builder
  1|litellm-proxy  |     if valid_token.project_id is not None:
  1|litellm-proxy  |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  1|litellm-proxy  | Exception: No api key passed in.
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:58244 - "GET /models HTTP/1.1" 401 Unauthorized
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:50768 - "POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:39830 - "POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:58864 - "POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:36926 - "POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:37540 - "POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:54882 - "POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  ```

  Normal inference calls (POST /chat/completions) succeed because they carry the Authorization header — only the preflight probe was missing it.

  **After (with auth header — preflight succeeds):**

  OpenClaw gateway logs showing healthy cron operations after the fix:
  ```
  17:52:47+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ cron.status 242ms
  17:52:47+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ channels.status 274ms
  17:52:47+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ cron.list 277ms
  17:52:47+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ cron.runs 290ms
  17:52:47+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ cron.runs 294ms
  17:52:54+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ logs.tail 242ms
  17:52:54+00:00 info gateway/ws ⇄ res ✓ logs.tail 181ms
  ```

  LiteLLM proxy log showing the successful preflight probe:
  ```
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:45914 - "GET /models HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:49934 - "POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:49938 - "POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:37726 - "POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  1|litellm-proxy  | INFO:     127.0.0.1:37736 - "POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** GET /models response changed from **401 Unauthorized** to **200 OK**. No more "No api key passed in" errors in LiteLLM proxy logs. Cron preflight cache populates correctly (previously every run retried because 401 was treated as "unavailable"). Normal inference calls (POST /chat/completions) continue to work as before — unchanged. No regressions observed in cron job execution.

- **What was not tested:** Non-default cron agent directories (tested with default agent only). SecretRef-based auth (tested with direct env var LITELLM_API_KEY). Authless providers like Ollama (should be unaffected as they skip auth entirely). OAuth/request-header auth profiles (not applicable to LiteLLM setup).

## Changes

`src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.ts`:
- Added `resolveProbeApiKey()` that resolves API key from config apiKey, auth-profiles.json, or env var
- `probeLocalProviderEndpoint` now receives and uses the resolved API key
- If no key is found (authless servers like Ollama), probe proceeds without auth — same as before

target.title: feat: add per-agent compaction overrides (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds per-agent compaction and contextPruning overrides so each configured agent can resolve compaction/runtime behavior from its active agent scope instead of always inheriting `agents.defaults`.

This is for multi-agent installations where main, channel/proxy, local/self-hosted, and Codex/app-server agents need different compaction model/provider/auth-profile, timeout, cache/pruning, thinking-level, and transcript-maintenance behavior.

## Linked Issues And Related Work

Closes #52732

Related: #52751, #69988, #19501, #15826, #43834, #6571, #6572, #30452, #57901, #58077, #59863, #60512, #80784, #82008

## Problem / Root Cause

Before this change, per-agent compaction settings were either rejected by schema or only partially honored. Several runtime paths still read `agents.defaults.compaction` / `agents.defaults.contextPruning` even when the active `agents.list[]` entry had its own settings.

That broke or weakened these setups:

- main/default agent compacts with one model/provider while another agent compacts with another
- local/self-hosted agents use local provider aliases for compaction
- Codex/app-server agents intentionally omit an OpenClaw compaction model override and keep native runtime behavior
- legacy/non-agent-shaped session keys still need an explicit non-main `agentId` preserved across queued/direct compaction and maintenance paths
- queued/CLI/context-engine compaction should use `compaction.thinkingLevel`, not the active run's normal thinking level

## What Changed

- Adds strict schema/docs support for `agents.list[].compaction` and per-agent `contextPruning` semantics.
- Deep-merges per-agent `compaction` and `contextPruning` blocks over `agents.defaults`, so partial overrides inherit unspecified fields. `{}` means “override nothing,” not “clear inherited defaults.”
- Resolves compaction model/provider/auth-profile/timeout/cache/pruning/reserve/thinking settings through the active session agent.
- Threads `sessionAgentId` through queued/direct compaction, CLI compaction, context-engine maintenance, Codex app-server lifecycle, embedded attempt cache-TTL finalization, memory flush, transcript rotation, and follow-up/notice paths.
- Keeps explicit `compaction.model` behavior separate from Codex/app-server native runtime selection.
- Adds regression coverage for legacy-session/non-main-agent scope in direct, queued, CLI, Codex lifecycle, cache-TTL, memory-flush, empty/partial merge, and compaction thinking-level propagation paths.

## Reviewer Notes

The key invariant is:

> A compaction run should resolve configuration from the same active agent scope as the session/run that triggered it, even when the session key is legacy-shaped and does not encode `agent:<id>:`.

That is why this PR explicitly passes `agentId` into compaction and maintenance params instead of relying only on parsing `sessionKey`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed in current head: Per-agent compaction/contextPruning settings resolve from the active session agent in the repaired paths ClawSweeper flagged: empty-block inheritance, queued/CLI context-engine compaction thinking-level propagation, Codex lifecycle maintenance, embedded cache-TTL finalization, direct compaction fallback selection, and Codex-runtime preflight reserve-floor projection.
- Real environment tested for current head: Local OpenClaw source checkout on host `Nano-01`, branch based on `origin/main` at `1a7669bc63a0c3731577465b130873e849fc2773`, current PR head `8e9a247425c032ec466d7328ab4acd6b76db2339`.
- Exact commands run after latest patch:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-settings.test.ts \
  src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts

./node_modules/.bin/oxlint \
  src/agents/command/cli-compaction.ts \
  src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.queued.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts

node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
git diff --check
```

Evidence after latest fix:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs ...
 Test Files 1 passed (1)
 Tests 24 passed (24)
 Test Files 5 passed (5)
 Tests 162 passed (162)
 [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 112.38s

./node_modules/.bin/oxlint ...targeted files...
 Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.

node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
 [base-config-schema] ok

node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
 OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256

git diff --check
 passed
```

Observed result after latest fix: Current head opts CLI and queued context-engine compaction builders into `compaction.thinkingLevel`, preserves empty-block inheritance for compaction thinking-level, and resolves Codex-runtime preflight compaction reserve floors via the same active-agent helper as Pi settings. Regressions prove the active non-main agent wins even for legacy-shaped session keys and empty per-agent compaction blocks inherit defaults.

What was not fully clean in this current-head pass: `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --pretty false` no longer reports errors from this patch, but the broad test-src typecheck still fails on pre-existing `src/commands/status.summary.redaction.test.ts` fixtures missing `configuredModel`, `selectedModel`, and `modelSelectionReason` fields from `SessionStatus`. I did not modify that unrelated status test surface in this PR.

## Supplemental Validation

Additional focused validation previously run on this branch:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.cache-ttl.test.ts
 Test Files 2 passed (2)
 Tests 58 passed (58)
 Test Files 1 passed (1)
 Tests 15 passed (15)
 [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 71.46s

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.agents.json --pretty false
 passed

npm run config:schema:check
 [base-config-schema] ok

npm run config:docs:check
 OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256
```

## Current Branch State

- Base: `origin/main` / `1a7669bc63a0c3731577465b130873e849fc2773`
- Head: `8e9a247425c032ec466d7328ab4acd6b76db2339`
- GitHub reports the PR as mergeable; checks are expected to be queued/running after the push.

target.title: fix/telegram-context-controls
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: Telegram DM context assembly used a hardcoded recent history limit of 10, and the `Conversation Context` block was injected without a config-level control. - Why it matters: Telegram DM could include prior user messages in the current agent input, unlike the expected behavior observed in Discord DM where only the current user message is delivered. - What changed: Added Telegram configuration options to control DM history injection and `Conversation Context` injection. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Existing behavior is preserved by default. If the new options are not set, the previous hardcoded history limit of 10 remains the default, and `Conversation Context` injection remains enabled. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only. Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes; terminal screenshots and copied live output count. Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence. - Behavior or issue addressed: - Telegram DM context assembly could inject prior recent messages and the `Conversation Context` block into the current agent input. - The recent DM history limit was hardcoded to 10. - There was no config-level control to disable the `Conversation Context` block. - Real environment tested: - OS: Linux Host - OpenClaw version: main as of 2026-05-17 - Runtime/container: Host runtime, built locally from source - Integration/channel: Telegram DM - Relevant config: - `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit: 0` - `channels.telegram.conversationContext: false` - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Built OpenClaw locally from the patched source. 2. Started OpenClaw with Telegram DM configured. 3. Set `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit` to `0`. 4. Set `channels.telegram.conversationContext` to `false`. 5. Sent a Telegram DM message to the agent. 6. Verified that only the current user message was delivered, matching the expected behavior observed in Discord DM. - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): - Screenshot attached to this PR. - Observed result after fix: - Telegram DM delivered only the current user message. - Previous Telegram DM history was not injected. - The `Conversation Context` block was not injected. - This matches the expected behavior observed in Discord DM. - What was not tested: - Telegram group/channel behavior was not manually verified. - Non-Telegram integrations were not manually verified beyond the Discord DM comparison. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): - Screenshot attached if available. ## Root Cause (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: - Telegram DM context assembly used a hardcoded recent history limit of 10 and always injected the `Conversation Context` block. - Missing detection / guardrail: - There was no config-level guardrail to tune or disable Telegram DM history/context injection. - Contributing context (if known): - The original behavior is preserved by default. - This PR follows the existing implementation rule and only exposes configuration controls for operators. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - Context/config assembly tests for Telegram DM history and conversation context behavior. - Scenario the test should lock in: - Default config preserves the existing recent DM history limit of 10. - `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit: 0` disables Telegram DM history injection. - `channels.telegram.conversationContext: false` disables `Conversation Context` injection. - Configured behavior applies without changing other channel defaults. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - The behavior is determined at the Telegram DM context assembly/config boundary, so validating that boundary is enough to catch regressions without requiring a full Telegram network E2E test. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - None known. - If no new test is added, why not: - Manual verification was performed against a real Telegram DM setup. Automated coverage should be added at the Telegram DM context assembly/config boundary where this behavior is determined. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Existing defaults are unchanged. New Telegram config options allow operators to: - configure Telegram DM history injection with `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit` - disable Telegram `Conversation Context` injection with `channels.telegram.conversationContext: false` No migration is required. Existing deployments keep the same behavior unless the new config options are explicitly set. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [Telegram DM user message] -> [Telegram DM context assembly] -> [hardcoded 10 recent DM messages + Conversation Context injected] -> [agent input] After: [Telegram DM user message] -> [Telegram DM context assembly] -> [Telegram config checked] -> [configured DM history + optional Conversation Context injection] -> [agent input] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - The amount of Telegram DM history and conversation context injected into the agent input is now configurable. - Defaults preserve existing behavior. - Operators can reduce injected context by setting `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit`. - Operators can disable the `Conversation Context` block with `channels.telegram.conversationContext: false`. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Linux Host - Runtime/container: Host runtime, built locally from source - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Telegram DM - Relevant config (redacted): - `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit: 0` - `channels.telegram.conversationContext: false` ### Steps 1. Build OpenClaw locally from the patched source. 2. Start OpenClaw with Telegram DM configured. 3. Set `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit` to `0`. 4. Set `channels.telegram.conversationContext` to `false`. 5. Send a Telegram DM message to the configured agent. 6. Compare the resulting agent input with the expected Discord DM behavior. ### Expected - Telegram DM should deliver only the current user message when `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit: 0`. - Telegram DM should not inject the `Conversation Context` block when `channels.telegram.conversationContext: false`. - The result should match the expected Discord DM behavior where only the current user message is delivered. ### Actual - OpenClaw built successfully from patched source. - With `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit: 0`, Telegram DM history was not injected. - With `channels.telegram.conversationContext: false`, the `Conversation Context` block was not injected. - Telegram DM input contained only the current user message, matching the expected Discord DM behavior. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Built OpenClaw from patched source. - Sent a real Telegram DM to the configured agent. - Verified `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit: 0` disables Telegram DM history injection. - Verified `channels.telegram.conversationContext: false` disables the `Conversation Context` block. - Verified the resulting Telegram DM behavior matches Discord DM behavior where only the current user message is delivered. - Verified that leaving the new options unset preserves the original default behavior, including the previous history limit of 10. - Edge cases checked: - Missing config uses existing defaults. - The previous hardcoded limit of 10 remains the default. - Setting `channels.telegram.dmRecentLimit: 0` disables Telegram DM history injection. - Setting `channels.telegram.conversationContext: false` disables the `Conversation Context` block. - What you did **not** verify: - Telegram group/channel behavior. - All non-Telegram integrations. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): Yes - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: - No migration is required. - Existing deployments keep the same behavior unless the new config options are explicitly set. - If the new options are not set, Telegram DM history continues to use the previous default limit of 10 and `Conversation Context` injection remains enabled. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: - Operators may set a larger Telegram DM history limit than necessary, increasing token usage or exposing more prior conversation context to the model. - Mitigation: - Existing default remains 10. - Operators can reduce the value or set it to 0 when Telegram DM history injection is not desired. - Risk: - Disabling Telegram `Conversation Context` injection may reduce continuity for some Telegram DM conversations. - Mitigation: - The setting is opt-in. - Existing behavior remains enabled by default. ## AI Assistance Disclosure AI-assisted: Used Codex for implementation assistance and ChatGPT for PR wording/review planning. I manually reviewed the code, built OpenClaw locally, and verified the behavior in a real Telegram DM setup.

target.title: feat(telegram): add guest mode
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: OpenClaw did not support Telegram Guest Mode, so users could not summon a Telegram bot for a one-off answer from supported chats unless the bot was already participating through normal Telegram message handling. - Why it matters: Guest Mode lets an allowed Telegram user ask the bot for a visible, one-shot answer in a external group or normal 1:1 private chats between users, without turning that chat into a normal OpenClaw conversation. - What changed: Telegram Guest Mode can now be enabled explicitly. Allowed guest requests are routed through an isolated direct-style agent turn, preserve sender metadata, preserve configured Telegram account/channel agent routing, and deliver exactly one visible response. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal Telegram group/direct message handling remains on the existing send path. Guest Mode is disabled by default and does not inherit per-group policies or normal group conversation bindings; it uses account-level Telegram allowlist rules while preserving configured account/channel agent routing. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #79077 - Partially addresses #79077 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram bots can answer Guest Bot queries from supported Telegram chats, including groups where the bot is not a member and 1:1 private chats with other users, when `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true` is configured. - Real environment tested: Ubuntu Linux, local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway, Telegram channel configured, OpenAI Codex with GPT-5.5. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Activate the guest mode in the BotFather telegram bot settings. 2. Set `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`. 3. Built OpenClaw from this branch. 4. Built Control UI assets. 5. Restarted the local gateway. 6. Sent a Telegram guest query to the bot from an (external group where the bot was not a member, or a private chat with another user). (`@openclawbot what time is it? What is my telegram id, what is this channel / group id?`) 7. Verified that one visible answer appeared in Telegram. 8. Verified that users not allowed by the account-level allowlist could not use Guest Mode. 9. Tested groups where the bot is already a member to check that normal Telegram group handling did not regress. - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): - Observed result after fix: The guest query created an isolated direct-style Telegram guest session and produced one visible Telegram answer. Unauthorized guest senders were blocked by account-level allowFrom. Normal groups where the bot is already a member continued to work through the existing Telegram message path. - What was not tested: Bot-to-bot mode, long-running multi-account (more than 3) production soak testing, and cross-platform release-package install proof. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, OpenClaw did not include guest_message in Telegram allowed updates and had no answerGuestQuery delivery path, so Guest Bot queries were not converted into agent turns. (So this did just not exist previously) ## Root Cause (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: N/A for a new feature. Telegram Guest Bots use a new update and response shape that the existing Telegram channel did not support. - Missing detection / guardrail: Existing Telegram tests covered normal message updates, but not guest_message allowed updates, guest session isolation, or answerGuestQuery delivery. - Contributing context (if known): Guest Mode is similar to inline behavior from a product perspective, but Telegram delivers it as a guest message that must be answered through the Bot API guest-answer endpoint rather than the normal send-message path. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/allowed-updates.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/webhook.test.ts - Scenario the test should lock in: A guest update is accepted, routed to an isolated guest session, keeps sender metadata available to the agent, preserves configured Telegram account/channel agent routing, does not inherit normal group bindings or group policies, and sends only one visible Telegram guest answer. Guest updates are only requested when Guest Mode is enabled, and guest update queueing is keyed by source chat + sender instead of the normal group lane. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the Telegram plugin boundary where allowed updates, bot capability detection, guest routing, session context, authorization, and delivery behavior meet, without requiring live Telegram credentials. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A. New tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Telegram Guest Mode can now be enabled with `channels.telegram.guest.enabled`. - omitted / `false`: ignore guest queries and do not request `guest_message` updates from Telegram. - `true`: request and handle Telegram guest queries. Guest Mode uses the Telegram account-level allowlist. It intentionally does not apply per-group allowlists or normal group conversation bindings because the bot is not a member of the source group in the guest interaction. ## Diagram (if applicable) For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`. ```text Before: [Telegram guest_message] -> [not in allowed updates / no guest delivery path] -> [no visible answer] After: [Telegram guest_message] -> [account-level allowlist] -> [isolated direct-style guest session] -> [agent turn] -> [answerGuestQuery] -> [one visible Telegram answer] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) Yes. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No. - New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) Yes. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No. - Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This adds a Telegram guest-response capability using Telegram Bot API `answerGuestQuery`. Guest access is gated by the existing Telegram account-level `allowFrom` list, and OpenClaw only requests `guest_message` updates when Guest Mode is explicitly enabled. Guest turns use isolated direct-style session keys, preserve configured Telegram account/channel agent routing, skip group conversation bindings, do not receive source chat history, and deliver only one visible response. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Ubuntu Linux. - Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway. - Model/provider: OpenAI Codex, GPT-5.5. - Integration/channel (if any): Telegram. - Relevant config (redacted): Telegram channel configured with `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`; bot token and account details redacted. ### Steps 1. Enable guest mode for the bot using the telegram BotFather. 2. Configure `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`. 3. Build OpenClaw from this branch. 4. Build Control UI assets. 5. Restart the local gateway. 6. Send a Telegram guest query to the bot from an external group where the bot is not a member (or a private chat with another user). 7. Confirm that Telegram shows one visible bot answer. 8. Send a guest query from a user that is not allowed by account-level allowFrom. 9. Send normal messages in groups where the bot is already a member. ### Expected - Allowed guest queries create an isolated guest session and produce one visible Telegram answer. - Unauthorized guest senders are blocked. - Normal Telegram group handling still works in groups where the bot is already a member. ### Actual - Allowed guest queries produced one visible answer. - Unauthorized guest senders were blocked. - Existing normal Telegram group handling still worked. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [X] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Live Telegram Guest Mode round trip from a group where the bot was not a member. - Normal Telegram private chats with the bot. - Normal Telegram private chats with other users using the Guest Mode work. - Unauthorized guest sender blocked by account-level allowlist. - Normal Telegram groups where the bot is already a member still handled messages normally. - Control UI assets load after pnpm ui:build. - Local retest after the account-bound guest routing fix. - Edge cases checked: - Unauthorized guest sender fallback. - Guest isolation from normal group bindings. (Needs to remove bot in between from the group) - What you did not verify: - Bot-to-bot mode. - Long-running multi-account (>3) production soak. - Cross-platform release-package install proof. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. Local Codex review was run during the beta-branch iteration and again after moving the patch onto current `origin/main`. Actionable findings from the earlier rounds were addressed before opening the PR. The final main-based Codex review reported no discrete actionable correctness issues. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes. - Config/env changes? (Yes/No) Yes. - Migration needed? (Yes/No) No. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Telegram Guest Mode supports only one visible answer for this interaction shape. - Mitigation: Guest delivery sends only the first final answer through answerGuestQuery. - Risk: Unauthorized users could trigger guest turns (new ingress style). - Mitigation: Guest authorization uses the Telegram account-level allowFrom list, matching the Telegram DM access boundary. ~Horo

target.title: [codex] make workspace prompt routing selectable
target.body:
## Summary This rebuild turns the Codex workspace prompt routing from a forced token-efficiency change into an explicit, user-selectable prompt-surface policy. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.workspacePromptSurface`: - `per_turn_context` (default): quality-first legacy behavior. `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain fresh current-turn context. - `thread_developer`: opt-in token-efficient/high-priority mode. Only stable profile files (`SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`) move to Codex thread developer instructions. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`: - `soul_when_present` (default): when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered, send Codex `personality: "none"` and let the OpenClaw bridge point personality/tone/voice at `SOUL.md`. - `codex`: keep Codex's native personality overlay. - `none`: always disable Codex's named native personality overlay. - Keeps `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` native to Codex project-doc loading instead of duplicating them in OpenClaw prompt text, while fingerprinting their root workspace content so existing OpenClaw bindings rotate when the docs change. - Removes the lightweight Codex `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression so cron/lightweight Codex runs preserve exact user input without disabling native project docs. Lightweight exact-command runs also do not disable Codex's native personality just because `SOUL.md` exists on disk; the SOUL override applies only when SOUL context is actually delivered. - Adds binding metadata/fingerprints so stale native Codex threads rotate when prompt surface, personality mode, native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. ## Why this PR exists The earlier Codex prompt separation work was right to stop mixing PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and native Codex prompt assumptions. Native Codex should keep its own base/model contract, while OpenClaw should add only the host-runtime bridge it owns: message delivery, dynamic OpenClaw tools, ACP/OpenClaw delegation boundaries, and runtime/channel context. The next optimization moved `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `USER.md` out of every turn and into Codex developer instructions. That was attractive for token efficiency, but the adversarial review found a real quality/regression risk: users edit soul/profile/memory files during long-running OpenClaw sessions, and native Codex thread developer instructions are not a per-turn live-reload surface. Once a native Codex thread is already bound, resume-time developer/personality changes can be ignored by the loaded thread unless OpenClaw intentionally rotates the binding. The final direction here is: quality is the default, token efficiency is a choice. ## Prompt shape ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions"] --> B["Codex native personality slot"] B --> C["OpenClaw Codex developer bridge"] C --> D{"workspacePromptSurface"} D -->|"per_turn_context (default)"| E["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
MEMORY / active BOOTSTRAP
fresh current-turn context"] D -->|"thread_developer (opt-in)"| F["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
Codex thread developer instructions"] E --> G["Current user request"] F --> G H["AGENTS.md / AGENTS.override.md"] --> I["Codex native project-doc loader"] I --> G J["HEARTBEAT.md"] --> K["Heartbeat collaboration-mode pointer"] K --> G ``` The bridge text is intentionally narrow. It says OpenClaw is the host runtime and Codex is the native execution harness, that OpenClaw may prepend current-turn workspace/memory/skill/channel context, and that this context is reference material for the current turn rather than a replacement for Codex system/developer instructions. It also explicitly avoids treating every OpenClaw turn as coding work. When non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered and `personalityMode` is left at the default, Codex receives the protocol enum `personality: "none"`. This is not model-visible text that says "have no personality"; Codex's protocol describes `none` as an empty native personality placeholder. The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md` via the OpenClaw bridge. Empty placeholder SOUL files keep Codex's native personality behavior. ## What we learned ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original legacy behavior
fresh per-turn soul/profile context"] --> B["Prompt separation
Codex gets Codex lane, PI gets PI lane"] B --> C["Token optimization
stable files moved to developerInstructions"] C --> D["Adversarial finding
developerInstructions are not reliable live-refresh for existing native threads"] D --> E["This PR
default legacy quality + opt-in thread developer mode + binding rotation"] ``` Key findings from the review: - `SOUL.md` is personality, not collaboration mode. `collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` is too strong and mode-oriented for general soul/profile content; it remains appropriate for heartbeat/cron pointer behavior. - `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` are Codex project-doc concerns. OpenClaw should not duplicate them into turn text, but it should stop suppressing native loading and rotate OpenClaw's native Codex binding when loaded project-doc content changes. - `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skills are volatile enough to remain per-turn context even in the token-efficient mode. - `thread_developer` needs freshness metadata. If users opt into it, profile-file edits must rotate the native Codex thread rather than silently relying on resume-time overrides. - The default should preserve the legacy quality/freshness behavior because token savings should not silently outweigh agent identity quality. OpenAI's Codex prompt write-up describes native project-doc loading with `AGENTS.override.md`, `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback filenames, and the `project_doc_max_bytes` cap: https://openai.com/index/unrolling-the-codex-agent-loop/ ## Authority and lifetime | Surface | Owner | Files / content | Lifetime | This PR | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Codex base/model instructions | Codex | Codex system/model stack | Native thread | OpenClaw does not override | | Codex personality slot | Codex protocol | `friendly`, `pragmatic`, `none` | Native thread/turn param | Configurable via `personalityMode`; defaults to `none` only when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered | | OpenClaw developer bridge | OpenClaw | Runtime/delivery/tool/delegation bridge | Native thread | Kept narrow and host-owned | | OpenClaw per-turn context | OpenClaw | Soul/profile/memory/bootstrap/skills/channel context | Every turn | Default quality-first surface | | Codex thread developer instructions | OpenClaw -> Codex | Stable soul/profile files only | Native thread | Opt-in `thread_developer`, with fingerprint rotation | | Native Codex project docs | Codex | `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, fallbacks | Native project-doc load | Preserved; no `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression | | Heartbeat collaboration pointer | OpenClaw | Pointer to `HEARTBEAT.md` | Heartbeat turn | Still separate from soul/profile | ## Scenario coverage | Scenario | Expected behavior | | --- | --- | | Default Codex app-server run with non-empty `SOUL.md` | Stable profile files are current-turn context; Codex native personality is disabled; bridge points personality at `SOUL.md`. | | Default Codex app-server run with empty `SOUL.md` | Empty placeholder content does not suppress Codex's native personality overlay. | | User edits `SOUL.md` mid-session in default mode | Next turn sees the edit because the file remains per-turn context. | | User opts into `thread_developer` | Stable profile files move to developer instructions; `MEMORY.md` and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain turn context. | | User edits `SOUL.md` in `thread_developer` mode | OpenClaw fingerprints the tracked developer files and starts a fresh native Codex thread. | | User edits `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` | OpenClaw fingerprints loaded native project-doc content and rotates the native Codex binding. | | Lightweight cron Codex run | OpenClaw still skips extra bootstrap prompt text for exact command preservation, does not disable native Codex project docs, and does not send `personality: "none"` unless the user explicitly configured `personalityMode: "none"`. | | Existing stale binding from the previous PR | Missing prompt-surface metadata forces a new native Codex thread, so old bindings do not preserve stale developer/user context. | | Manual `/codex resume ` binding | The minimal manual binding resumes once, then persists the current prompt-surface/personality metadata after successful resume. | ## Implementation notes - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts` adds the new config enums and resolvers. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` selects the workspace prompt surface, computes the workspace fingerprint, chooses the positive SOUL bridge behavior, and keeps native project docs out of OpenClaw turn text. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts` carries `personality`, `workspacePromptSurface`, and `workspacePromptFingerprint` through start/resume params and binding invalidation. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.ts` persists the prompt-surface metadata. - Docs now describe the architecture in `docs/plugins/codex-harness-runtime.md`, `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`, and `docs/concepts/system-prompt.md`. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Codex workspace prompt routing is selectable, defaults to fresh per-turn profile context, rotates stale native Codex bindings when native project docs or thread-developer profile files change, and only disables Codex's native personality when non-empty SOUL context is actually delivered or the user explicitly forces `personalityMode: "none"`. - Current narrowed head tested: `ad037cc543` on branch `codex/workspace-context-routing-v2`, rebased on `upstream/main` `a13468320c`. - Scope note: this head removes the prior fs-safe default flip, archive fallback, Windows ACL policy change, and dependency expectation cleanup from the PR. The only non-Codex file left is `extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts`, a behavior-preserving object-spread lint cleanup required because the repo's fail-safe changed gate lints the extension surface on this branch. - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`, using a temporary OpenClaw gateway and Codex app-server model route. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` - Evidence after fix: ```text [gateway-codex-live] project-doc-budget-binding-proof { "defaultHiddenEditKeptThread": true, "hiddenEditKeptThread": true, "visibleEditRotatedThread": true, "defaultFirst": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "defaultHidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "first": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "hidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "visible": { "threadId": "019e45f3-6951-7eb0-83ab-5b009ececa6f", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "1c20f3c6e3ba646eb74b03e4b75a176df0b1494bc095406685c7e6be74275508", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" } } Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 1 passed | 2 skipped (3) Duration 94.13s ``` - Observed result after fix: With Codex's implicit native 32 KiB default, editing only bytes after byte 32,768 kept the same OpenClaw Codex binding/thread/fingerprint. With `project_doc_max_bytes = 8`, editing only bytes outside the explicit native budget kept the same binding/thread/fingerprint, while editing bytes inside the native budget rotated both the native thread and workspace prompt fingerprint. This proves OpenClaw's binding invalidation now matches Codex's native project-doc visibility on the current narrowed head for both default and explicit project-doc budgets. - What was not tested: Broad cross-platform and full-matrix coverage are left to GitHub Actions; the live proof used local macOS Codex app-server auth/model access. ## Validation Current-head focused validation from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "project_doc_max_bytes|default project-doc budget" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 3 tests passed, 195 skipped. Current-head live Codex harness proof: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 1 test passed, 2 skipped. Changed-gate validation: ```bash node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs -- extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. `pnpm check:changed` completed typecheck, lint, dependency/package guards, duplicate coverage, runtime sidecar loader guard, conflict-marker/changelog checks, and runtime import-cycle checks. ## Maintainer review notes This PR intentionally keeps the Codex-native lane narrow: - It does not change harness selection policy, model routing, auth routing, or Codex app-server transport behavior. - It preserves native Codex base/model instructions and native project-doc loading instead of replacing them with OpenClaw prompt text. - It keeps quality-first per-turn profile context as the default and makes token-efficient thread developer instructions opt-in. - It rotates native Codex bindings when the selected prompt surface, personality policy, visible native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. - It leaves volatile `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skill context in the per-turn lane even when stable soul/profile files are opted into thread developer instructions.

target.title: feat(mattermost): add /model dialog picker
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Mattermost `/oc_model` and `/oc_models` still relied on the older message picker, so they could not use Mattermost's richer dialog flow.
- Why it matters: the Discord picker already feels native and multi-step; Mattermost needed a comparable native picker path instead of staying button-first.
- What changed: plain `/oc_model` and `/oc_models` now open a Mattermost dialog when `trigger_id` is available, dialog refresh/submit callbacks stay inside the Mattermost extension, and the final apply still routes through the existing shared `/model` command path.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no shared interactive-core expansion, no unrelated config/schema work, and the older message picker still remains as fallback when a dialog cannot be opened.
- Compatibility note: the provider -> model/runtime refresh path relies on Mattermost dynamic dialog refresh. Upstream documents that refresh path as a Mattermost Server `11.1+` capability, and older servers were not validated in this PR.

## Links

- Issue/PR refs: none

## Verification

- `CI=true PNPM_CONFIG_PM_ON_FAIL=ignore PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/model-picker.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts`
- `CI=true PNPM_CONFIG_PM_ON_FAIL=ignore PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/model-picker.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/model-picker.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts`
- `CI=true PNPM_CONFIG_PM_ON_FAIL=ignore PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `CI=true PNPM_CONFIG_PM_ON_FAIL=ignore PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm test src/infra/outbound/source-delivery-plan.test.ts`
- Rebased onto current `origin/main` and resolved the single overlapping `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/model-picker.ts` import conflict from current main.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost native `/oc_model` slash-command routing and dialog callback reachability on the live devbox-to-vp deployment that backs this plugin test setup.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw gateway running from this worktree on `devbox`, published through Tailscale Serve at `https://devbox.tail9cbe19.ts.net`; Mattermost running on `vp` and calling the gateway over that public tailnet URL.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Restart the gateway from the `codex-mm-model-dialog` worktree on `devbox`.
  2. Let Mattermost native `oc_*` commands register against `https://devbox.tail9cbe19.ts.net/api/channels/mattermost/command`.
  3. Probe the slash callback from the Mattermost host and verify it responds as a live POST-only endpoint.
  4. Run plain `/oc_model` in the Mattermost workspace tied to that deployment.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted runtime logs and copied live output from the real deployment.

```text
11:37:01 [mattermost] registered command /oc_model (id=rr9eyu8daif6p87czrtkwxb6fe)
11:37:02 [mattermost] slash commands activated for account default (8 commands)
11:37:02 [mattermost] slash commands registered (8 commands across 1 teams, callback=https://devbox.tail9cbe19.ts.net/api/channels/mattermost/command)
```

```text
$ curl -k -sS --connect-timeout 5 --max-time 10 -D - https://devbox.tail9cbe19.ts.net/api/channels/mattermost/command
HTTP/2 405
allow: POST
```

- Observed result after fix: The live deployment registered native Mattermost commands against the reachable devbox callback URL instead of the old `localhost` route, the callback was reachable from the Mattermost VPS, and the live tester on that deployment reported `/oc_model` working after the routing and channel-lookup fixes. This follow-up branch update also carries the same verified slash channel facts through dialog submit when later channel lookup is forbidden.
- What was not tested: A fresh screenshot or video of the post-rebase dialog submit flow is not attached here; the new submit-fallback behavior itself is locked by the added regression coverage in `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/model-picker.test.ts` and `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts`, and the broader rebased Mattermost shard including `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.test.ts` and `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts` stays green.

## Behavior Notes

- The new picker is Mattermost-owned UI: dialog open, refresh, and submit live in `extensions/mattermost/**`.
- Provider-dependent dialog refresh relies on Mattermost dynamic dialog refresh support, which upstream documents for Mattermost Server `11.1+`; this PR was validated only against the live server used in this branch testing, not against older Mattermost releases.
- If Mattermost does not provide a usable `trigger_id`, OpenClaw falls back to the existing message-based picker instead of breaking `/oc_model`.
- Slash-command authorization now falls back to channel information already present in the slash payload when `GET /channels/<id>` is forbidden, and dialog submit now reuses the signed fallback channel snapshot for the same restricted-channel case.
