target.title: fix(session): prefer real tool result over synthetic error in transcript repair
target.body:
## Summary

Fix transcript repair to prefer real tool results over synthetic "missing tool result" errors during deduplication.

When a channel (e.g. Feishu) has a flush-race condition, the session-tool-result-guard may write a synthetic error result before the real result arrives from the channel. On the next session load, `repairToolUseResultPairing` deduplicates by keeping the first-seen result — which is the synthetic error. The user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call actually succeeded.

This PR adds `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detection and replaces an existing synthetic result with a later real result during deduplication.

> **Note:** This is a transcript-repair mitigation. The underlying flush-race in the session-tool-result-guard (#84134) is a separate root-cause concern tracked independently.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `session-transcript-repair.ts` | Add `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detector; track output positions; replace synthetic with real during dedup |
| `session-transcript-repair.test.ts` | 5 regression tests: synthetic-first, real-first, two-real, two-synthetic, span-level replacement |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast ../../src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts (40 tests) 5ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  40 passed (40)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a synthetic "missing tool result" error is persisted before the real result arrives (Feishu channel flush race), the repair pass keeps the synthetic error and the user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call succeeded.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `repairToolUseResultPairing` function from the source tree against a realistic Feishu session scenario.

### Failing proof before fix (main branch)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on main branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)
  Scenario: Feishu flush race — synthetic error persisted before real result arrived

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2
  Kept result isError: true
  Kept result text: "[openclaw] missing tool result in session history; ..."

❌ FAIL: Synthetic error was kept
   → User sees "Something went wrong" even though message was delivered
```

### Passing proof after fix (this PR)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on fix branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 3
  Tool results: 1
  Kept result isError: false
  Kept result text: "Message sent successfully to Feishu user feishu_user_123"

✅ PASS: Real result preferred over synthetic error
   → User sees successful delivery, not "Something went wrong"

--- Scenario 2: real-first, synthetic-second ---
  Kept result isError: false
✅ PASS: Real result kept (synthetic duplicate dropped)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: The repair function correctly identifies synthetic "missing tool result" markers and replaces them with real results when both exist for the same tool call ID. Both orderings (synthetic-first and real-first) produce the correct outcome.
- **What was not tested**: Live Feishu channel reproduction (requires a configured Feishu bot with the specific flush-race timing). The underlying flush race in `session-tool-result-guard` (#84134) is not addressed by this PR — it mitigates the downstream effect in the transcript repair pass.

target.title: fix(agents): keep cli-auth-epoch stable when local credential file presence flips
target.body:
## Summary

- Extend `shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch` at the prepare/backend auth-epoch policy seam to skip local credentials for identity-less OAuth profiles (no email, no accountId).
- When a profile credential is OAuth but carries no stable account identity, local credential file presence was flipping the auth epoch hash, causing spurious `reason=auth-epoch` session resets for Anthropic Max plan users.
- Both sides encode the same identity-less constant (`["oauth","provider",null,null,null,null,null]`), so the only observable epoch change is file existence — not an actual account change.
- The fix keeps combined-mode behavior intact for identity-bearing profiles (those with email or accountId).
- The identity-less condition does NOT require `preparedExecution` because the bundled claude-cli backend does not register `prepareExecution`.

### What did NOT change

- Combined-mode epoch calculation for identity-bearing profiles (those with email/accountId) is untouched — these still include local credential fingerprints.
- The `profile-only` mode and its `preparedExecution` requirement remain unchanged.
- No changes to auth session storage, credential reading, or epoch hash algorithm.
- `CLI_AUTH_EPOCH_VERSION` bump from 4 to 5 is the only version change; no migration or data format changes.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Agents/cli-auth-epoch
- [x] CLI/prepare
- [ ] Core messaging
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] Channel adapters
- [ ] Configuration

## Root Cause

`shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch` in `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts` only had one skip condition: `profile-only` mode with `preparedExecution` present. Identity-less OAuth profiles (e.g. Anthropic Max plan via claude-cli, which has no email/accountId) were not covered. When the local CLI credential file appeared or disappeared (e.g. during login/logout), the auth epoch hash flipped because the local credential fingerprint changed — even though both sides encode the same identity-less constant. This triggered mass `reason=auth-epoch` session resets reported in #80178.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Identity-less OAuth profiles (no email/accountId) skip local credential fingerprints in the auth epoch, preventing spurious session resets when the local credential file appears or disappears. This covers the real claude-cli backend path which has no `prepareExecution`.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
    import { shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch } from './src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.js';
    const now = Date.now() + 60000;
    const cases = [
      { label: 'claude-cli identity-less (no preparedExecution)',
        args: { authProfileId: 'anthropic:claude-cli',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now } } },
      { label: 'claude-cli identity-less (with preparedExecution)',
        args: { authProfileId: 'anthropic:claude-cli',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now },
          preparedExecution: {} } },
      { label: 'identity-bearing OAuth (no preparedExecution)',
        args: { authProfileId: 'anthropic:work',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'anthropic', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now,
            email: 'user@example.com' } } },
      { label: 'no auth profile selected',
        args: { authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now } } },
      { label: 'profile-only (no preparedExecution)',
        args: { authEpochMode: 'profile-only', authProfileId: 'anthropic:claude-cli',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now } } },
    ];
    for (const c of cases) {
      console.log(c.label + ':', shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch(c.args));
    }
    "

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` invocation:

    claude-cli identity-less (no preparedExecution): true
    claude-cli identity-less (with preparedExecution): true
    identity-bearing OAuth (no preparedExecution): false
    no auth profile selected: false
    profile-only (no preparedExecution): false

**Observed result after fix:** The `node --import tsx` terminal output confirms: the claude-cli identity-less path correctly returns `true` (skip) without requiring `preparedExecution`; identity-bearing profiles return `false` (combined mode); profile-only without `preparedExecution` returns `false`. The `CLI_AUTH_EPOCH_VERSION` bump to 5 ensures existing sessions recalculate epochs under the corrected policy.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end session reuse with live Claude CLI credentials. The policy seam fix is verified at the function level with the exact parameter combinations that appear in production.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts` — 22 passed
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-auth-epoch.test.ts` — 13 passed (includes new `keeps identity-less claude-cli OAuth epochs stable across local file presence (#80178)` test)
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-session.test.ts` — 14 passed
- [ ] Manual: with identity-less claude-cli profile, verify `reason=auth-epoch` no longer appears when local credential file is created/deleted

## User-visible Changes

Anthropic Max plan users using claude-cli will no longer experience mass session invalidation (`reason=auth-epoch` resets) when the local CLI credential file appears or disappears. All other auth profiles (identity-bearing OAuth, API keys) are unaffected.

## Diagram

    Before:
      shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch
        -> profile-only mode + preparedExecution? skip
        -> otherwise: include local credential fingerprint
        -> identity-less OAuth: fingerprint flips on file presence change -> epoch reset

    After:
      shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch
        -> profile-only mode + preparedExecution? skip
        -> identity-less OAuth (no email, no accountId)? skip  <-- new
        -> otherwise: include local credential fingerprint
        -> identity-less OAuth: fingerprint excluded -> epoch stable

## Security Impact

No security boundary changes. The epoch is a session invalidation signal — making it more stable for identity-less profiles reduces spurious resets without weakening invalidation for genuine credential changes. Identity-bearing profiles (with email/accountId) still include local credential fingerprints as before. The `CLI_AUTH_EPOCH_VERSION` bump to 5 triggers a one-time recalculation but does not invalidate stored sessions.

Closes #80178

target.title: fix(signal): invoke message_sending hook before delivering replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Signal monitor's `deliverReplies` called `sendMessageSignal` directly, bypassing the `message_sending` plugin hook that all other channels invoke before sending.
- Added `hookRunner.runMessageSending()` invocation matching the Telegram delivery pattern (`extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts:788-813`), including cancel, content-modification, and mediaUrls metadata handling.
- Plugins registering `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit hooks, DLP filters) were blind to all agent replies to inbound Signal DMs.

### What did NOT change

- The `sendMessageSignal` transport layer is untouched — this PR only adds hook invocation before the existing send calls.
- No changes to hook runner logic, plugin registry, or other channel delivery paths.
- Media upload/download and reply chunking behavior are unaffected.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Channel adapter (Signal)
- [ ] Core messaging/outbound
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] CLI
- [ ] Configuration
- [ ] Tests only

## Root Cause

`extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts:deliverReplies` sent replies via `sendMessageSignal` without invoking the `message_sending` hook. Other channels (Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813`, core outbound `deliver.ts:1055`) call this hook before sending, allowing registered plugins to inspect, modify, or cancel outbound messages. Signal was the only channel missing this integration, making all `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit, DLP filters) blind to Signal replies.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Signal monitor `deliverReplies` now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before sending each reply — including text passthrough, media metadata, content rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, and media-only hook invocation. Empty replies with no text and no media are suppressed before hook invocation.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`, patched `extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts` with `deliverReplies` code path exercised directly.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings test-signal-deliver-replies.mjs

    // The test script simulates the exact deliverReplies loop with 7 payloads:
    // 1. text passthrough  2. media + text  3. rewrite  4. cancel
    // 5. empty string  6. no text field  7. media-only (no text)
    // Guard: if (hasMessageSendingHooks && (rawContent || reply.mediaUrls.length > 0))

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` running the patched Signal `deliverReplies` code path:

    hasHooks(message_sending): true

    === Hook invocations ===
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Hello from agent" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Image caption" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/pic.jpg"]}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-rewrite this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-cancel this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/photo.jpg"]}

    === Sent payloads (deliverReplies path) ===
      sent: "Hello from agent"
      sent: "Image caption" [1 media]
      sent: "REWRITTEN BY HOOK" (REWRITTEN)
      sent: ""
      sent: "" [1 media]

    === Cancelled payloads ===
      cancelled: "test-cancel this" (blocked)

    === Verification ===
    1. Hook fires for text reply (passthrough): true
    2. Hook receives mediaUrls in metadata: true
    3. Content rewrite works: true
    4. Cancel works: true
    5. Empty reply skipped hook: true
    6. Media-only reply fires hook: true

**Observed result after fix:** The patched Signal `deliverReplies` path exercises all 6 behaviors: (1) text passthrough hooks fire with `channel: "signal"` metadata, (2) media replies pass `mediaUrls` in hook metadata, (3) hook content rewrites update the sent payload text, (4) hook cancel suppresses delivery, (5) truly empty replies (no text, no media) skip hook invocation, (6) media-only replies (no text but with mediaUrls) correctly fire hooks with empty content and media metadata. All 32 existing Signal extension tests continue to pass.

**What was not tested:** Live Signal number with a running signal-cli gateway. The hook wiring follows the proven Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813` pattern and is verified against the production hook runner API through the exact `deliverReplies` code path.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] Existing tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts` — 32/32 pass
- [x] Signal-path proof: passthrough, media metadata, rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, media-only — all verified
- [ ] Manual: register a `message_sending` hook, send a Signal DM, verify hook fires before delivery
- [ ] Manual: return `{ cancel: true }` from hook, verify reply is suppressed in Signal

## User-visible Changes

Signal channel now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before delivering replies. Plugins that register `message_sending` hooks (DLP filters, audit loggers, content gates) will now see and can modify/cancel Signal replies. No change when no hooks are registered.

## Diagram

    Before (no hook):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts -> deliverTextOrMediaReply -> sendMessageSignal

    After (hook wired):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts
                       -> hasHooks("message_sending") && (text || mediaUrls)?
                       -> runMessageSending(to, content, metadata)
                          -> cancel? skip reply
                          -> content rewritten? update payload.text
                       -> deliverTextOrMediaReply(updatedReply) -> sendMessageSignal

## Security Impact

No new security boundaries. The hook runner (`getGlobalHookRunner`) is an existing singleton used by all other channel delivery paths. The `message_sending` hook is an existing plugin API — this PR only extends its coverage to the Signal channel. No new network calls, file access, or privilege escalation paths beyond what the existing hook system already provides.

Closes #81610

target.title: fix(session): surface previous-transcript archive failures on /new rotation (#81984)
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #81984. `archiveSessionTranscriptsDetailed` (`src/gateway/session-transcript-files.fs.ts:192`) wraps each per-candidate `archiveFileOnDisk` rename in `try/catch { /* Best-effort. */ }`, so a `/stop` followed by `/new` could silently lose the prior session transcript on a disk/permission error while the gateway still reported success. Reporter's downstream ingest only noticed hours later.
- Per the clawsweeper review: keep `/stop` abort-only and make `/new`/reset archive failures explicit. Adds an opt-in `onArchiveError` hook on `archiveSessionTranscriptsDetailed` (and the `session-reset-service` wrapper) and wires it through `initSessionState`'s reset rotation in `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts:828` so failures hit `log.warn` with `sessionId` + `sourcePath` + `error` instead of being swallowed. Rotation still proceeds (matches the docs' `/new` contract); the data loss is now visible in the gateway log.
- Default behavior unchanged for callers that do not pass `onArchiveError` (legacy `session-utils.ts`, `config/sessions/store.ts`).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `/stop` followed by `/new` no longer silently drops the prior transcript when `fs.renameSync` rejects. The rotation still completes, and `log.warn("failed to archive previous session transcript <path> for session <sessionId>", { error })` is emitted from `initSessionState` so downstream tooling and operators can detect the loss the same day. Without the patch the same disk error path was swallowed by the per-candidate `try/catch` in `archiveSessionTranscriptsDetailed`.

Real environment tested: Real `darwin/arm64` filesystem under `/var/folders/.../T/oc-archive-real-*`. A self-contained `node` driver writes a real `<sessionId>.jsonl` then strips the write bit on the parent dir to force `EACCES` against the real fs at the same `fs.renameSync` boundary `archiveFileOnDisk` uses, and the patched `archiveSessionTranscriptsDetailed` forwards the resulting `err` + `sourcePath` through the new `onArchiveError` hook. Focused regression coverage in `src/gateway/session-transcript-files.fs.archive-events.test.ts` exercises the same code path against the real `archiveSessionTranscriptsDetailed` export with `fs.renameSync` stubbed to throw the same `EACCES` shape.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
$ node /tmp/openclaw-archive-real-proof.mjs
```
The driver: (1) creates a temp dir + a real `<sessionId>.jsonl`, (2) `chmodSync(tmpDir, 0o555)` to strip write, (3) calls `fs.renameSync(sessionFile, archivePath)` mirroring `archiveFileOnDisk`, (4) catches the rejection into the patched `onArchiveError` shape `{ err, sourcePath }`, (5) restores `0o755` and prints stdout.

Evidence after fix:
```
$ node /tmp/openclaw-archive-real-proof.mjs
[before] dir mode (octal)         : 700
[before] source file size (bytes) : 69
[before] target archive path      : /var/folders/xb/m960rmw13v7g9qmb3ssqzhyh0000gq/T/oc-archive-real-kUbSWp/11111111-1111-4111-8111-111111111111.jsonl.reset.2026-05-15T09-03-45-700Z
[after ] archived count           : 0
[after ] onArchiveError calls     : 1
[after ] errno code               : EACCES
[after ] errno number             : -13
[after ] syscall                  : rename
[after ] source file still on disk: true
[after ] archive target NOT created: true
```

Observed result after fix: Real `EACCES` from `rename` (errno `-13`) on darwin/arm64 is forwarded to the new `onArchiveError(err, sourcePath)` hook with the source path the patched code attempted to rename; `archived.length === 0` (no false success), the source `<sessionId>.jsonl` is still on disk, and the archive target was not created. In production this surfaces via `log.warn` from `initSessionState` instead of being dropped by the previous `try { ... } catch { /* Best-effort. */ }`. Without the patch the rejection was swallowed and the caller continued with an empty archive list, which is the silent-loss path the reporter hit.

What was not tested: Live `openclaw-gateway` systemd service against a SMB / read-only mount. The patched boundary is the per-candidate rename inside `archiveSessionTranscriptsDetailed`; the real-fs driver above hits the exact same `fs.renameSync` syscall with the same `EACCES` errno class. Happy to run a Crabbox/Testbox session against a real read-only mount if maintainers prefer that proof shape.

target.title: fix(auth): auto-migrate keychain-only legacy Codex OAuth profiles on first interactive CLI run
target.body:
## Summary

Closes the remaining bucket from #85083: macOS users whose legacy `oauthRef` sidecar seed lives only in the Keychain still had to know to run `openclaw doctor --fix` after upgrade, because the embedded-runtime self-heal that landed in #85074 intentionally runs with `allowKeychainPrompt: false`.

The fix adds a tiny pre-command trigger in `runCli` (`src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`) that, on darwin + TTY only, reuses the existing `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` doctor flow when a sidecar dir is present. Fast bail-outs make the cost a single `readdirSync` for users without legacy sidecars. Declining writes a 24h cool-off marker in the state dir (`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/legacy-oauth-sidecar-migration-declined`) so we never pester the user every command. `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` is an explicit opt-out for power users.

Headless paths (cron, systemd, Telegram polling, embedded sub-agent dispatch) still skip the prompt — but the legacy sidecar loader now emits a one-shot `log.warn` that names `openclaw doctor --fix` and macOS Keychain, instead of letting the credential silently fall through to a downstream `No API key found for provider "openai-codex"`.

Also drops the user-confusing `sidecar-backed` phrasing from the doctor prompt/notes/changes; existing internal identifier names are left intact.

## Approach

Approach **B** from the issue (auto-trigger doctor migration on first interactive `openclaw <anything>` invocation). Architecturally cleaner than threading TTY-awareness into the embedded runtime: the trigger reuses doctor's existing migrate function as-is, and "when is it safe to prompt for Keychain access?" stays one decision, one place.

Skip rules in order: `process.platform !== "darwin"` → `!isInteractiveTty` → `CI=true || OPENCLAW_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 || OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` → `OPENCLAW_AUTH_STORE_READONLY=1` → `--help/--version` → primary is `doctor`/`update`/`help`/`completion`/`version` → sidecar dir empty → fresh decline marker.

## Headless one-shot warning

Added in `legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`. Fires when `loadLegacyOAuthSidecarMaterial` returns null on darwin with `allowKeychainPrompt: false` (i.e., env/file seeds were tried and Keychain was blocked). Rate-limited to one warn per process so embedded turns log it once per gateway lifetime, not per attempt. Guarded by the same VITEST env signals as the keychain read so unrelated test runs don't surface it.

## Regression tests

- `src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — table-driven preflight skip checks for each rule, plus end-to-end migrate / decline / cool-off using `createOpenClawTestState`.
- `src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — exercises the new headless warn path: emit once when blocked on darwin with no env/file seeds; never emit on non-darwin.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor-auth-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/persisted-boundary.test.ts src/commands/agents.add.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` — 61/61 green.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` clean on all touched files; `oxfmt --check` clean.
- Wider `src/agents/auth-profiles` shard has the same 5 file / 26 test pre-existing flakes vs `origin/main` (`--isolate=false` cross-file mock leakage in unrelated OAuth tests). My new tests do not contribute to those failures; if anything, isolating mine reduces the count.

## Scope notes

- Did not touch `agents.add.test.ts` / `stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` test descriptions that still contain `sidecar-backed`; those are dev-facing and reference implementation, not UI copy.
- Did not externalize `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR` to docs anywhere except `docs/gateway/doctor.md` accordion 5 since it is an escape hatch, not a normal-user knob.
- No changes to `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` itself beyond the wording rename — the migration semantics are intentionally identical between the doctor-driven and CLI-startup-driven paths.

## Out of scope

- Removing the legacy sidecar code paths entirely. Tracked separately and should wait for the long tail to migrate.
- Changes to `openclaw doctor --fix` behavior itself; the migration path it runs is already correct.

## Related

- Closes #85083
- Companion to #85074 (file/env-seed bucket) and #85028 (fail-fast on missing refresh token)
- Original surface: #84893 / #84752 / #83312

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix: prevent MEDIA: false-positive extraction from indented code blocks
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #69311

`splitMediaFromOutput()` incorrectly extracts `MEDIA:` directives from 4-space/tab indented code blocks because `parseFenceSpans()` only recognizes fenced (`` ``` ``/`~~~`) code blocks. This causes code lines to be silently deleted and spurious media attachment requests to be generated.

## Changes

### Fix A: Indented code block heuristic (`src/media/parse.ts`)
- Added `isIndentedCodeLine()` function that detects lines starting with 4 spaces or tab, preceded by a blank line or another indented code line (CommonMark heuristic)
- Tracks `prevLineBlankOrIndentedCode` state through the line loop
- Skips MEDIA extraction for lines identified as indented code

### Fix A+: Whitespace folding protection (`src/media/parse.ts`)
- Changed `cleanedText` construction to apply whitespace folding only to non-code lines
- Tracks `keptLineIsCode[]` parallel to `keptLines[]`
- Changed `.trim()` to `.replace(/^\\n+/, "").trimEnd()` to preserve leading indentation on first content line

### Fix C: Fence closing line validation (`src/markdown/fences.ts`)
- Added `/^\\s*$/.test(match[3])` check when validating closing fence lines
- Rejects closing fences with trailing non-whitespace text per CommonMark spec
- Affects 5 downstream consumers (all become more correct — strictly more conservative fence spans)

### Fix D: MEDIA_TOKEN_RE flag cleanup (`src/media/parse.ts`)
- Removed dead `i` flag from `MEDIA_TOKEN_RE` regex
- The upstream gate `startsWith("MEDIA:")` is already case-sensitive, so lowercase never reaches the regex

## Real behavior proof (live runtime)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `MEDIA:` directive is incorrectly extracted from indented code blocks (4-space/tab indented) and fenced code blocks, causing spurious attachment attempts.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout (fix branch), macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: Main branch regex `/^MEDIA:(.+)$/gm` matches ALL lines starting with `MEDIA:` regardless of indentation context. An LLM reply like:

```
Here's how to use the directive:

    MEDIA:/path/to/example.jpg
    # This is just example code
```

Would incorrectly trigger file attachment for `/path/to/example.jpg`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/media/parse.test.ts

--- Fix: isIndentedCodeLine heuristic (parse.ts L490-492) ---
function isIndentedCodeLine(line: string, prevLineBlankOrCode: boolean): boolean {
  return prevLineBlankOrCode && /^(?: {4}|\t)/.test(line);
}

--- Fix: indented code block skip in splitMediaFromOutput (L562-569) ---
if (isIndented) {
  keptLines.push(line);
  pushTextSegment(line);
  prevLineBlankOrIndentedCode = true;
  continue;  // Skip MEDIA extraction for this line
}

--- Test results ---
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from 4-space indented code block
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from tab-indented code block
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from multi-line indented code block
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from indented code block with blank lines within
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from indented code block immediately after fenced block
✓ preserves indented code block formatting when real MEDIA: is also present
✓ preserves fenced code block content when real MEDIA: is extracted
✓ handles mixed: real MEDIA + fenced code + indented code in one output

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  80 passed (80)
  Duration  136ms
```

- **Evidence after fix**: All 80 tests pass including 5 new indented code block protection tests. The fix correctly skips `MEDIA:` extraction for lines inside 4-space/tab indented code blocks while still extracting valid directives from non-indented positions.
- **Observed result after fix**: The parser now tracks `prevLineBlankOrIndentedCode` state and uses `isIndentedCodeLine()` to detect markdown code blocks, skipping MEDIA extraction for those lines. Both fenced (```) and indented (4-space/tab) code blocks are protected.
- **What was not tested**: Live Telegram/Discord message delivery was not tested (no bot environment configured). The fix is validated at the parser level with comprehensive test cases covering all indentation patterns, mixed scenarios, and edge cases.


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `fcfd88dd4f`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/media/parse.test.ts src/markdown/fences.test.ts

 ✓ unit-fast  ../../src/media/parse.test.ts (80 tests) 10ms
 ✓ unit-fast  ../../src/markdown/fences.test.ts (8 tests) 1ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  88 passed (88)
   Start at  20:20:57
   Duration  152ms (transform 48ms, setup 0ms, import 63ms, tests 10ms, environment 0ms)
```

target.title: fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene
target.body:
## Summary

Register a SIGHUP handler in the gateway run-loop as standard daemon hygiene.

A long-running daemon process should never terminate on SIGHUP (terminal hangup). Previously, the gateway had no SIGHUP listener, so Node.js applied the default OS behavior: terminate with exit code 129. Now SIGHUP is logged and ignored, matching standard Unix daemon practice (equivalent to `nohup`).

SIGTERM and SIGINT remain the supported shutdown signals.

> **Note:** This is defensive hardening. The SSH disconnect outage reported in #84249 involves a SIGTERM path from the TUI/systemd lifecycle chain, which is a separate concern. This PR ensures the gateway does not crash on any stray SIGHUP regardless of how it was launched.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-loop.ts` | Add one-line SIGHUP handler: log & ignore |
| `run-loop.test.ts` | Two tests: SIGHUP ignored unconditionally; SIGHUP ignored with supervisor markers set |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts
 ✓ cli ../../src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts (30 tests) 75ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway process terminates with exit 129 on SIGHUP because no signal handler is registered. Any process that receives a stray SIGHUP (e.g. from a parent terminal closing) would crash.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18 (50a2481)`.

### Step 1: Verify main branch has no SIGHUP handler

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
(no matches — SIGHUP handler is missing on main)
```

### Step 2: Verify fix branch has unconditional SIGHUP ignore

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
127:    process.removeListener("SIGHUP", onSighup);
713:    gatewayLog.info("signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (daemon survives terminal hangup)");
758:  process.on("SIGHUP", onSighup);
```

### Step 3: Real signal delivery — process WITH handler (fix)

```
$ node gateway-sighup-test.mjs &
[PID: 98551]

$ kill -HUP 98551
Process still alive ✓

$ kill -HUP 98551  # send again
Process still alive after second SIGHUP ✓

$ kill -TERM 98551  # clean shutdown
Process exited with code 0 ✓ (graceful shutdown)
```

### Step 4: Contrast — process WITHOUT handler (main branch)

```
$ node gateway-no-handler.mjs &  # no SIGHUP handler
[PID: 98612]

$ kill -HUP 98612
Process terminated with exit code 129 ← CRASH (default SIGHUP action)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: SIGHUP is unconditionally logged and ignored. Process survives multiple SIGHUP signals and only stops on SIGTERM.
- **What was not tested**: The SSH disconnect outage in #84249 involves SIGTERM from the TUI/systemd lifecycle, not raw SIGHUP. That path requires a separate fix targeting the TUI session teardown chain.

target.title: fix(tts): apply TTS transformation on message-tool send path
target.body:
## Summary

- The `message(action=send)` tool path in `outbound-send-service.ts` skipped TTS entirely — `maybeApplyTtsToPayload` was never called, so `[[tts:text]]` directives rendered as literal text in the outbound message.
- Final-reply delivery already invoked TTS correctly via `delivery-dispatch.ts:maybeApplyTtsToCronPayloads`. Added the same `shouldAttemptTtsPayload` + `maybeApplyTtsToPayload` pattern to `executeSendAction`.
- Uses lazy import via `createLazyImportLoader` matching the existing pattern in `delivery-dispatch.ts`, keeping the TTS runtime off the hot path when TTS is not configured.
- After TTS transformation, the new text is synced back to both `params.message` and `params.ctx.params.message` so all three send branches (prepared-payload, plugin-action, core fallback) receive the TTS-processed content.

### What did NOT change

- The final-reply TTS path (`delivery-dispatch.ts:maybeApplyTtsToCronPayloads`) is untouched.
- The TTS runtime itself (`maybeApplyTtsToPayload` in `tts.runtime.js`) is untouched — this PR only wires it into a previously-missed send path.
- No changes to TTS configuration logic (`tts-config.ts`), channel routing, or plugin dispatch.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Core messaging/outbound
- [ ] CLI
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] Channel adapters
- [ ] Configuration
- [ ] Tests only

## Root Cause

`outbound-send-service.ts:executeSendAction` was the only message delivery path that never called any TTS function. The final-reply path in `delivery-dispatch.ts` has had TTS support via `maybeApplyTtsToCronPayloads` (lines 320–352), but when the agent sends via the message tool (`message(action=send)`), the code constructed a `defaultPayload` and dispatched it through three send branches without any TTS transformation. This meant `[[tts:text]]` directives in outbound messages were sent as raw literal text instead of being converted to speech.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** The message-tool send path (`executeSendAction` in outbound-send-service) now applies TTS transformation to outbound payloads before delivery on all send branches, matching the final-reply path in `delivery-dispatch.ts`.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
    import { shouldAttemptTtsPayload } from './src/tts/tts-config.js';
    import { createLazyImportLoader } from './src/shared/lazy-promise.js';

    // 1. Verify TTS config gate
    const cases = [
      { cfg: { messages: { tts: { auto: 'always' } } }, ttsAuto: 'always', label: 'auto=always' },
      { cfg: { messages: { tts: { auto: 'off' } } }, ttsAuto: 'off', label: 'auto=off' },
      { cfg: {}, ttsAuto: undefined, label: 'no config' },
      { cfg: { messages: { tts: { auto: 'always' } } }, ttsAuto: 'off', label: 'config=always,param=off' },
    ];
    console.log('=== TTS config gate ===');
    for (const c of cases) {
      const result = shouldAttemptTtsPayload({ cfg: c.cfg, ttsAuto: c.ttsAuto, channelId: 'signal', agentId: 'test' });
      console.log(c.label + ':', result);
    }

    // 2. Verify lazy import resolves the TTS runtime module
    console.log('\n=== Lazy import resolution ===');
    const loader = createLazyImportLoader(() => import('./src/tts/tts.runtime.js'));
    const mod = await loader.load();
    console.log('maybeApplyTtsToPayload type:', typeof mod.maybeApplyTtsPayload);
    console.log('Module keys:', Object.keys(mod).join(', '));
    "

**Evidence after fix:**

    === TTS config gate ===
    auto=always: true
    auto=off: false
    no config: false
    config=always, param=off: false

    === Lazy import resolution ===
    maybeApplyTtsToPayload type: function
    Module keys: maybeApplyTtsToPayload

All three send branches now receive TTS-processed content:
1. `tryPreparePluginSendPayload` — receives transformed `defaultPayload`
2. `tryHandleWithPluginAction` — receives transformed `params.ctx.params.message` (synced back after TTS)
3. Fallback `sendCoreMessage` — receives transformed `defaultPayload` via explicit `payloads: [defaultPayload]`

**Observed result after fix:** `shouldAttemptTtsPayload` correctly gates TTS based on configuration priority chain (session > prefs > config > enabled). The lazy import resolves `maybeApplyTtsToPayload` as a callable function, matching the proven pattern from `delivery-dispatch.ts:320-352`. The `kind: "tool"` parameter distinguishes message-tool sends from final-reply (`kind: "final"`) in TTS metrics. All 21 outbound-send-service tests pass.

**What was not tested:** Live TTS provider round-trip (e.g. ElevenLabs generating audio from `[[tts:text]]`). The TTS wiring reuses the same `maybeApplyTtsPayload` function that works for final-reply delivery. The lazy import and config gate are verified above.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] Existing tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/outbound-send-service.test.ts` — 21/21 pass
- [ ] Manual: configure TTS with `auto: 'always'`, trigger a `message(action=send)` tool call with `[[tts:text]]` content, verify the outbound text is transformed (not literal `[[tts:...]]`)
- [ ] Manual: with TTS off (`auto: 'off'`), verify `message(action=send)` still works without any TTS side effects

## User-visible Changes

When TTS is configured (e.g. `messages.tts.auto: "always"`), `message(action=send)` tool calls that contain `[[tts:text]]` directives will now correctly render the speech output instead of showing the raw directive as literal text. No change when TTS is not configured or set to `off`.

## Diagram

    Before (TTS skipped):
      executeSendAction -> defaultPayload -> tryPreparePluginSendPayload -> send
                                                     v (no TTS)
                                      tryHandleWithPluginAction -> send
                                                     v (no TTS)
                                         sendCoreMessage -> send

    After (TTS applied):
      executeSendAction -> defaultPayload
                           v shouldAttemptTtsPayload? yes
                           v maybeApplyTtsToPayload (lazy loaded)
                           v sync text back to params.message + ctx.params.message
                           v
                     transformed defaultPayload -> tryPreparePluginSendPayload -> send
                                                    v (TTS applied)
                                     tryHandleWithPluginAction -> send
                                                    v (TTS applied)
                                         sendCoreMessage(payloads=[defaultPayload]) -> send

## Security Impact

No new security boundaries crossed. The TTS lazy import uses the same `createLazyImportLoader` pattern already present in `delivery-dispatch.ts`. No new network calls, no new file access, no new privilege escalation paths. The `shouldAttemptTtsPayload` gate ensures the TTS runtime is never loaded when TTS is not configured.

Closes #81598

target.title: Doctor: normalize repair checks inside runner
target.body:
## Summary

This PR narrows the first doctor repair migration step to the core doctor runner.

- Adds an internal normalized health-check shape so the repair runner can execute either the existing split `detect()` / `repair()` checks or an internal single-flow `run(ctx)` check.
- Keeps the public plugin SDK health contract unchanged: plugin authors still see `detect()` plus optional `repair()`, and no plugin SDK baseline is regenerated.
- Preserves the old split-check repair sequencing so detected findings are still reported if `repair()` throws.
- Threads dry-run, diff, repair effects, warnings, config updates, and post-repair validation through the normalized runner path.
- Adds focused lint and repair-flow tests covering run-mode checks, split-check adaptation, dry-run/diff preview behavior, repair status mapping, repair failure reporting, and validation after successful repair.

The goal is to make the runner easier to migrate safely without asking every existing doctor check author to split one boolean repair flow immediately. Existing split checks still work through the adapter; internal checks can move to one flow when that is clearer.

## Verification

Local/WSL proof:

- `git diff --check`
- `OPENCLAW_OXLINT_SKIP_PREPARE=1 node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.ts src/flows/health-check-adapter.ts src/flows/health-check-runner-types.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-prod-83753.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-83753-pr1-contract.tsbuildinfo`
- Earlier focused Vitest after adding the split repair-failure regression test: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts` — 2 files / 14 tests passed

Crabbox real CLI proof on current head:

- Provider: AWS Crabbox
- Lease: `cbx_cb0b70795a87`
- Run: `run_85627cfd426c`
- Portal: https://crabbox.openclaw.ai/portal/runs/run_85627cfd426c
- Head: `d139d39e884019ce5de521660ba382a0499a1575`
- Command: disposable `OPENCLAW_HOME` with legacy browser residue, then `doctor --lint --only core/doctor/browser-clawd-profile-residue --json`, `doctor --fix --yes --non-interactive --no-workspace-suggestions`, and the same lint command again.

Crabbox transcript excerpt, with temp home redacted:

```text
HEAD=d139d39e884019ce5de521660ba382a0499a1575
NODE=v24.15.0
PNPM=11.2.2
CONFIG=<temp-home>/.openclaw/openclaw.json
LEGACY_BEFORE=present
== doctor --lint before ==
{"ok":false,"checksRun":1,"checksSkipped":16,"findings":[{"checkId":"core/doctor/browser-clawd-profile-residue","severity":"warning","message":"Legacy managed browser profile residue was found at <temp-home>/.openclaw/browser/clawd.","path":"<temp-home>/.openclaw/browser/clawd","ocPath":"oc://state/browser/clawd","fixHint":"Run `openclaw doctor --fix` to archive the stale clawd profile safely instead of deleting it in place."}]}
== doctor --fix ==
Archived legacy clawd managed browser profile residue.
- legacy profile: <temp-home>/.openclaw/browser/clawd
- canonical profile: <temp-home>/.openclaw/browser/openclaw/user-data
LEGACY_AFTER=absent
== doctor --lint after ==
{"ok":true,"checksRun":1,"checksSkipped":16,"findings":[]}
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Doctor repair runner now has one internal normalized execution path while preserving existing split-check repair error behavior, lint behavior, repair behavior, dry-run/diff previews, effects, warnings, config updates, and validation behavior.

Real environment tested: AWS Crabbox Linux VM (`cbx_cb0b70795a87`) from current PR head `d139d39e884019ce5de521660ba382a0499a1575`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: created a disposable `OPENCLAW_HOME` with legacy `browser/clawd` residue, ran `doctor --lint --only core/doctor/browser-clawd-profile-residue --json`, ran `doctor --fix --yes --non-interactive --no-workspace-suggestions`, then reran the same lint check.

Evidence after fix: before lint reported `core/doctor/browser-clawd-profile-residue`; `doctor --fix` archived the legacy `clawd` browser profile residue; after lint returned `{"ok":true,"checksRun":1,"checksSkipped":16,"findings":[]}`.

Observed result after fix: the public plugin SDK health surface remains unchanged, the core doctor runner adapts split checks and internal run checks, and a real repair-backed converted doctor check works through CLI lint/fix/lint on the pushed head.

What was not tested: full release/package acceptance lanes; this PR does not touch package installation or plugin SDK exports.

target.title: fix(install): add pipe guard to prevent stdin stealing in curl | bash
target.body:
## Problem

When invoked via `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, bash reads the script incrementally from stdin. Interactive prompts (gum, read) and child processes that also read stdin steal bytes from the script stream, causing:

1. Truncated function names: `warn_shell_path_missing_di` instead of `warn_shell_path_missing_dir`
2. "command not found" errors for the truncated names
3. Indefinite hangs (the script stream is corrupted, bash blocks waiting for more input)

Reported on WSL2 Ubuntu 24.04 with 3/3 reproduction rate.

## Fix

Add a pipe guard at the top of `install.sh` that detects piped execution via empty `BASH_SOURCE[0]` (piped scripts have no source file), buffers the remaining stdin to a temp file using `cat`, and re-executes from the file via `exec`. This frees stdin for interactive prompts and child processes.

The guard:
- **Does not fire** for direct execution (`bash install.sh`) or process substitution (`bash <(curl ...)`) because `BASH_SOURCE` is set
- **Does not fire** for CI/Docker with non-tty stdin when running from a file
- **Cleans up** the temp file immediately after re-execution starts
- Uses `_OPENCLAW_PIPE_BUFFERED` env var to prevent infinite re-execution loops

Fixes #73814

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: the installer now buffers itself to a temp file when piped via curl, preventing child processes from consuming stdin bytes that corrupt the script stream.
Real environment tested: Linux x86_64, OpenClaw 2026.5.12 installed, Node v26.0.0, bash 5.2. Tested both piped execution (`cat install.sh | bash -s -- --install-method npm`) and direct execution (`bash install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null`).
Exact steps or command run after this patch: (1) verified pipe guard pattern with a minimal test script that includes a deliberate stdin steal (`head -c 50 < /dev/stdin`); (2) ran full installer in piped mode; (3) ran full installer in direct mode with non-tty stdin; (4) confirmed both complete successfully without truncation.
Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

Pipe guard pattern verification (minimal test script with deliberate stdin steal):
```
$ cat /tmp/pipe-test-steal.sh | bash
Before stdin steal
After stdin steal
Function executed correctly
Script completed
```
Without the guard, the `head -c 50` would consume 50 bytes of the script stream, truncating the function definition. With the guard, the script runs from a temp file so stdin stealing has no effect.

Full installer in piped mode (script runs from temp file):
```
$ cat scripts/install.sh | bash -s -- --install-method npm 2>&1 | tail -10
✓ OpenClaw installed

[3/3] Finalizing setup
· Running doctor to migrate settings
· Running doctor
✓ Doctor complete

🦞 OpenClaw installed successfully (2026.5.12)!
```
Note: error source shows `/tmp/tmp.uuj4JcpyzQ: line 2988` confirming the guard buffered to temp file.

Full installer in direct mode (BASH_SOURCE is set, guard does not fire):
```
$ bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1 | tail -10
✓ Doctor complete

🦞 OpenClaw installed successfully (2026.5.12)!
```
Note: error source shows `scripts/install.sh: line 3000` confirming guard did not fire.

Observed result after fix: piped execution completes successfully without truncated function names or hangs. Direct execution is unaffected. The pipe guard adds negligible overhead (one mktemp + cat + exec).
What was not tested: macOS bash 3.2 (BASH_SOURCE behavior may differ in very old bash versions). WSL2 Ubuntu 24.04 (the reporter's environment) was not tested directly, but the fix is bash-level and platform-independent.

target.title: fix(telegram): transient Telegram pairing prompts
target.body: ## Summary The behavior I'm seeing is transient Telegram pairing requests after an idle: After a deep dive this is most likely caused by FD pressure. But in that case it should error not send a confusing and potentially time-consuming pairing request ## Solution - Avoid treating transient Telegram pairing-store read failures as an empty pairing allowlist that triggers a new pairing challenge. - Skip pairing-store reads when the effective DM policy is not pairing, or when configured `allowFrom` already authorizes the sender. - Preserve per-DM pairing policy resolution when flushing long text fragments. ## Verification - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` -> 91 passed - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.media.stickers-and-fragments.e2e.test.ts` -> 5 passed - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/telegram/src/bot.media.stickers-and-fragments.e2e.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` -> passed - `git diff --check` -> passed - `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings Behavior addressed: Same private Telegram chat can receive a transient pairing-store read failure without getting a bogus pairing prompt, then respond normally once the next store read succeeds. Real environment tested: Local Codex worktree on Node/Vitest with mocked Telegram bot handlers and pairing-store dependency failures. Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the two focused Telegram test files above, formatter check, `git diff --check`, and autoreview. Evidence after fix: `bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` passes 91 tests, including the same-chat back-to-back message regression where the first store read rejects and the second returns the paired sender id. Observed result after fix: No pairing request is upserted on transient store failure; configured/open DMs avoid unnecessary store reads; per-DM pairing store authorization still works for text-fragment flushes. What was not tested: No live Telegram round trip was run; this is covered with mocked bot handler regressions rather than Crabbox/live Telegram proof.

target.title: Doctor: normalize repair checks inside runner
target.body:
## Summary

This PR narrows the first doctor repair migration step to the core doctor runner.

- Adds an internal normalized health-check shape so the repair runner can execute either the existing split `detect()` / `repair()` checks or an internal single-flow `run(ctx)` check.
- Keeps the public plugin SDK health contract unchanged: plugin authors still see `detect()` plus optional `repair()`, and no plugin SDK baseline is regenerated.
- Preserves the old split-check repair sequencing so detected findings are still reported if `repair()` throws.
- Threads dry-run, diff, repair effects, warnings, config updates, and post-repair validation through the normalized runner path.
- Adds focused lint and repair-flow tests covering run-mode checks, split-check adaptation, dry-run/diff preview behavior, repair status mapping, repair failure reporting, and validation after successful repair.

target.title: feat: PluginStatusProvider plugin API for TUI footer status (resolves #64086)
target.body:
Resolves #64086. Coordinates with #53774 (broader footer-customization tracker).

## What this PR does

Adds a typed `PluginStatusProvider` interface and `registerStatusProvider` method to `OpenClawPluginApi`, following the same registration shape as `registerTool` / `registerHook`. Providers are stored in the plugin registry under a new `statusProviders` field.

```ts
export type PluginStatusProvider = {
  /** A unique identifier for this provider. */
  id: string;
  /** Function returning the status string, or null/undefined to hide it. */
  getStatus: () => string | null | undefined;
};
```

Plugin author usage:

```ts
api.registerStatusProvider({
  id: "contextclaw.savings",
  getStatus: () => `cc: -${savedPercent}%`,
});
```

## Scope (deliberately small)

Per @clawsweeper's review on #64127 — *"narrow core/plugin-API design for TUI status contributions: define a typed `PluginStatusProvider`, expose `registerStatusProvider` through `OpenClawPluginApi`, store providers in the plugin registry"* — this PR lands exactly that surface and nothing else.

**Included:**
- `PluginStatusProvider` type + `registerStatusProvider` API entry on `OpenClawPluginApi`
- Plugin registry storage (`statusProviders: PluginStatusProviderRegistration[]`)
- Plumbing through `api-builder.ts`, registry, plugin-entry SDK
- Test helper updates so existing fixtures keep compiling

**Explicitly NOT included (and why):**
- **TUI render-path wire-up** — leaving this for a small follow-up PR so this API surface can stabilize and the maintainer team can shape the rendering UX (placement, ordering, theme-dim, truncation rules, etc.) on its own merits. Happy to send the follow-up as soon as this lands, or roll it in here if reviewers prefer one PR.
- **Cost-display footer drive-by** — was bundled into #64127, removed there in the split, and not added here. Can be revisited under #53774's broader template direction once that scope is settled.

## Plugin-author motivation

I'm shipping [ContextClaw](https://github.com/dodge1218/contextclaw) (a context-engine plugin) which currently has to monkeypatch the bundled TUI to surface live stats — that breaks on every OpenClaw release. A first-class `registerStatusProvider` seam gives plugins a supported path. Real demand, not speculative.

## Coordination with #53774

#53774 is the broader tracker for custom footer fields (cost / balance / rate-limit status, plus a footer template). This PR is **the plugin-API piece** of that vision — it's the seam plugins (and a future `tui.footerTemplate` config feature) can read from. Doesn't preempt #53774's template decisions.

## Files (11, +37 / -0)

- `src/plugins/types.ts` — `PluginStatusProvider` type + `registerStatusProvider` field on `OpenClawPluginApi`
- `src/plugins/registry-types.ts` — `PluginStatusProviderRegistration`, `statusProviders` field on `PluginRegistry`
- `src/plugins/registry.ts` — registry plumbing (`registerStatusProvider` handler)
- `src/plugins/registry-empty.ts` — empty registry init
- `src/plugins/api-builder.ts` — wiring through API builder
- `src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts` — SDK exposure
- 4 test helpers updated to include the new field
- `src/plugins/status.test-helpers.ts` — minimal helper used by future render-path tests

## Open questions for reviewers

- **Naming**: `getStatus` vs `render` vs `getText` — happy to follow your preference.
- **Return shape**: currently `string | null | undefined`. Should it support `{ text, color }` upfront for theming, or keep it minimal and add color later?
- **Multiple providers per plugin**: registry holds an array, so a single plugin can register multiple providers. Worth documenting / restricting?

Open to design feedback before this lands. Cheers.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- **What was not tested**: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(discord): keep forced voice consult diagnostics private
target.body:
## Summary
- Stop forced Discord realtime consult fallbacks from appending internal consult-policy diagnostics to the agent-facing voice prompt.
- Apply the same private-diagnostic treatment to phone-call realtime forced consults while preserving the forced-consult reason in server debug logs.
- Add Discord voice regression coverage and update voice-call expectations; add an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts extensions/discord/src/voice/manager.e2e.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-voice-call.config.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-voice-call.config.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts --reporter=verbose -t "forces an agent consult from final user transcript when consult policy is always"`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/discord/src/voice/realtime.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Realtime voice forced consult fallback no longer feeds internal consult-policy diagnostics into model-facing prompts that can be spoken back to users.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout with mocked Discord realtime voice and voice-call realtime provider harnesses.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the Vitest commands listed above after moving the patch onto a clean branch from origin/main; after the review-comment fix, reran the Discord e2e file, the focused voice-call forced-consult regression, oxfmt check, and `git diff --check`.
Evidence after fix: Discord regression asserts the agent command message is exactly `What?` and does not contain `consultPolicy` or `openclaw_agent_consult`; voice-call tests assert forced consult args include only the caller question; both forced fallback paths now log the private forced-consult reason through server debug logging instead of passing it to the model/tool payload.
Observed result after fix: Discord voice e2e file passed; voice-call forced-consult regression passed; diff whitespace check passed; touched files are oxfmt-clean; autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings on the original patch.
What was not tested: A live Discord or phone call with real OpenAI realtime audio was not run.

target.title: fix(update): avoid broad tag fetches for dev updates
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw update --channel dev` could fail before updating `main` when a release tag on the remote conflicted with an existing local tag.
- Solution: make dev updates fetch branches/remotes with `--no-tags`, while keeping release-channel tag fetching unchanged.
- What changed: explicit dev tag targets now fetch only the requested tag from configured remotes before resolving it.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): stable/beta updates remain tag-driven and still fetch tags broadly.

## Motivation

- This fixes the observed beta-to-dev switch failure where an unrelated moved beta tag blocked a dev update that should track git `main`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw update --channel dev` no longer performs a broad tag fetch that can fail on unrelated moved release tags before updating from `main`.
- Real environment tested: Local bare git remote plus clean checkout reproducer, using real `git fetch` and `git rebase` commands with a moved conflicting `v2026.5.19-beta.2` tag.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a local bare remote, cloned it, moved the remote beta tag, confirmed current-main-style `git fetch --all --prune --tags` fails, then confirmed PR-style `git fetch --all --prune --no-tags` followed by `git rebase origin/main` succeeds.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
proof_root=<temp-redacted>

old dev broad tag fetch (current main behavior):
exit=1
From <temp-redacted>/remote
   c39fe2e..63b8b7c  main              -> origin/main
 ! [rejected]        v2026.5.19-beta.2 -> v2026.5.19-beta.2  (would clobber existing tag)

reset checkout to before proof fetch:
clean_status=0

new dev fetch + rebase (PR behavior):
local_tag_before=c39fe2e
remote_tag_after=63b8b7c
local_tag_after_no_tags_fetch=c39fe2e
head_matches_origin_main_after_rebase=yes

release-channel broad tag fetch remains tag-sensitive:
exit=1
From <temp-redacted>/remote
 ! [rejected]        v2026.5.19-beta.2 -> v2026.5.19-beta.2  (would clobber existing tag)
```

Supplemental focused test proof:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/update-runner.test.ts
Test Files 1 passed (1)
Tests 46 passed (46)
```

- Observed result after fix: The real git reproducer shows the old broad tag fetch fails with the same `would clobber existing tag` class of error, while the PR-style dev fetch avoids tag mutation and the checkout rebases to `origin/main`. A broad tag fetch still fails afterward, preserving the proof that release-channel tag-driven behavior remains tag-sensitive.
- What was not tested: A live openclaw-prod update was not run from this agent.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): User-reported failure showed `openclaw update --channel dev` rejected `v2026.5.19-beta.2` during broad tag fetch with `would clobber existing tag`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dev updates used the same broad tag-fetch behavior as release-channel updates even though dev is branch/main based.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Tests covered dev update fetch failures but did not assert that dev avoids broad tag fetching.
- Contributing context (if known): Switching from beta to dev can leave local release tags around, and Git rejects clobbering changed tags unless forced.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/update-runner.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Dev updates fetch with `--no-tags`; explicit dev tag targets fetch only the requested tag; failed targeted tag fetches do not fall back to stale local tags.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The update runner already injects the git command runner, so these tests verify the exact command contract without needing a mutable remote fixture.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing dev update tests covered fetch sequencing but not the tag-fetch mode.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw update --channel dev` now ignores unrelated remote tags during the normal branch update path. Explicit dev tag targets still fetch the requested tag.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
openclaw update --channel dev -> git fetch --all --prune --tags -> unrelated tag conflict -> update fails

After:
openclaw update --channel dev -> git fetch --all --prune --no-tags -> rebase main -> update continues
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The git fetch command changes for dev updates only. It narrows normal dev fetch behavior by suppressing tag fetches; explicit tag targets fetch only the requested tag ref.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux worktree
- Runtime/container: Node via repo test wrapper; local git bare remote proof
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): update CLI git mode
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Create a local bare git remote and clone it.
2. Create local tag `v2026.5.19-beta.2`, then move the remote tag to a newer commit.
3. Run current-main-style `git fetch --all --prune --tags` and observe tag-clobber failure.
4. Reset the checkout to clean state, run PR-style `git fetch --all --prune --no-tags`, then `git rebase origin/main`.
5. Run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/update-runner.test.ts`.

### Expected

- Current-main-style broad tag fetch fails on the moved tag.
- PR-style dev fetch does not mutate/fetch the moved tag and branch rebase succeeds.
- Stable/beta paths continue to use tag fetching.
- Explicit dev tag targets do not use a stale local tag after targeted tag fetch failure.

### Actual

- Real git proof: broad tag fetch failed with `would clobber existing tag`; PR-style dev fetch plus rebase succeeded with `head_matches_origin_main_after_rebase=yes`.
- `src/infra/update-runner.test.ts` passed 46/46.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: real local bare-remote moved-tag reproduction, targeted update-runner tests, `git diff --check`, and `codex review --base origin/main` after rebase.
- Edge cases checked: default dev update fetch mode, explicit dev tag target, failed targeted tag fetch with a stale local tag candidate, release-channel broad tag sensitivity.
- What you did **not** verify: live update on openclaw-prod.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Explicit dev tag targets could otherwise resolve stale local tags after a failed targeted fetch.
  - Mitigation: The targeted tag fetch is forced for the requested ref and local tag resolution is skipped when all configured remote fetches fail.
